topics.title: Introduction to Modelling and Fundamental Laws: Definitions of Modelling, Uses of Mathematical Modelling, Classification of Modelling Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Modelling and Fundamental Laws: Definitions of Modelling, Uses of Mathematical Modelling, Classification of Modelling Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Model (and Mathematical Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Process Model)**
> A **process model** is a mathematical description (set of equations) that quantitatively represents the physical, chemical, or biological phenomena occurring in a process system, expressing the relationship among process variables (inputs, outputs, parameters, and disturbances) over a defined domain of time and/or space.

A *mathematical model* is therefore a collection of **algebraic equations, differential equations, partial differential equations, or integral relations** that capture the behavior of a real process. The variables involved are classified as:

- **Independent variables** (e.g., time $t$ and/or spatial coordinates $x$, $y$, $z$)
- **Dependent variables** (e.g., temperature $T$, concentration $C_A$, pressure $P$)
- **Parameters** (e.g., density $\rho$, specific heat $C_p$, rate constant $k$)
- **Forcing/Input variables** (e.g., feed flow rate $F_0$)
- **Output variables** (the quantities of interest one wishes to predict)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**
> A model is *not* the system itself — it is a **surrogate representation**. The art of modelling lies in retaining the essential features of the real system while discarding non-essential detail.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an architect who needs to communicate the appearance of a **20-storey building** to a client. You do not bring the client to the site every time — instead, you give them a **scale drawing, a 3D CAD model, or a miniature prototype**. The drawing *represents* the building accurately enough for the purpose of design review.

A **process model** plays exactly the same role in chemical engineering:

| Real World (Process) | Surrogate (Model) |
|---|---|
| Industrial ammonia reactor | System of ODEs for $T(t)$ and $C_{NH_3}(t)$ |
| Distillation column with 50 trays | MESH equations (Material, Equilibrium, Summation, Heat) |
| Polymerization reactor | Population balance + energy balance PDEs |

Just as an architect can test the building's structural safety using the model, an engineer can test process safety, optimize yield, and predict product purity using the mathematical model — **without ever touching the real plant**.

## 1.3 Uses / Scope of Mathematical Modelling

> [!TIP]
> **Why do we model?** Because the real plant is *expensive, dangerous, slow, and often inaccessible* during design and operation.

The principal uses, all of which are KTU-high-yield points, are summarized below:

1. **Design and Scale-up** — Translate laboratory bench-scale data to commercial plant scale.
2. **Process Optimization** — Maximize profit, minimize energy, maximize conversion/selectivity.
3. **Process Control** — Develop model-based controllers (MPC, DMC), tune PID controllers.
4. **Safety and Hazard Analysis** — Predict runaway reactions, overpressure, fire/explosion scenarios.
5. **Operator Training** — Power high-fidelity dynamic simulators (OTS) that replicate plant behavior.
6. **Troubleshooting and Debottlenecking** — Diagnose the cause of off-spec product or low throughput.
7. **Economic Forecasting** — Couple process model with cost model for feasibility studies.
8. **Research Tool** — Test hypotheses about reaction mechanisms, transport phenomena without experiments.

## 1.4 Classification of Modelling Techniques (Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Critical Classification**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates explicit knowledge of the *five primary classification axes*. A model can simultaneously belong to one class under *each* axis.

The five classification axes are:

1. **Lumped Parameter vs Distributed Parameter** (spatial dimension)
2. **Steady-State vs Dynamic / Unsteady-State** (time dimension)
3. **Linear vs Non-Linear** (mathematical form)
4. **Theoretical (Mechanistic) vs Empirical (Black-box) vs Semi-Empirical** (origin of equations)
5. **Deterministic vs Stochastic** (treatment of randomness)

Each of these will be elaborated in **Section 2** with examples and characteristic equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A single real process can be classified in multiple ways.** For instance, a perfectly mixed, heated, stirred tank reactor (CSTR) under constant inlet conditions is *lumped, steady-state, non-linear, theoretical,* and *deterministic*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Modelling Hierarchy — Levels of Detail

Mathematical models can be arranged on a spectrum from "purely empirical" to "purely fundamental":

- **Level 0 — Black-Box / Empirical Model:** No physics; curve-fitted to data. Example: $y = a x^b$.
- **Level 1 — Semi-Empirical / Gray-Box Model:** Combines physical structure with fitted parameters. Example: $Sh = a \cdot Re^b \cdot Sc^c$.
- **Level 2 — Theoretical / Mechanistic Model:** Built from conservation laws and constitutive relations. Example: Navier–Stokes equation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Engineering Rule of Thumb**
> Always start with the **simplest model that can answer the engineering question**. Add complexity only when the simpler model fails. This is the principle of *parsimony* in modelling.

## 2.2 Five-Axis Detailed Classification

### Axis A — Spatial Treatment

| Class | Assumption | Characteristic Equation | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lumped Parameter** | Properties uniform throughout the system volume | Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) | Well-mixed CSTR, perfectly mixed tank |
| **Distributed Parameter** | Properties vary with position in space | Partial Differential Equation (PDE) | Tubular reactor, heat exchanger tube |

Mathematical expression:

$$ \text{Lumped:} \quad \frac{dX}{dt} = f(X, t) $$

$$ \text{Distributed:} \quad \frac{\partial X}{\partial t} = f\!\left(X, \frac{\partial X}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial^2 X}{\partial x^2}, \ldots \right) $$

### Axis B — Temporal Treatment

| Class | Assumption | Math Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady-State** | No accumulation; time derivatives vanish | $f(X) = 0$ (Algebraic) |
| **Dynamic / Unsteady** | Accumulation term active | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = f(X, t)$ (ODE/PDE) |

### Axis C — Mathematical Form

| Class | Property | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | Superposition holds: $f(aX_1 + bX_2) = a f(X_1) + b f(X_2)$ | First-order reaction, linear heat conduction |
| **Non-Linear** | Superposition fails | Arrhenius kinetics, multi-component distillation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear models** are computationally cheap (closed-form solutions exist) but **rarely accurate** for real processes. **Non-linear models** are accurate but usually require numerical solution.

### Axis D — Origin of Equations

| Class | Basis | KTU-Critical Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Theoretical (Mechanistic / White-box)** | Conservation laws + constitutive equations | Most reliable, parameter-independent, no fitting required |
| **Empirical (Black-box)** | Data regression; e.g., ANN, polynomial | Easy, fast; valid only within fitted range |
| **Semi-Empirical (Gray-box)** | Hybrid of physical structure + fitted constants | Best engineering compromise |

### Axis E — Treatment of Uncertainty

| Class | Property | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Deterministic** | Outputs are uniquely defined by inputs | Most chemical engineering models |
| **Stochastic / Probabilistic** | Outputs are random variables with distributions | Polymerization chain length, particle size distribution |

## 2.3 The Universal Modelling Procedure (KTU Standard Steps)

A KTU 2024 answer that "lists the steps of developing a model" must hit the following sequence:

1. **Define the system** — Identify process boundaries (control volume).
2. **Identify the phenomenon** — Mass transfer? Heat transfer? Reaction? Momentum?
3. **Apply conservation laws** — Mass, energy, momentum, species.
4. **Introduce constitutive relations** — e.g., Fourier's law, Fick's law, Newton's law, ideal gas law.
5. **Specify the input / forcing functions** — Time-dependent feeds, disturbances.
6. **Define parameters** — Physical constants, transport coefficients.
7. **Set initial and boundary conditions** (IC and BC).
8. **Solve the model** — Analytically (if linear) or numerically.
9. **Validate and refine** — Compare predictions with experimental data; iterate.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the mnemonic:** *S-P-C-C-I-P-I-S-V* → **System → Phenomenon → Conservation → Constitutive → Inputs → Parameters → IC/BC → Solve → Validate**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the **backbone of the introductory module**. Each one is essential and has been asked in KTU exams in some form.

| # | Law / Equation | Statement | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conservation of Mass** | Mass is neither created nor destroyed in non-nuclear processes | $\dfrac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \underbrace{\dot{m}_{gen}}_{=0}$ |
| 2 | **Conservation of Energy** (First Law) | Energy accumulation equals net in minus net out plus generation | $\dfrac{dU}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}$ |
| 3 | **Conservation of Momentum** | Newton's 2nd law for a control volume | $\sum F = \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\rho v \, dV + \int_{CS}\rho v (v \cdot n) \, dA$ |
| 4 | **Conservation of Species $i$** | Accumulation = In − Out + Generation | $\dfrac{dn_i}{dt} = F_{i,in} - F_{i,out} + r_i V$ |
| 5 | **Fourier's Law** (Conduction) | Heat flux proportional to temperature gradient | $q = -k \nabla T$ |
| 6 | **Fick's Law** (Diffusion) | Mass flux proportional to concentration gradient | $J = -D \nabla C$ |
| 7 | **Newton's Law of Viscosity** | Momentum flux proportional to velocity gradient | $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ |
| 8 | **Arrhenius Rate Law** | Reaction rate constant temperature dependence | $k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ |
| 9 | **Ideal Gas Law** | $P V = n R T$ | Equation of state for ideal gas |
| 10 | **Lumped Capacitance Criterion** | When $Bi < 0.1$ the system can be modelled as lumped | $Bi = \dfrac{h L_c}{k_b}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Discipline (KTU Examiner's expectation)**: Always state **SI units** in any numerical answer. $T$ in **K** (not °C), $E_a$ in **J/mol**, $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**, $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **Arrhenius equation** is used in safety relief valve sizing for runaway reactions.
- **Fourier's law** underlies every heat exchanger design.
- **Fick's law** controls separation by diffusion (membranes, gas absorption).
- **Conservation laws** are the non-negotiable starting point for *every* model — failure to apply them is the single largest source of model errors, as flagged by KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose of this section:** To show the *complete, line-by-line construction* of two canonical introductory models — a *lumped, dynamic, non-linear model* (mixing tank) and a *distributed, dynamic, linear model* (transient heat conduction in a slab). KTU 2024 questions often derive a third of the marks from such derivations.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lumped Parameter Model: Mixing Tank with a Reaction

**Problem Statement (canonical):**
A well-mixed tank of volume $V$ initially contains a solution of species $A$ at concentration $C_{A0}$. A feed stream of concentration $C_{Af}$ enters at volumetric flow rate $F$, and an effluent stream leaves at the *same* flow rate $F$ (constant volume). Species $A$ decomposes by a first-order reaction $A \to \text{Products}$ with rate $r_A = -k C_A$. Derive an ODE for $C_A(t)$.

**Derivation (each step explicitly shown):**

**Step 1 — Define the system and control volume.**
The system boundary is the liquid inside the tank. The volume $V$ is constant (since $F_{in} = F_{out} = F$).

**Step 2 — Write the general species balance for $A$.**

$$ \text{Rate of accumulation} = \text{Rate in} - \text{Rate out} + \text{Rate of generation} $$

**Step 3 — Express each term mathematically.**

- **Accumulation:** The mass of $A$ in the tank is $m_A = C_A \cdot V$ (per unit volume basis). So:

$$ \text{Accumulation} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(C_A \, V\right) $$

Since $V$ is constant:

$$ \text{Accumulation} = V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} $$

- **Rate in:** $F \cdot C_{Af}$
- **Rate out:** $F \cdot C_A$ (because the tank is well-mixed, the exit concentration equals the tank concentration)
- **Rate of generation:** $r_A \cdot V = -k C_A \cdot V$

**Step 4 — Assemble the balance equation.**

$$ V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = F C_{Af} - F C_A - k C_A V $$

**Step 5 — Divide throughout by $V$ to obtain the standard form.**

$$ \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = \dfrac{F}{V}\, C_{Af} - \dfrac{F}{V}\, C_A - k \, C_A $$

**Step 6 — Define the residence time** $\tau = V / F$ (space-time) for cleaner notation.

$$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = \dfrac{C_{Af}}{\tau} - \left(k + \dfrac{1}{\tau}\right) C_A\;} $$

**Step 7 — Add the initial condition.**

$$ C_A(0) = C_{A0} $$

This is a **first-order, linear, non-homogeneous ODE**. Its analytical solution (at steady-state, $t \to \infty$, $dC_A/dt = 0$) is:

$$ C_{A,ss} = \dfrac{C_{Af}}{1 + k \tau} $$

And the dynamic solution for a step change in $C_{Af}$ (with $C_{A0}$ as initial value) is:

$$ C_A(t) = C_{A,ss} + \left(C_{A0} - C_{A,ss}\right) e^{-(k + 1/\tau)\,t} $$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** The exponent $\lambda = k + 1/\tau$ is the inverse of the *process time constant* $T_p$. For control loop tuning, $T_p = 1/\lambda$ tells the engineer *how fast* the process will respond to a disturbance.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Distributed Parameter Model: Transient Heat Conduction in a Slab

**Problem Statement (canonical):**
Derive the governing equation for one-dimensional, transient heat conduction in a homogeneous slab of thermal conductivity $k_b$, density $\rho$, and specific heat $C_p$. The temperature $T$ varies only with $x$ and $t$ (no heat generation).

**Derivation (every step shown):**

**Step 1 — Draw the control volume.**
Consider an infinitesimal slice of thickness $dx$ and unit cross-sectional area $A$ at location $x$ inside the slab.

**Step 2 — Energy balance on the slice.**
In the absence of internal heat generation, the energy balance is:

$$ \text{Energy in at } x - \text{Energy out at } (x + dx) = \text{Rate of change of internal energy} $$

**Step 3 — Express heat flux using Fourier's law.**
At position $x$, the conductive heat flux (into the slice) is $q_x = -k_b \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_x$.
At position $x + dx$, the heat flux out of the slice is:

$$ q_{x+dx} = -k_b \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_{x+dx} $$

**Step 4 — Write the net energy inflow.**

$$ \text{Net in} = \left(q_x - q_{x+dx}\right) A = -k_b A \left[\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_x - \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_{x+dx}\right] $$

**Step 5 — Apply a Taylor series expansion of the flux at $(x+dx)$ around $x$.**

$$ \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_{x+dx} = \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_x + \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} \, dx + O(dx^2) $$

Substituting back:

$$ \text{Net in} = -k_b A \left[-\dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}\, dx\right] = k_b A \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}\, dx $$

**Step 6 — Express the rate of change of internal energy.**
The internal energy in the slice is $dU = \rho \, C_p \, A \, dx \, T$. Differentiating:

$$ \dfrac{dU}{dt} = \rho \, C_p \, A \, dx \, \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} $$

**Step 7 — Equate and simplify.**

$$ \rho \, C_p \, A \, dx \, \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k_b \, A \, \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}\, dx $$

Cancel $A \, dx$ from both sides:

$$ \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \underbrace{\dfrac{k_b}{\rho \, C_p}}_{\alpha}\, \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} $$

**Step 8 — Final Governing Equation (Fourier's Heat Equation).**

$$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}\;} $$

where $\alpha = k_b / (\rho C_p)$ is the **thermal diffusivity** with units **m²/s**.

**Step 9 — Typical boundary and initial conditions.**

- Initial: $T(x, 0) = T_i$ (uniform initial temperature)
- BC1 (symmetry at center of slab): $\partial T / \partial x = 0$ at $x = 0$
- BC2 (convection at surface $x = L$): $-k_b \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_{x=L} = h\!\left[T(L, t) - T_\infty\right]$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Models to Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below directly answers a common KTU 14-mark question: *"Classify the model of an isothermal CSTR / a non-isothermal PFR / a distillation column, and write its governing equation."*

| System | Classification (all 5 axes) | Governing Equation | Order |
|---|---|---|---|
| Isothermal CSTR (no rxn) | Lumped, Dynamic, Linear, Theoretical, Deterministic | $V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = F(C_{Af} - C_A)$ | 1st order ODE |
| Isothermal CSTR (1st order rxn) | Lumped, Dynamic, Non-linear, Theoretical, Deterministic | $V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = F(C_{Af} - C_A) - k C_A V$ | 1st order ODE |
| Non-isothermal CSTR | Lumped, Dynamic, Non-linear, Theoretical, Deterministic | $V \rho C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p (T_f - T) + (-\Delta H_r) k C_A V - UA(T - T_c)$ | Coupled ODEs |
| Plug Flow Reactor (PFR) | Distributed, Steady, Non-linear, Theoretical, Deterministic | $F \dfrac{dC_A}{dV} = -k C_A$ | 1st order ODE in $V$ |
| Heat conduction in slab | Distributed, Dynamic, Linear, Theoretical, Deterministic | $\partial T / \partial t = \alpha \, \partial^2 T / \partial x^2$ | 2nd order PDE |
| Heat exchanger (LMTD) | Lumped, Steady, Linear, Theoretical, Deterministic | $Q = U A \, \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Algebraic |
| Catalytic converter (auto) | Distributed, Dynamic, Non-linear, Semi-empirical, Deterministic | Species + energy PDE with fitted kinetics | Coupled PDEs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Universal Modelling Procedure Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid flow is the *exact* sequence expected in a KTU 14-mark answer to *"Explain with a flow diagram the steps involved in mathematical modelling."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Define System and Draw Control Volume] --> A2[Step 2: Identify Governing Phenomena]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Apply Conservation Laws - Mass, Energy, Momentum, Species]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Introduce Constitutive Relations - Fourier, Fick, Newton, Arrhenius]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Identify Inputs Forcing Functions and Parameters]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Specify Initial and Boundary Conditions]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Simplify and Classify Model]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: Solve Analytically or Numerically]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9: Validate Against Experimental Data]
    A9 --> A10[Step 10: Refine and Iterate if Mismatch]
    A10 --> A11[Final Validated Model]
    A9 -.Mismatch.-> A4
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Five-Axis Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Process Model Classification]
    B0 --> B1[Axis 1: Spatial Treatment]
    B0 --> B2[Axis 2: Temporal Treatment]
    B0 --> B3[Axis 3: Mathematical Form]
    B0 --> B4[Axis 4: Origin of Equations]
    B0 --> B5[Axis 5: Treatment of Uncertainty]

    B1 --> B1a[Lumped - ODE]
    B1 --> B1b[Distributed - PDE]

    B2 --> B2a[Steady State - Algebraic]
    B2 --> B2b[Dynamic Unsteady - ODE or PDE]

    B3 --> B3a[Linear - Superposition Holds]
    B3 --> B3b[Nonlinear - No Superposition]

    B4 --> B4a[Theoretical White box]
    B4 --> B4b[Empirical Black box]
    B4 --> B4c[Semi Empirical Gray box]

    B5 --> B5a[Deterministic]
    B5 --> B5b[Stochastic Probabilistic]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Process Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Inputs and Disturbances] --> C2[Process Model Block]
    C4[Parameters Constants] --> C2
    C5[Initial and Boundary Conditions] --> C2
    C2 --> C3[Outputs and State Variables]
    C3 --> C6[Validation Against Plant Data]
    C6 --> C7[Refined Process Model]
    C7 -. Feedback Loop .-> C2
    C6 --> C8[Decision: Design or Control or Optimization]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** The *feedback loop* from validation to the model block is the iteration cycle. A model is never "finished" — it is continually refined as more plant data becomes available.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Lumped vs Distributed Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Lumped Parameter System] --> D1a[Uniform T x across the volume]
    D1 --> D1b[Output depends only on time t]
    D1a --> D1c[Governed by ODE]
    D1b --> D1c

    D2[Distributed Parameter System] --> D2a[T x varies with position]
    D2 --> D2b[Output depends on t and x]
    D2a --> D2c[Governed by PDE]
    D2b --> D2c
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> - Part A short answer: 3 marks each (no choice, answer in 4–6 lines)
> - Part B long answer: 14 marks each (with internal choice, sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks)
> - Bloom's levels: *Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate*

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]
**Define a mathematical model. List any four uses of mathematical models in chemical engineering.**

**Model Answer:**
A *mathematical model* is a set of equations (algebraic, differential, or integral) that quantitatively describes the behaviour of a process system by relating its inputs, outputs, parameters, and state variables.

Four uses:
1. Process design and scale-up from laboratory to industrial scale.
2. Process optimization for maximum yield or minimum energy.
3. Process control system design (e.g., PID and model-predictive control).
4. Safety analysis and operator training using dynamic simulators.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four uses)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between lumped and distributed parameter models with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lumped | Distributed |
|---|---|---|
| Spatial variation | Assumed uniform | Varies with position |
| Equation type | ODE | PDE |
| Example | Well-mixed CSTR | Tubular plug-flow reactor |

In a lumped parameter system, intensive properties (e.g., concentration, temperature) are assumed constant throughout the control volume. In a distributed parameter system, properties vary with spatial coordinates, requiring at least one partial derivative. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Pattern:** Each sub-part typically carries **7 marks**. Provide diagrams, free-body diagrams, and clearly state assumptions. Marks are awarded for: (i) statement of assumptions — 1 mark, (ii) equation assembly — 3 marks, (iii) simplification — 2 marks, (iv) final boxed answer — 1 mark.

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, 14 Marks]

**(a)** *Explain in detail the different classifications of mathematical models used in process engineering. Support your answer with one example for each class.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Mathematical models can be classified along five independent axes:

1. **Lumped vs Distributed** — Lumped assumes uniform properties (e.g., well-mixed tank → ODE); Distributed accounts for spatial gradients (e.g., tubular reactor → PDE).

2. **Steady-State vs Dynamic** — Steady-state assumes no accumulation (algebraic equations, e.g., steady CSTR); Dynamic includes time dependence (e.g., start-up of a batch reactor → ODE in $t$).

3. **Linear vs Non-Linear** — Linear models obey superposition (e.g., first-order reaction, $dC_A/dt = -kC_A$); Non-linear do not (e.g., Arrhenius temperature dependence, $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$).

4. **Theoretical vs Empirical vs Semi-Empirical** — Theoretical derives equations from conservation laws and constitutive relations (e.g., heat conduction equation); Empirical uses data regression (e.g., polynomial fit of distillation curve); Semi-empirical blends the two (e.g., heat transfer correlation $Nu = a Re^b Pr^c$).

5. **Deterministic vs Stochastic** — Deterministic gives unique outputs (most process models); Stochastic involves randomness (e.g., polymerization chain-length distribution).

A single process can fall in one class per axis. For example, an isothermal, perfectly mixed, first-order CSTR is **lumped, dynamic, linear, theoretical, deterministic** and is described by:

$$ V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = F C_{Af} - F C_A - k C_A V $$

*(7 marks: 1 mark per axis explanation, 1 mark for the consolidated example, 1 mark for the ODE)*

---

**(b)** *Derive the governing equation for a well-mixed isothermal tank in which a first-order reaction $A \to B$ takes place. Assume constant volume, constant density, and constant feed conditions. Define the residence time and obtain the steady-state concentration of $A$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
- Isothermal (so $T$ = constant; energy balance is not required)
- Constant density and constant volume $V$ (so $F_{in} = F_{out} = F$)
- Perfect mixing (so exit concentration = tank concentration)
- First-order kinetics: $-r_A = k C_A$
- Single input stream of concentration $C_{Af}$

**Species balance on $A$** (3 marks):

$$ \text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} $$

$$ V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = F \, C_{Af} - F \, C_A + (-k C_A) V $$

**Define residence time** $\tau = V/F$ (1 mark) and rearrange:

$$ \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = \dfrac{C_{Af}}{\tau} - \left(k + \dfrac{1}{\tau}\right) C_A $$

**Steady-state** ($dC_A/dt = 0$) (1 mark):

$$ 0 = \dfrac{C_{Af}}{\tau} - \left(k + \dfrac{1}{\tau}\right) C_{A,ss} $$

**Solve for $C_{A,ss}$** (1 mark):

$$ \boxed{\;C_{A,ss} = \dfrac{C_{Af}}{1 + k \tau}\;} $$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, 14 Marks — *Alternative choice to Q A*]

**(a)** *With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the steps involved in developing a mathematical model of a chemical process. Mention at least two examples of how the model is used in industry.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Flowchart of the modelling procedure** (4 marks — full marks for the Mermaid-style flow from Section 4.1):

Define system → Identify phenomena → Apply conservation laws → Add constitutive relations → Specify inputs and parameters → Set IC/BC → Simplify and classify → Solve → Validate → Refine.

**Two industrial uses** (3 marks — 1.5 each):

1. **Process Design:** A heat-exchanger model based on the LMTD equation, $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$, allows engineers to size the area $A$ for a specified duty $Q$ — eliminating the need for expensive pilot-scale trials.

2. **Process Control:** The dynamic model of a distillation column (MESH equations) is the basis for model-predictive controllers (MPC) that maintain product purity despite feed disturbances — used widely in petroleum refineries.

3. (Optional third) **Safety:** A reactor model with Arrhenius kinetics predicts the adiabatic temperature rise and time-to-runaway for a reactor, guiding the design of relief systems.

---

**(b)** *Derive the one-dimensional transient heat conduction equation for a homogeneous slab, clearly stating assumptions and boundary conditions.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
- Homogeneous, isotropic material of constant thermal conductivity $k_b$, density $\rho$, and specific heat $C_p$.
- No internal heat generation.
- One-dimensional conduction (temperature varies only along $x$).
- No convection within the solid (pure conduction).

**Control volume:** Infinitesimal slice of thickness $dx$ and area $A$ (1 mark).

**Energy balance** (3 marks):

$$ \rho C_p A \, dx \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = -k_b A \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_x - \left(-k_b A \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_{x+dx}\right) $$

**Taylor series expansion** of flux at $x+dx$ (1 mark):

$$ \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_{x+dx} = \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_x + \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} dx $$

**Substitute and simplify** (1 mark):

$$ \rho C_p \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k_b \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\;\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}\;} $$

where $\alpha = k_b/(\rho C_p)$ is the thermal diffusivity.

**Typical boundary and initial conditions** (1 mark):
- IC: $T(x, 0) = T_i$
- BC at $x = 0$ (symmetry): $\partial T / \partial x = 0$
- BC at $x = L$ (convective surface): $-k_b \partial T/\partial x = h (T_s - T_\infty)$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong frame of reference:** Many students write the *rate of generation* as $+kC_A$ instead of $-kC_A$. The sign convention is **minus** for *consumption*.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\tau$ definition:** Examiners explicitly award a mark for defining the residence time. Students who skip this lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up lumped vs distributed:** A CSTR is *lumped*; a PFR is *distributed*. A CSTR with measured temperature and concentration profiles inside the tank is, strictly, a *distributed* parameter system — be careful with the question's wording.
> 4. **No units in the final answer:** Always state units explicitly. Final answers without units lose at least 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Missing IC/BC:** A differential equation without initial and boundary conditions is mathematically incomplete. Always close your model with a complete set of conditions.
> 6. **Confusing kinetics with rate of reaction:** $-r_A$ is the *rate of disappearance per unit volume*; multiply by $V$ to get the volumetric rate for the balance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Use this list as a **last-minute revision sheet** the night before the exam.

- [ ] A **process model** is a *set of equations* — not the system itself.
- [ ] The **five classification axes** are: (1) lumped/distributed, (2) steady/dynamic, (3) linear/non-linear, (4) theoretical/empirical/semi-empirical, (5) deterministic/stochastic.
- [ ] The **three fundamental conservation laws** are: mass, energy, momentum — all reduce to "Accumulation = In − Out + Generation".
- [ ] The **four constitutive relations** to memorize: Fourier's law ($q = -k\nabla T$), Fick's law ($J = -D\nabla C$), Newton's law of viscosity ($\tau = -\mu \, dv/dy$), Arrhenius ($k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$).
- [ ] A **lumped model** → ODE; a **distributed model** → PDE.
- [ ] A **steady-state** model has $dX/dt = 0$ → algebraic equation.
- [ ] The **residence time** is $\tau = V/F$ (units: seconds).
- [ ] The **thermal diffusivity** is $\alpha = k_b / (\rho C_p)$ (units: m²/s).
- [ ] Always state **assumptions** before writing a model (isothermal? constant density? perfect mixing? plug flow?).
- [ ] Always specify **initial conditions (IC)** and **boundary conditions (BC)** for differential models.
- [ ] The **modelling procedure** is: *System → Phenomenon → Conservation → Constitutive → Inputs → Parameters → IC/BC → Solve → Validate → Refine*.
- [ ] **Start with the simplest model** that addresses the engineering question; add complexity only when needed.
- [ ] Use **SI units** throughout — $T$ in K, $E_a$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- [ ] A model is **continuously refined** — validation is an iterative loop, not a one-time event.
- [ ] **Linear models** obey superposition and have closed-form solutions; **non-linear models** require numerical solvers (Newton–Raphson, RK4, etc.).
- [ ] For **safety analysis**, kinetic models with Arrhenius temperature dependence are essential to predict runaway behaviour.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Modelling Principles and Fundamental Laws of Chemical Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Modelling Principles and Fundamental Laws of Chemical Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Process Modelling?

**Process Modelling** is the art and science of representing a real chemical/physical process using a set of mathematical equations (algebraic, differential, or integral) that capture the essential behaviour of the system without needing to perform the actual experiment every time.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a *model* is a **mathematical surrogate** of a real process that allows us to predict outputs from given inputs using the laws of physics, chemistry, and thermodynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> A chemical process model is a translation of the **fundamental conservation laws** (mass, energy, momentum) plus **constitutive equations** (equations of state, reaction kinetics, transport laws) into a solvable mathematical framework.

### 1.2 Why do Chemical Engineers Model?

A model is built for any of these five practical reasons:

1. **Design** – sizing a new reactor, distillation column, or heat exchanger.
2. **Scale-up** – transferring lab data to a plant-scale unit.
3. **Optimisation** – finding the best operating point (temperature, pressure, flow).
4. **Control** – designing a PID/controller around the process dynamics.
5. **Safety & Troubleshooting** – predicting runaway reactions, pressure surges, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – System & Surroundings:**
> A **system** is the selected region of the universe chosen for analysis (e.g., a CSTR tank). Everything outside is the **surroundings**. The boundary separating them may be real (reactor wall) or imaginary (a control volume around a pipe section).

### 1.3 Types of Models (Classification Matrix)

| Criterion | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Spatial variation** | Lumped (0-D) | Distributed (1-D, 2-D, 3-D) |
| **Time dependence** | Steady State (SS) | Unsteady / Dynamic (transient) |
| **Predictability** | Deterministic | Stochastic / Probabilistic |
| **Linearity** | Linear | Non-linear |
| **Equation type** | Algebraic | Differential / Integral |

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy – "The Recipe vs. The Cook"

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a model as a **recipe** for predicting how a cake (process) will rise. The *recipe* (model) uses flour, eggs, oven temperature, time (inputs) and predicts cake height, taste, texture (outputs). The *cook* (solver/computer) executes the recipe. A perfect recipe means the cook never has to *guess* — that's the power of a good model.

The **fundamental laws of chemical engineering** are the *ingredients* of every recipe:
- Conservation of Mass → Mass balance
- Conservation of Energy → Energy balance
- Conservation of Momentum → Bernoulli / Navier–Stokes
- Thermodynamic Equation of State → P-V-T relations
- Reaction Kinetics → rate laws
- Transport Laws → Fick's, Fourier's, Newton's laws

These six building blocks are the **axioms** of chemical engineering modelling.

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants (Must Memorise for KTU)

> **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)**
> **Standard Pressure:** **P° = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa**
> **Standard Temperature:** **T° = 25 °C = 298.15 K**
> **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Fundamental Building Blocks of a Chemical Process Model

A complete process model is the **union** of conservation laws and constitutive (closure) equations. The conservation laws *cannot be solved* without the constitutive relations, and vice versa.

### A. Conservation Laws (the "must satisfy" equations)

These are derived from the **General Balance Equation (GBE)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of Accumulation} &= \text{Rate of In} - \text{Rate of Out} \\
&\quad + \text{Rate of Generation} - \text{Rate of Consumption}
\end{aligned}
$$

In symbolic form:

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = \dot{X}_{in} - \dot{X}_{out} + \dot{X}_{gen} - \dot{X}_{cons}
$$

Where $X$ can be **mass, moles, energy, or momentum** (a conserved quantity).

#### (i) Total Mass Balance (Continuity Equation)

$$
\frac{dm}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}
$$

For constant density, incompressible flow: $\dot{m} = \rho \, Q$, where **Q = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)**.

#### (ii) Component Mass (Mole) Balance — for species *i*

$$
\frac{dN_i}{dt} = \sum \dot{n}_{i,in} - \sum \dot{n}_{i,out} + \dot{r}_i \, V
$$

Here $\dot{r}_i$ is the **rate of generation per unit volume** (mol/m³·s), and **V** is the reactor volume.

#### (iii) Energy Balance (First Law of Thermodynamics)

$$
\frac{dU}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s + \sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}
$$

For steady-state, no shaft work, negligible KE/PE:

$$
\dot{Q} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in}
$$

#### (iv) Momentum Balance (Mechanical Energy Balance)

$$
\frac{d(mv)}{dt} = \sum F_{ext} = \dot{m}_{in} v_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} v_{out} + F_{pressure} + F_{gravity} + F_{friction}
$$

### B. Constitutive Equations (the "closure" relations)

These provide the missing physics:

1. **Equation of State** (e.g., Ideal Gas): $P V = n R T$
2. **Reaction Kinetics**: $-r_A = k C_A^n$
3. **Fick's Law (mass diffusion)**: $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$
4. **Fourier's Law (heat conduction)**: $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dz}$
5. **Newton's Law of Viscosity**: $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{dv}{dz}$
6. **Equation of Continuity for component *i*:** $\dfrac{\partial C_i}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J_i} + r_i$

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3 Conservation Laws + 3 Constitutive Transport Laws = the complete framework** for mass, energy, and momentum transport in any chemical process.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Modelling Strategy (The KTU Approach)

When asked to "develop a model" in the KTU exam, always follow this 5-step procedure:

1. **Define the system** – draw a clear schematic, mark the control volume, label all streams (in/out).
2. **List assumptions** – lumped/distributed, SS/transient, ideal/real, isothermal/non-isothermal, well-mixed or not.
3. **Write the General Balance Equation** for the chosen conserved quantity.
4. **Substitute constitutive equations** to close the model.
5. **Simplify & solve** – use steady-state, ideal behaviour, or constant density to reduce the equation to solvable form.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Law / Equation | Mathematical Form | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Balance Equation (GBE) | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \dot{X}_{in} - \dot{X}_{out} + \dot{X}_{gen} - \dot{X}_{cons}$ | Any process model derivation |
| 2 | Total Mass Balance (SS) | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | CSTR, PFR, mixer steady state |
| 3 | Component Balance (SS) | $\dot{n}_{i,in} = \dot{n}_{i,out} + r_i V$ | Reactor design |
| 4 | Ideal Gas Equation of State | $P V = n R T$ | Gases at low pressure, ~1 atm |
| 5 | Real Gas (vdW EOS) | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = nRT$ | Gases near critical point |
| 6 | Energy Balance (SS, no work) | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}(h_{out} - h_{in})$ | Heat exchangers, reactors |
| 7 | Mechanical Energy Balance | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} + \Delta \dfrac{v^2}{2} + g\Delta z + \hat{W}_s + F_{fric} = 0$ | Pipe flow, pumps, turbines |
| 8 | Fick's First Law | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Molecular diffusion |
| 9 | Fourier's Law | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dz}$ | Heat conduction in solids |
| 10 | Newton's Viscosity Law | $\tau_{yx} = -\mu \dfrac{dv_x}{dy}$ | Momentum transport in fluids |
| 11 | Continuity Equation | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$ | Fluid flow, compressible/incompressible |
| 12 | Arrhenius Rate Law | $k = A \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Temperature-dependent reactions |
| 13 | Mole Fraction Definition | $\sum x_i = 1$ | Closing the composition set |
| 14 | Mass Fraction Definition | $\sum w_i = 1$ | Multicomponent mixtures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Pattern Tip:** In Part B questions, examiners expect to see the *General Balance Equation written first*, then the *constitutive substitution*, and finally *steady-state / idealisation assumptions* applied. Skipping the GBE step costs 1–2 marks every time.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Design** → use component mass balance + Arrhenius kinetics.
- **Distillation Column** → component balances on each tray + vapour-liquid equilibrium.
- **Pipeline Sizing** → mechanical energy balance (Bernoulli) + friction factor.
- **Heat Exchanger** → energy balance + overall heat transfer coefficient.
- **Polymerisation** → population balance + kinetic chain-length distribution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Component Mass Balance for an Ideal CSTR

A **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** is well-mixed, so the composition inside equals the exit composition. We apply the GBE to species *A* reacting as $A \rightarrow B$ with rate $-r_A = k C_A$.

**Step 1 — Write the GBE for moles of A:**

$$
\frac{dN_A}{dt} = \dot{n}_{A,in} - \dot{n}_{A,out} + r_A V
$$

**Step 2 — Define the rate of reaction (note the sign convention):**

$$
r_A = -k C_A
$$

**Step 3 — Express molar flow rates in terms of concentration and volumetric flow:**

$$
\dot{n}_A = C_A \, Q
$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate (m³/s) and $C_A$ is the molar concentration (mol/m³).

**Step 4 — Substitute into the GBE:**

$$
\frac{dN_A}{dt} = C_{A,in} Q - C_{A,out} Q - k C_{A,out} V
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the CSTR assumption (perfect mixing):**

Since the contents are well-mixed, $C_A = C_{A,out} = C_{A,inside}$ at all times. Also, $N_A = C_A V$, so:

$$
V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = Q(C_{A,in} - C_A) - k C_A V
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Steady-State assumption** $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= Q(C_{A,in} - C_A) - k C_A V \\
Q C_{A,in} &= Q C_A + k C_A V \\
Q C_{A,in} &= C_A (Q + kV) \\
\boxed{\,C_A = \dfrac{Q \, C_{A,in}}{Q + kV} = \dfrac{C_{A,in}}{1 + k\tau}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

where **$\tau = V/Q$** is the **residence time** (seconds), the canonical design parameter of any flow reactor.

> This is the **most-tested equation** in KTU's process modelling papers — the *design equation of a CSTR*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ideal Gas Law from Kinetic Theory (Abridged)

Start from the molecular-scale pressure expression (ideal monatomic gas):

$$
P = \frac{1}{3} \rho \overline{c^2}
$$

where $\rho$ is the mass density and $\overline{c^2}$ is the mean-square molecular speed. Substituting $\rho = nM/V$ and the kinetic result $\overline{c^2} = 3k_B T/m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{nM}{V} \cdot \frac{3 k_B T}{m} \\
  &= \frac{n M k_B T}{m \, V} \\
  &= \frac{n N_A k_B T}{V} \\
  &= \frac{n R T}{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

since $R = N_A k_B$. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,P V = n R T\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Generalised Bernoulli Equation (Mechanical Energy Balance)

For a control volume at steady-state with no pump or turbine, the first law applied to a fluid element gives:

$$
\int_{V} \rho \frac{D}{Dt}\!\left(\frac{v^2}{2} + gz\right) dV = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{visc}
$$

After integration along a streamline for an **incompressible, inviscid fluid**:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^{\,2}}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^{\,2}}{2} + g z_2
$$

In differential form:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{dP}{\rho} + v\,dv + g\,dz = 0\,}
$$

For a real fluid, friction head loss $h_f$ and pump head $h_p$ are added:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta v^2}{2} + g \Delta z + h_f - h_p = 0
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Conservation vs. Constitutive Laws

| Aspect | Conservation Law | Constitutive Law |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Fundamental physics (cannot be violated) | Empirical / molecular theory |
| Universality | Same form for any substance | Substance-specific |
| Examples | Mass, Energy, Momentum balance | EOS, Fick, Fourier, Newton |
| Closure role | Provides the **skeleton** | Provides the **flesh** |
| Can it stand alone? | No — needs constitutive laws | No — needs conservation laws |
| Test for completeness | Add up: Conservation + Constitutive ⇒ Model | (closed loop) |

## 3.5 Application Example — Flash Drum Modelling

A feed $F$ (mol/s) with composition $z_i$ enters a flash drum at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$. Vapour $V$ leaves the top (composition $y_i$), liquid $L$ leaves the bottom (composition $x_i$). Derive the model.

**Step 1 — Total mass balance (steady state):**

$$
F = V + L
$$

**Step 2 — Component mass balance (component *i*):**

$$
F z_i = V y_i + L x_i
$$

**Step 3 — Vapour–Liquid Equilibrium (constitutive):**

$$
y_i = K_i x_i
$$

where $K_i$ is the **equilibrium constant** (function of $T$, $P$, composition).

**Step 4 — Sum of mole fractions (closure):**

$$
\sum y_i = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum x_i = 1
$$

These four equation sets constitute a complete, closed model of the flash drum. Solving gives $V$, $L$, $y_i$, and $x_i$ for a given $F$, $z_i$, $T$, $P$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Process Modelling Workflow

The following Mermaid block captures the iterative loop followed in any modelling exercise, from physical plant to validated mathematical model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Chemical Process / Plant] --> B[Define System Boundary & CV]
    B --> C[List Assumptions: SS, Lumped, Ideal]
    C --> D[Write General Balance Equation GBE]
    D --> E[Apply Constitutive Laws EOS, Kinetics, Transport]
    E --> F[Mathematical Model Set of ODEs or Algebraic]
    F --> G[Solve Analytically or Numerically]
    G --> H[Simulate and Compare with Plant Data]
    H --> I{Model Validated?}
    I -- No --> J[Refine Assumptions or Add Equations]
    J --> C
    I -- Yes --> K[Use Model for Design Control Optimisation]
```

## 4.2 Architecture Map — The Six Building Blocks of a Process Model

This Mermaid block shows how the three conservation laws and three constitutive transport laws combine to give the complete transport-phenomena framework.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Conservation_Laws
        CL1[Mass Balance]
        CL2[Energy Balance]
        CL3[Momentum Balance]
    end
    subgraph Constitutive_Laws
        CT1[Equation of State: PVT]
        CT2[Reaction Kinetics: Arrhenius]
        CT3[Transport Laws: Fick Fourier Newton]
    end
    CL1 --- CT1
    CL1 --- CT2
    CL2 --- CT1
    CL2 --- CT3
    CL3 --- CT3
    Conservation_Laws --> Model[Complete Process Model]
    Constitutive_Laws --> Model
    Model --> Outputs[Design Control Optimisation Safety]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Model Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Modelling Problem] --> Q1{Spatial variation significant?}
    Q1 -- No --> Lumped[Lumped Parameter Model: ODE]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Dist[Distributed Parameter Model: PDE]
    Lumped --> Q2{Time variation significant?}
    Dist --> Q2
    Q2 -- No --> SS[Steady State: Algebraic Equations]
    Q2 -- Yes --> TR[Transient: Time Dependent ODE or PDE]
    SS --> Q3{Fluid phase?}
    TR --> Q3
    Q3 -- Single --> SP[Single Phase Model]
    Q3 -- Multiple --> MP[Multiphase Model: add phase fractions]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of a Generic Control Volume for Balance Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Fin[Stream In m_in, h_in, x_in] --> CV[Control Volume: Accumulation and Generation]
    CV --> Fout[Stream Out m_out, h_out, x_out]
    Qdot[Heat Transfer Q_dot across boundary] --> CV
    Wdot[Shaft Work W_dot into system] --> CV
    Rgen[Generation or Consumption term rV] --> CV
    CV --> Output[Balance: dX_dt = In - Out + Gen - Cons]
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — How the Six Laws Interact in a Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Inlet[Inlet Stream: F, T_in, C_A_in] --> TotalM[Total Mass Balance]
    TotalM --> CompM[Component Mass Balance]
    Kin[Arrhenius Kinetics: k = A exp(-Ea/RT)] --> CompM
    CompM --> Outlet[Outlet: F, T_out, C_A_out]
    Outlet --> Energy[Energy Balance: Q_dot]
    Energy --> Recirc[Feedback to Component Balance via T dependent k]
    CompM --> Momentum[Momentum Balance for Pumping Pressure Drop]
    Momentum --> PumpSizing[Pump or Compressor Sizing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the General Balance Equation used in chemical process modelling. Mention the physical significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The General Balance Equation (GBE) is written as:
$$\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \dot{X}_{in} - \dot{X}_{out} + \dot{X}_{gen} - \dot{X}_{cons}$$

- **$dX/dt$** — rate of **accumulation** of the conserved quantity $X$ (mass, moles, energy, or momentum) inside the control volume.
- **$\dot{X}_{in} - \dot{X}_{out}$** — net rate of $X$ entering by convection or diffusion.
- **$\dot{X}_{gen} - \dot{X}_{cons}$** — net rate of $X$ produced or destroyed by chemical reaction (zero for non-reacting species and for total mass).

The GBE is the **starting point** of every conservation-law-based model. (3 marks: 1 mark for the equation, 2 marks for explanation of all four terms.)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**Distinguish between lumped parameter and distributed parameter models. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Lumped Parameter | Distributed Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Spatial variation | Negligible (0-D) | Significant (1-D, 2-D, 3-D) |
| Equation type | Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) or algebraic | Partial Differential Equation (PDE) |
| State variable | Function of time only | Function of space *and* time |
| Example | Well-mixed CSTR | Plug Flow Reactor (PFR) along axial coordinate |
| Computational cost | Low | High |

(3 marks: 1 for tabular comparison, 1 for example of each.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Total)

> Each Part B question offers two alternatives. Solve **either** Choice A **or** Choice B in full. Each choice has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

---

### CHOICE A — 14 Marks

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
A liquid mixture containing species **A** at concentration $C_{A,in} = 2.0$ mol/L enters an **ideal CSTR** of volume **V = 500 L** at a volumetric flow rate **Q = 50 L/min**. The first-order reaction $A \rightarrow B$ proceeds with rate constant **k = 0.04 min⁻¹** at the operating temperature.

#### (a) Develop the steady-state mathematical model for this reactor and derive the exit concentration of A. [7 marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the system and assumptions (1 mark):**
- Control volume = the CSTR contents.
- Steady state: $dC_A/dt = 0$.
- Well-mixed: $C_A$ inside = $C_{A,out}$.
- Constant density, single reaction, isothermal.

**Step 2 — Write the GBE for component A (2 marks):**

$$
V\frac{dC_A}{dt} = Q(C_{A,in} - C_A) - k C_A V
$$

**Step 3 — Apply steady state (1 mark):**

$$
0 = Q(C_{A,in} - C_A) - k C_A V
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $C_A$ algebraically (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q C_{A,in} &= C_A (Q + kV) \\
C_A &= \frac{Q C_{A,in}}{Q + kV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values (1 mark):**

$$
C_A = \frac{(50)(2.0)}{50 + (0.04)(500)} = \frac{100}{50 + 20} = \frac{100}{70} = 1.4286 \text{ mol/L}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{C_{A,out} \approx 1.43 \text{ mol/L}}$

#### (b) Calculate the residence time, conversion of A, and the rate of B production in mol/min. [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Residence time (1 mark):**

$$
\tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{500 \text{ L}}{50 \text{ L/min}} = 10 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Conversion of A (2 marks):**

$$
X_A = \frac{C_{A,in} - C_{A,out}}{C_{A,in}} = \frac{2.0 - 1.4286}{2.0} = 0.2857 \quad \text{or} \quad 28.57\%
$$

**Step 3 — Rate of A consumption (1 mark):**

$$
-r_A = k C_{A,out} = (0.04)(1.4286) = 0.05714 \text{ mol/(L·min)}
$$

**Step 4 — Rate of B production (using stoichiometry 1 mol A → 1 mol B) (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{n}_B = r_B \cdot V = (k C_A) V = (0.04)(1.4286)(500) = 28.57 \text{ mol/min}
$$

**Step 5 — Final summary (1 mark):**

- $\tau = 10$ min
- $X_A = 28.57\%$
- $\dot{n}_B = 28.57$ mol/min

---

### CHOICE B — 14 Marks

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
A gas mixture of **N₂ (60 mol%)** and **H₂ (40 mol%)** is fed at a total molar flow rate of **100 mol/s**, temperature **T = 500 K**, and pressure **P = 10 atm** to a perfectly mixed reactor. The mixture behaves as an **ideal gas**.

#### (a) Derive a model to compute the volumetric flow rate at the inlet and the average molecular weight of the mixture. [7 marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — General Balance Equation for total moles (1 mark):**
At steady state, $\sum \dot{n}_{in} = \sum \dot{n}_{out}$ (no reaction for total moles in a non-reacting sense, but we will write it formally).

**Step 2 — Average molecular weight (constitutive, 2 marks):**

$$
\bar{M} = \sum y_i M_i = (0.60)(28) + (0.40)(2) = 16.8 + 0.8 = 17.6 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply ideal gas law to find volumetric flow (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{V}_{in} = \frac{\dot{n} R T}{P}
$$

Convert P to consistent units: $P = 10 \text{ atm} = 10 \times 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1013.25 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.

$$
\dot{V}_{in} = \frac{(100)(8.314)(500)}{1.01325 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{V}_{in} = \frac{415\,700}{1.01325 \times 10^{6}} = 0.4103 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}
$$

**Result:** $\dot{V}_{in} \approx 0.410$ m³/s

**Step 5 — Sanity check (1 mark):**
Using $R = 0.08206$ L·atm/(mol·K) directly:

$$
\dot{V}_{in} = \frac{(100)(0.08206)(500)}{10} = 410.3 \text{ L/s} = 0.4103 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s} \quad \checkmark
$$

#### (b) If the reactor operates at steady state with no reaction (storage only), write the total and component mass balances. What is the exit volumetric flow rate? [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Total mass balance at steady state (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_{in} \dot{V}_{in} = \rho_{out} \dot{V}_{out}
$$

**Step 2 — Component mass balance for N₂ and H₂ (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{n}_{N_2, in} = \dot{n}_{N_2, out} \quad \Rightarrow \quad (0.60)(100) = 60 \text{ mol/s} = \dot{n}_{N_2, out}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2, in} = \dot{n}_{H_2, out} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 40 \text{ mol/s} = \dot{n}_{H_2, out}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion from conservation (1 mark):**
Total molar outflow = 100 mol/s, with the same composition 60% N₂, 40% H₂.

**Step 4 — Exit volumetric flow at same T, P (2 marks):**
Since T, P, and composition are unchanged:

$$
\dot{V}_{out} = \dot{V}_{in} = 0.4103 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\dot{V}_{out} \approx 0.410 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the General Balance Equation (GBE):** Examiners allocate 1–2 marks *just* for writing the GBE before substituting. Never jump straight to the final equation.
> 2. **Sign errors in generation/consumption:** $r_A$ is negative for a reactant A. A common pitfall is writing $+r_A$ instead of $+r_A V$ where $r_A$ already includes the sign.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert atm → Pa, L → m³, or min → s. Always write units explicitly in the answer.
> 4. **Forgetting the closure condition:** $\sum y_i = 1$ or $\sum x_i = 1$ must be stated for any multicomponent model.
> 5. **Mixing up steady-state vs transient:** If the question says "steady state", the $dX/dt$ term is *zero*; if it says "start-up" or "transient", keep the derivative. Marks depend on this.
> 6. **Ideal vs real gas:** Always check the temperature and pressure. Use $PV = nRT$ only when explicitly stated as ideal or when $T > 2T_c$ and $P < 0.1 P_c$.
> 7. **Residence time confusion:** $\tau = V/Q$ in a CSTR, while in a PFR, $d\tau = dV/Q$. Mixing these up is a guaranteed mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **process model** is a mathematical description of a real process using conservation + constitutive laws.
- [ ] The **General Balance Equation (GBE)** is the universal starting point: $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \dot{X}_{in} - \dot{X}_{out} + \dot{X}_{gen} - \dot{X}_{cons}$
- [ ] **Three conservation laws**: mass, energy, momentum.
- [ ] **Three constitutive transport laws**: Fick (mass), Fourier (heat), Newton (momentum).
- [ ] **Equation of state (ideal gas)**: $PV = nRT$ with $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- [ ] **Arrhenius rate law**: $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$.
- [ ] **CSTR design equation (SS, 1st order)**: $C_A = C_{A,in} / (1 + k\tau)$, where $\tau = V/Q$.
- [ ] **Residence time** $\tau = V/Q$ — single most important reactor parameter.
- [ ] **Lumped vs Distributed**: 0-D ODE vs 1-D/2-D/3-D PDE.
- [ ] **Steady state**: $dX/dt = 0$ simplifies balances to algebraic equations.
- [ ] **Closure conditions**: $\sum y_i = 1$ and $\sum x_i = 1$.
- [ ] **Bernoulli equation** (mechanical energy): $\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} + \dfrac{\Delta v^2}{2} + g \Delta z + h_f - h_p = 0$.
- [ ] **Sign convention**: Generation is **+ve** for products, **−ve** for reactants; consumption is the opposite.
- [ ] **Density of water at 25 °C**: $\rho = 997$ kg/m³ (commonly used reference value).
- [ ] **Average molecular weight of air**: $\bar{M}_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol (useful for sanity checks).
- [ ] Always **state assumptions** (steady state, isothermal, ideal, well-mixed) before writing any equation — this is the KTU evaluation rubric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Energy Equations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the rigorous framework of **Process Modelling & Simulation**, an **Energy Equation** (also called the **Energy Balance** or **First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a control volume**) is a mathematical statement that accounts for all forms of energy entering, leaving, accumulating, and being generated or consumed within a defined system boundary over a specified time interval. It is the thermal analogue of the material balance and is a cornerstone for designing reactors, heat exchangers, distillation columns, and full chemical plants. The most general form of the energy balance, expressed on a **rate basis** for a process unit, is: $$ \frac{dE_{\text{sys}}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{s} + \sum_{\text{in}} F_{i} h_{i} - \sum_{\text{out}} F_{j} h_{j} $$ where each term has a precise physical meaning (see Section 2). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 / Energy Equations:** > The course PEPOT527 requires the student to be able to (i) state the general energy balance, (ii) specialise it to ideal reactor types (Batch, CSTR, PFR), and (iii) couple it with the material balance to predict temperature profiles and conversion. This topic is heavily weighted in the End Semester Examination. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Bank Account" Imagine a **savings bank account** that stores money (energy) instead of currency: | Bank Term | Energy Analogue | Sign Convention | |---|---|---| | Money **deposited** into the account | Energy **entering** the system with inlet streams | **Positive** | | Money **withdrawn** from the account | Energy **leaving** with outlet streams | **Negative** | | Interest **earned** (a bonus) | Heat **generated** by exothermic reaction | **Positive** | | Bank **charges** (a debit) | Heat **consumed** by endothermic reaction | **Negative** | | Net change in balance over a month | **Accumulation** of energy inside the system | Can be + or − | | External cash deposit (gift) | Heat transfer $\dot{Q}$ from a jacket/coil | **Positive** in | | Automatic utility deduction | Shaft work $\dot{W}_{s}$ done **by** the system | **Negative** | Just as `Final Balance = Initial Balance + Deposits − Withdrawals + Interest − Charges`, the energy equation states: $$ \text{Final Energy} = \text{Initial Energy} + \text{Energy In} - \text{Energy Out} + \text{Generated} - \text{Consumed} $$ ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$ - **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_{\text{ref}} = 298.15 \; \text{K}$ (or $25^{\circ}\text{C}$) - **Standard Reference Pressure:** $P_{\text{ref}} = 1 \; \text{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$ - **Standard Heat of Formation** $\Delta H_{f}^{\circ}$: enthalpy change when 1 mole of a compound is formed from its elements in their standard states — unit: **kJ/mol**. - **Standard Heat of Combustion** $\Delta H_{c}^{\circ}$: enthalpy change when 1 mole of a substance burns completely in $\text{O}_{2}$ to give products $\text{CO}_{2}$ and $\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$ at standard conditions — unit: **kJ/mol**. > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** Heat **released** by the system is **negative** (exothermic, $\Delta H < 0$), and heat **absorbed** is **positive** (endothermic, $\Delta H > 0$). This is the IUPAC thermochemical convention. Always confirm which convention your data table uses before solving numerical problems. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The General Energy Balance — Five Logical Terms The First Law of Thermodynamics applied to an open system (control volume) yields the **General Energy Balance**. The student must internalise that it contains exactly **five distinct physical terms**, regardless of the reactor geometry. ### Step 1 — Accumulation Term $$ \text{Accumulation} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[ \int_{V} \rho \, u \, dV \right] $$ **Why?** It represents the rate of change of internal energy stored inside the system. For steady-state, it is **zero**. For unsteady/transient operation, it must be retained. ### Step 2 — Convective Energy Flow $$ \dot{E}_{\text{flow}} = \sum_{\text{out}} F_{j} h_{j} \; - \sum_{\text{in}} F_{i} h_{i} $$ **Why?** A flowing stream carries both internal energy $u$ and flow work $Pv$. Combining them gives the **specific enthalpy** $h = u + Pv$, which is the natural energy currency for flowing streams. ### Step 3 — Heat Transfer $$ \dot{Q} = \text{rate of heat addition through the system boundary (e.g., jacket, coil)} $$ Positive when heat enters the system. ### Step 4 — Shaft Work $$ \dot{W}_{s} = \text{rate of mechanical/electrical work crossing the boundary} $$ Positive when work is done **on** the system (e.g., agitator, compressor). ### Step 5 — Heat of Reaction (Generation / Consumption) $$ \dot{E}_{\text{rxn}} = - \sum_{k} \Delta H_{k}(T) \, r_{k} \, V $$ The negative sign arises from the sign convention: an exothermic reaction releases heat, contributing **positively** to the system energy when $\Delta H < 0$. ## 2.2 Simplified Steady-State Form For a **steady-state** process with a **single inlet** and **single outlet**, no shaft work, and one reaction, the energy balance collapses to a beautiful, compact form: $$ \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{s} + F_{\text{in}} h_{\text{in}} - F_{\text{out}} h_{\text{out}} + \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, \xi = 0 $$ where $\xi$ is the **extent of reaction** in mol/s (or kmol/s). ## 2.3 Specialisation to Reactor Types | Reactor Type | Accumulation | Form of Energy Balance | |---|---|---| | **Batch** (unsteady, closed) | $\neq 0$ | $\frac{dU}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{s} - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, r \, V$ | | **CSTR** (steady, well-mixed) | $= 0$ | $0 = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{s} + F_{0} h_{0} - F h - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, r \, V$ | | **PFR** (steady, plug flow) | $= 0$ | $0 = \dot{Q}_{z} + F \, \frac{dh}{dz} dz - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T) \, r(z) \, dV$ | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formulas — Memorise These for the ESE.** The table below contains every equation you are likely to be tested on under the topic "Energy Equations" in PEPOT527 Module 1. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Typical Units | When to Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | First Law (open system, rate form) | $\frac{dE}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{s} + F_{\text{in}} h_{\text{in}} - F_{\text{out}} h_{\text{out}}$ | $\text{J/s} = \text{W}$ | Any steady or unsteady open system | | 2 | Definition of enthalpy | $h = u + Pv$ | $\text{J/mol}$ | Converting between $u$ and $h$ | | 3 | Sensible heat change | $\Delta h_{\text{sens}} = \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} C_{p} \, dT$ | $\text{J/mol}$ | Heating/cooling a stream with no phase change | | 4 | Constant-$C_p$ sensible heat | $\Delta h_{\text{sens}} = C_{p}(T - T_{\text{ref}})$ | $\text{J/mol}$ | Approximation valid over small $\Delta T$ | | 5 | Heat of reaction at temperature $T$ (Kirchhoff) | $\Delta H_{T}^{\circ} = \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_{p} \, dT$ | $\text{J/mol}$ | When reaction temperature $\neq 298 \; \text{K}$ | | 6 | CSTR energy balance | $0 = \dot{Q} - F_{0} C_{p}(T - T_{0}) - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} \, r \, V$ | $\text{W}$ | Well-mixed continuous reactor | | 7 | PFR energy balance | $\dot{m} C_{p} \frac{dT}{dz} = -\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} \, r(T) - U A (T - T_{a})$ | $\text{W/m}$ | Tubular reactor with possible heat exchange | | 8 | Batch energy balance | $N C_{p} \frac{dT}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} \, r \, V$ | $\text{W}$ | Closed vessel, unsteady | | 9 | Adiabatic flame / reactor temperature | $T_{\text{ad}} = T_{0} + \frac{-\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} \, X}{C_{p,\text{avg}}}$ | $\text{K}$ | No heat exchange ($\dot{Q}=0$) | | 10 | Heat of reaction from heats of formation | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = \sum \nu_{i} \Delta H_{f,i}^{\circ}(\text{prod}) - \sum \nu_{i} \Delta H_{f,i}^{\circ}(\text{react})$ | $\text{kJ/mol}$ | When only $\Delta H_{f}^{\circ}$ data is given | | 11 | Heat of reaction from heats of combustion | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = \sum \nu_{i} \Delta H_{c,i}^{\circ}(\text{react}) - \sum \nu_{i} \Delta H_{c,i}^{\circ}(\text{prod})$ | $\text{kJ/mol}$ | When only $\Delta H_{c}^{\circ}$ data is given | | 12 | Overall heat transfer | $\dot{Q} = U A \, \Delta T_{\text{LM}}$ | $\text{W}$ | Jacket / coil with known $UA$ | | 13 | Log-mean temperature difference | $\Delta T_{\text{LM}} = \frac{\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2}}{\ln(\Delta T_{1}/\Delta T_{2})}$ | $\text{K}$ | Counter-/co-current heat exchangers | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > The energy equation is not just an academic exercise — it is the **mathematical heart of every chemical plant simulator** (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, CHEMCAD, DWSIM). When you click "Run" in such software, it simultaneously solves the material balance, the energy balance, the phase equilibrium, and the reaction kinetics in a tightly coupled Newton–Raphson iteration. Mastering these equations by hand is exactly what trains you to debug, validate, and interpret such simulations later in your career. Specific industrial applications include: - **Reactor Safety:** predicting runaway temperatures in exothermic polymerisations. - **Reactor Scale-up:** sizing cooling jackets for production-scale vessels. - **Energy Integration:** pinch analysis and heat-exchanger network synthesis. - **Furnace & Boiler Design:** adiabatic flame temperature calculations. - **Cryogenic Processes:** liquefaction of $\text{N}_{2}$, $\text{O}_{2}$, $\text{LNG}$. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Every line of algebra is shown explicitly. No logical gap is left for the reader to fill. This section is designed so that a KTU student can reproduce the derivations verbatim in the exam hall. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Balance for a CSTR (Continuous Stirred-Tank Reactor) **Starting point:** The general First Law rate equation for an open system: $$ \frac{dE_{\text{sys}}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{s} + \sum_{\text{in}} F_{i} h_{i} - \sum_{\text{out}} F_{j} h_{j} $$ **Assumption 1 — Steady State:** The contents of the CSTR are perfectly mixed, and the operating conditions do not change with time. Therefore: $$ \frac{dE_{\text{sys}}}{dt} = 0 $$ **Assumption 2 — Negligible Shaft Work:** For most liquid-phase CSTRs, the agitator work is small compared with the heat of reaction. We set: $$ \dot{W}_{s} \approx 0 $$ **Assumption 3 — Single Inlet, Single Outlet:** Total molar flow $F_{0}$ enters with enthalpy $h_{0}$, and total molar flow $F$ leaves with enthalpy $h$. By steady-state mass balance, $F = F_{0}$. The energy equation becomes: $$ 0 = \dot{Q} + F_{0} h_{0} - F h + \dot{E}_{\text{rxn}} $$ **Step A — Expand the stream enthalpies around a reference temperature $T_{\text{ref}}$:** We use $h = h^{\circ}(T_{\text{ref}}) + \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} C_{p} \, dT$. Subtracting $h_{0}$ from $h$ eliminates the reference enthalpy because **both** streams are measured from the **same** $T_{\text{ref}}$: $$ h - h_{0} = \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} C_{p} \, dT - \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T_{0}} C_{p} \, dT = \int_{T_{0}}^{T} C_{p} \, dT $$ **Step B — Approximate $C_{p}$ as constant over the operating range:** $$ \int_{T_{0}}^{T} C_{p} \, dT \approx C_{p}(T - T_{0}) $$ **Step C — Express the heat of reaction rate term:** For a single reaction with rate $r$ (mol per volume per time) occurring in volume $V$, the rate of energy release is: $$ \dot{E}_{\text{rxn}} = -\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T) \, r \, V $$ The negative sign appears because the reaction is **inside** the system: exothermic ($\Delta H < 0$) heats the system up, endothermic cools it. **Step D — Assemble the CSTR Energy Balance:** $$ 0 = \dot{Q} + F_{0} \, C_{p}(T_{0} - T) - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T) \, r \, V $$ **Final Form (KTU Board Standard Notation):** $$ \boxed{ \; \dot{Q} - F_{0} C_{p}(T - T_{0}) - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T) \, r \, V = 0 \; } $$ **Physical Interpretation of Each Term:** - $\dot{Q}$ — heat added through jacket/coil (W). - $F_{0} C_{p}(T - T_{0})$ — sensible heat required to raise inlet from $T_{0}$ to reactor temperature $T$ (W). - $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, r \, V$ — heat released (negative) or absorbed (positive) by reaction (W). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Balance for a PFR (Plug Flow Reactor) **Starting point:** Apply the First Law to a differential slice $dz$ of the PFR at axial position $z$. The slice has volume $dV = A_{c} dz$, where $A_{c}$ is the cross-sectional area. **Steady state**, **no shaft work**, and the stream carries total enthalpy flow $F(z) h(z)$ at the inlet of the slice and $F(z) h(z) + d[F h]$ at the outlet. The differential energy balance over the slice is: $$ 0 = \dot{Q}_{\text{slice}} - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, r \, dV + F h \big\vert_{z} - F h \big\vert_{z+dz} $$ The last two terms combine into the negative of a derivative: $$ F h \big\vert_{z} - F h \big\vert_{z+dz} = - d(F h) = - F \, dh - h \, dF $$ For a constant-density (or constant-volumetric-flow) system, $dF = 0$ (total moles conserved in a single reaction only if no change in total moles; we ignore this nuance and use $F$ as a constant mass-based flow). Then: $$ 0 = \dot{Q}_{\text{slice}} - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, r \, dV - F \, dh $$ **Step A — Relate $dh$ to $dT$:** For a single phase with constant $C_{p}$: $$ dh = C_{p} \, dT $$ **Step B — Relate $\dot{Q}_{\text{slice}}$ to a heat-transfer model:** If the PFR has a coolant at temperature $T_{a}$ with overall coefficient $U$ and surface area $dA$: $$ \dot{Q}_{\text{slice}} = U \, dA \, (T_{a} - T) $$ Often $dA$ is written as $a \, dV$ where $a$ is the surface area per unit volume of the tube. **Step C — Final PFR Energy Balance:** $$ \boxed{ \; F C_{p} \frac{dT}{dz} = -\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T) \, r(T) + U \, a \, [T_{a} - T(z)] \; } $$ This is an **ordinary differential equation** in $z$, coupled with the material balance $$ F \frac{dX}{dz} = r(T, X) \, A_{c} $$ The two ODEs are solved simultaneously to obtain the temperature and conversion profiles $T(z)$ and $X(z)$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Balance for a Batch Reactor A batch reactor is **closed** (no flow in or out) and **unsteady** (composition and temperature change with time). The accumulation term therefore survives. **Starting point:** $$ \frac{dU}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{s} - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, r \, V $$ **Step A — Convert internal energy to enthalpy:** For a constant-volume liquid-phase batch with no shaft work, $dU \approx dH = N C_{p} dT$, where $N$ is the total moles in the vessel. **Step B — Final form:** $$ \boxed{ \; N C_{p} \frac{dT}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T) \, r(T) \, V \; } $$ This ODE is integrated over time to obtain the temperature trajectory $T(t)$ and (via the material balance) the conversion trajectory $X(t)$. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Kirchhoff's Law (Heat of Reaction at Any Temperature) It is often necessary to evaluate $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}$ at the actual reactor temperature $T$ when tabulated data is only available at the standard reference $T_{\text{ref}} = 298 \; \text{K}$. **Hypothetical path argument:** Consider the reaction $$ \text{A} \rightarrow \text{B}, \qquad \Delta H_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{\circ} $$ We can go from reactants at $T$ to products at $T$ via a three-step hypothetical path: 1. Cool reactants from $T$ to $T_{\text{ref}}$: releases/absorbs $\int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} C_{p,A} \, dT$. 2. React at $T_{\text{ref}}$: gives $\Delta H_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{\circ}$. 3. Heat products from $T_{\text{ref}}$ to $T$: releases/absorbs $\int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} C_{p,B} \, dT$. Because enthalpy is a **state function**, the total $\Delta H$ is independent of the path: $$ \Delta H_{T}^{\circ} = \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} C_{p,A} \, dT + \Delta H_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{\circ} + \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} (-C_{p,B}) \, dT $$ Collecting the two heat-capacity integrals: $$ \Delta H_{T}^{\circ} = \Delta H_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{\circ} + \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} \left( C_{p,A} - C_{p,B} \right) dT $$ Defining $\Delta C_{p} \equiv C_{p,B} - C_{p,A}$ (products − reactants) and flipping the sign in the integral: $$ \boxed{ \; \Delta H_{T}^{\circ} = \Delta H_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{\circ} + \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} \Delta C_{p} \, dT \; } $$ For constant $\Delta C_{p}$: $$ \Delta H_{T}^{\circ} = \Delta H_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{\circ} + \Delta C_{p} (T - T_{\text{ref}}) $$ ## 3.5 Worked Example — Adiabatic CSTR Temperature **Problem (typical KTU 2-mark / short answer):** A liquid-phase elementary reaction $\text{A} \rightarrow \text{B}$ takes place adiabatically in a CSTR. Inlet conditions: $F_{A0} = 10 \; \text{mol/s}$, $T_{0} = 350 \; \text{K}$, $C_{p} = 200 \; \text{J/(mol\cdot K)}$ (assumed constant), $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = -80 \; \text{kJ/mol}$. Find the operating temperature at $X = 0.80$. **Solution:** Adiabatic means $\dot{Q} = 0$. The CSTR energy balance reduces to: $$ -F_{A0} C_{p}(T - T_{0}) - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} \, r \, V = 0 $$ But by the material balance, $r \, V = F_{A0} X$. Substituting: $$ -F_{A0} C_{p}(T - T_{0}) = \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} \, F_{A0} X $$ The flow $F_{A0}$ cancels: $$ -T + T_{0} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} \, X}{C_{p}} $$ Solving for $T$: $$ T = T_{0} - \frac{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} \, X}{C_{p}} $$ Plug in values (note unit conversion: $\Delta H = -80{,}000 \; \text{J/mol}$): $$ T = 350 \; \text{K} - \frac{(-80{,}000)(0.80)}{200} = 350 + 320 = 670 \; \text{K} $$ **Answer:** The adiabatic operating temperature is $T = 670 \; \text{K} = 397^{\circ}\text{C}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check:** The reaction is exothermic ($\Delta H < 0$), and the reactor is adiabatic (no heat removed). The temperature **must** rise above $T_0$ — and indeed it does, by $320 \; \text{K}$. This is the hallmark of adiabatic exothermic operation and a common cause of thermal runaway in industrial reactors. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagrams render the logical architecture of an energy balance and the standard solution procedure. They are designed to be drawn free-hand or reproduced exactly in the answer script for full marks. ## 4.1 Energy-Balance Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Inlet Stream 1
F1, h1, T1] --> B1[Mixing Point] A2[Inlet Stream 2
F2, h2, T2] --> B1 A3[Inlet Stream N
FN, hN, TN] --> B1 B1 --> C[System Control Volume
Reactor, Mixer, etc.] E[Heat Transfer Q_dot
Jacket, Coil, Fired Heater] --> C F[Shaft Work W_dot
Agitator, Compressor] --> C C --> D1[Outlet Stream 1
F, h, T] C --> D2[Outlet Stream 2
F, h, T] C --> D3[Outlet Stream N
F, h, T] G[Heat of Reaction
Delta H r V] -.-> C H[Accumulation
dE by dt] -.-> C ``` **How to read this diagram:** - **Solid arrows** = material/energy flow crossing the system boundary. - **Dashed arrows** = energy sources/sinks generated or stored **inside** the boundary. - The control volume is the central object to which the First Law is applied. ## 4.2 Energy-Balance Solution Procedure (Algorithm) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Start: Draw the system and label all streams] --> P2[Write material balances
Identify known X, F_A0, etc.] P2 --> P3{Is temperature constant?} P3 -- Yes, isothermal --> P4[Set T = T_specified
Energy balance gives Q_dot] P3 -- No, non-isothermal --> P5[Write energy balance
in terms of T] P5 --> P6[Couple with material balance
Express r in terms of X and T] P6 --> P7{Choose reactor type} P7 -- CSTR --> P8[Algebraic equation
Solve for T or Q_dot] P7 -- PFR --> P9[ODE in z
Integrate numerically] P7 -- Batch --> P10[ODE in t
Integrate numerically] P8 --> P11[Validate: sign, magnitude, units] P9 --> P11 P10 --> P11 P4 --> P11 P11 --> P12[End: Report T, Q_dot, conversion] ``` ## 4.3 State-Function Path Diagram (Kirchhoff's Law) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Reactants at T] -- delta H T --> S2[Products at T] S1 -- cool to T_ref --> S3[Reactants at T_ref] S3 -- react at T_ref
delta H standard --> S4[Products at T_ref] S4 -- heat to T --> S2 ``` **Interpretation:** The direct path $\text{Reactants}(T) \to \text{Products}(T)$ gives $\Delta H_T^{\circ}$. The three-step detour through the reference temperature must give the **same** total $\Delta H$ because enthalpy is a state function. This is the conceptual basis of Kirchhoff's Law (Section 3.4). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Reasons for Losing Marks:** > 1. **Sign error on $\Delta H$** — confusing exothermic (negative) with endothermic (positive). KTU examiners deduct a full mark for sign errors in energy equations. > 2. **Forgetting the $F_{A0}$ term** — many students write $C_p(T-T_0)$ without multiplying by the molar flow. Always include the flow term. > 3. **Mixing up $\Delta H_f$ and $\Delta H_c$** — when computing $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}$, remember: from formation, products − reactants; from combustion, reactants − products. The order flips! > 4. **Skipping the reference state** — every enthalpy must be measured from a stated $T_{\text{ref}}$.
