If the inlet is not at 298 K, the sensible-heat integral $\int_{T_0}^{T} C_p dT$ must appear. > 5. **Not specifying steady state** — KTU expects you to **state** your assumptions (steady, well-mixed, constant $C_p$, etc.) before writing the final equation. One mark is reserved for the assumption list. --- ## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **State the general energy balance equation for an open, non-steady system. Explain each term with its unit.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The general energy balance on a rate basis is: $$ \frac{dE_{\text{sys}}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{s} + \sum_{\text{in}} F_{i} h_{i} - \sum_{\text{out}} F_{j} h_{j} $$ | Term | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---| | $\frac{dE_{\text{sys}}}{dt}$ | Rate of accumulation of energy inside the system | $\text{W}$ or $\text{J/s}$ | | $\dot{Q}$ | Rate of heat transfer to the system through the boundary (positive in) | $\text{W}$ | | $\dot{W}_{s}$ | Rate of shaft work done **by** the system (positive out) | $\text{W}$ | | $F_i h_i$ | Energy carried in by inlet stream $i$ | $\text{W}$ | | $F_j h_j$ | Energy carried out by outlet stream $j$ | $\text{W}$ | (1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for naming the terms, 1 mark for units and sign convention.) ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted] **Differentiate between the standard heat of formation and the standard heat of combustion. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Standard Heat of Formation ($\Delta H_f^{\circ}$):** The enthalpy change when **one mole** of a compound is formed from its constituent elements in their **standard states** (1 atm, 25 °C). Example: $\text{C(s)} + \text{O}_{2}\text{(g)} \to \text{CO}_{2}\text{(g)}$, $\Delta H_f^{\circ} = -393.5 \; \text{kJ/mol}$. - **Standard Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H_c^{\circ}$):** The enthalpy change when **one mole** of a substance undergoes complete combustion in oxygen to yield $\text{CO}_{2}\text{(g)}$ and $\text{H}_{2}\text{O(l)}$ at standard conditions. Example: $\text{CH}_{4}\text{(g)} + 2\text{O}_{2}\text{(g)} \to \text{CO}_{2}\text{(g)} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O(l)}$, $\Delta H_c^{\circ} = -890.4 \; \text{kJ/mol}$. **Key distinction:** Formation starts from **elements**; combustion ends at **fully oxidised products** $\text{CO}_{2}$ and $\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$. By convention, $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ of an element in its standard state is **zero**; $\Delta H_c^{\circ}$ of $\text{CO}_{2}$ and $\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$ is **zero**. (1.5 marks each for definition + example.) --- ## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between Question A and Question B. --- ### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme] — 14 Marks **(a)** Derive the energy balance equation for a **CSTR** carrying out a single liquid-phase reaction $\text{A} \rightarrow \text{B}$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The exothermic liquid-phase reaction $\text{A} \rightarrow \text{B}$ is carried out in a CSTR. Given: - $F_{A0} = 50 \; \text{mol/s}$ - $T_0 = 300 \; \text{K}$ - $X = 0.70$ - $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = -60{,}000 \; \text{J/mol}$ (assumed constant) - $C_p = 180 \; \text{J/(mol\cdot K)}$ (constant for both A and B) - Reactor operates **adiabatically**. Calculate the operating temperature $T$ and the heat that **must be removed** if the temperature is to be limited to $T_{\max} = 340 \; \text{K}$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** The derivation follows the four-step procedure of Section 3.1. Key marks distribution: - (1 mark) Writing the general First Law equation. - (1 mark) Listing assumptions: steady state, well-mixed, constant $C_p$, no shaft work, single reaction, constant density. - (2 marks) Expanding the enthalpy difference $h - h_0 = C_p(T - T_0)$. - (2 marks) Introducing the reaction term $-\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, r \, V$ with the correct sign. - (1 mark) Final boxed equation: $$ 0 = \dot{Q} - F_{A0} C_p (T - T_0) - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, r \, V $$ **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Adiabatic temperature:** Set $\dot{Q} = 0$. By material balance, $rV = F_{A0} X$: $$ 0 = 0 - F_{A0} C_p (T - T_0) - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, F_{A0} X $$ Cancel $F_{A0}$: $$ T = T_0 - \frac{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, X}{C_p} $$ Substitute: $$ T_{\text{ad}} = 300 - \frac{(-60{,}000)(0.70)}{180} = 300 + 233.33 = 533.33 \; \text{K} $$ This is far above $T_{\max} = 340 \; \text{K}$ — so the reactor must be cooled. **Step 2 — Heat to be removed for $T = 340 \; \text{K}$:** Use the same CSTR energy balance but solve for $\dot{Q}$: $$ \dot{Q} = F_{A0} C_p (T - T_0) + \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, F_{A0} X $$ Substitute (with $T = 340 \; \text{K}$): $$ \dot{Q} = (50)(180)(340 - 300) + (-60{,}000)(50)(0.70) $$ $$ \dot{Q} = (50)(180)(40) + (-2{,}100{,}000) $$ $$ \dot{Q} = 360{,}000 - 2{,}100{,}000 = -1{,}740{,}000 \; \text{W} $$ The negative sign indicates heat must be **removed** from the system. **Final Answer:** The adiabatic temperature is $T_{\text{ad}} = 533.3 \; \text{K}$. To limit the temperature to $340 \; \text{K}$ at $X = 0.70$, the cooling system must remove $\dot{Q} = -1.74 \; \text{MW}$ (i.e., 1.74 MW of heat duty). **(7 marks: 3 for adiabatic T, 3 for the heat duty equation and substitution, 1 for the final answer with correct sign and units.)** --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme] — 14 Marks (Alternative to Question A) **(a)** Derive the energy balance equation for a **PFR** carrying out a single gas-phase reaction $\text{A} \rightarrow \text{B}$, including a coolant term. Show all steps. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The elementary gas-phase reaction $\text{A} \rightarrow \text{B}$ is carried out in a PFR operating at steady state. The rate law is $r = k C_A$ with $k = 0.05 \; \text{s}^{-1}$ at $350 \; \text{K}$ and an activation energy $E = 80 \; \text{kJ/mol}$. The reactor has $F_{A0} = 1 \; \text{mol/s}$, $C_{A0} = 0.1 \; \text{mol/L}$, $T_0 = 350 \; \text{K}$, $C_p = 40 \; \text{J/(mol\cdot K)}$, and $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = -30{,}000 \; \text{J/mol}$. The reactor is **adiabatic**. Find the maximum temperature reached and the corresponding conversion. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Follow the four-step procedure of Section 3.2. Mark distribution: - (1 mark) Drawing the differential slice and writing the general energy balance. - (1 mark) Applying steady state and the relation $F h \vert_z - F h \vert_{z+dz} = -F \, dh$. - (2 marks) Substituting $dh = C_p dT$ and the reaction term $-\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, r \, dV$. - (2 marks) Adding the coolant term $U \, dA \, (T_a - T)$ or $U a (T_a - T) dV$. - (1 mark) Final boxed equation: $$ F C_p \frac{dT}{dz} = -\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T) \, r(T) + U a \, (T_a - T) $$ **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Adiabatic PFR ($\dot{Q}=0$) energy balance reduces to:** $$ F C_p \frac{dT}{dz} = -\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, r(z) $$ Material balance: $$ F \frac{dX}{dz} = r \, A_c $$ Dividing the two equations (to eliminate $dz$ and $r$): $$ \frac{dT}{dX} = \frac{-\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}}{C_p} $$ **Step 2 — Integrate from inlet ($T_0 = 350$, $X = 0$) to exit ($T$, $X$):** $$ \int_{T_0}^{T} dT = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}}{C_p} \int_{0}^{X} dX $$ $$ T - T_0 = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \, X}{C_p} $$ $$ T = 350 - \frac{(-30{,}000) X}{40} = 350 + 750 X $$ **Step 3 — Maximum temperature occurs at maximum conversion. For a first-order PFR, $X \to 1$ as $z \to \infty$. Therefore:** $$ T_{\max} = 350 + 750(1.0) = 1100 \; \text{K} $$ **Step 4 — Account for temperature dependence of $k$ (more rigorous):** Using the Arrhenius equation with $T_{\text{ref}} = 350 \; \text{K}$: $$ k(T) = k_0 \exp\!\left[ -\frac{E}{R}\!\left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{350} \right) \right] $$ Since $k$ increases with $T$, the reaction accelerates as it proceeds. The actual $T$–$X$ relation with $\Delta C_p \neq 0$ would also modify the slope. For the KTU exam, the linearised form $T = 350 + 750X$ is sufficient for full marks. **Final Answer:** For an adiabatic PFR with the given data, $T_{\max} \approx 1100 \; \text{K}$ at $X \to 1.0$. **(7 marks: 2 for setting up the energy balance, 2 for the integration, 2 for the numerical substitution, 1 for the final answer with units.)** --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Exam Hall** - **General Energy Balance** has exactly **five terms**: accumulation, convective flow (in − out), $\dot{Q}$, $\dot{W}_s$, and reaction heat. - **Sign Convention (IUPAC):** $\Delta H < 0$ for exothermic (heat released); $\Delta H > 0$ for endothermic (heat absorbed). $\dot{Q} > 0$ if heat enters the system. - **Enthalpy, not internal energy**, is the energy currency for flowing streams: $h = u + Pv$. - **CSTR (steady):** $\dot{Q} - F_0 C_p (T - T_0) - \Delta H \, r V = 0$ — an **algebraic** equation for $T$ or $\dot{Q}$. - **PFR (steady):** $F C_p dT/dz = -\Delta H \, r(T) + U a (T_a - T)$ — an **ODE in $z$**, coupled with material balance. - **Batch (unsteady):** $N C_p dT/dt = \dot{Q} - \Delta H \, r V$ — an **ODE in $t$**. - **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\Delta H_T = \Delta H_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$ — required when the reactor temperature is far from 298 K. - **From $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$:** $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,\text{prod}}^{\circ} - \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,\text{react}}^{\circ}$. - **From $\Delta H_c^{\circ}$:** $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{c,\text{react}}^{\circ} - \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{c,\text{prod}}^{\circ}$ (note the **flip** in order). - **Adiabatic temperature rise** for an exothermic reaction in a CSTR: $T_{\text{ad}} = T_0 - \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} X / C_p$. - **Always state your assumptions** (steady state, well-mixed, constant $C_p$, no shaft work) — KTU awards 1–2 marks for this list alone. - **Always check units:** $\dot{Q}$ in $\text{W}$, $F$ in $\text{mol/s}$, $C_p$ in $\text{J/(mol\cdot K)}$, $\Delta H$ in $\text{J/mol}$, $V$ in $\text{m}^{3}$ or $\text{L}$. - **Sanity-check the answer** — exothermic + adiabatic must give $T > T_0$; endothermic + adiabatic must give $T < T_0$.

topics.title: Continuity Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Continuity Equation** is the fundamental mathematical statement of the **Conservation of Mass** principle for a flowing fluid. It is the governing partial differential equation (PDE) that expresses the rate of change of mass within a control volume in terms of the net mass flux across its boundaries, plus any volumetric sources or sinks.

In its most general form, applied to a fluid of density $\rho$ moving with velocity vector $\vec{v}$, the continuity equation states that:

> *The rate of accumulation of mass inside a control volume equals the net rate of mass entering the control volume plus the rate of mass generation by chemical reactions inside it.*

For a single-phase, single-component system with no chemical reaction, the principle of conservation of mass reduces to the **simple statement that the total mass flow rate entering a control volume equals the total mass flow rate leaving it, plus the rate of accumulation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The continuity equation forms the foundation of all transport process models (momentum, heat, and mass transfer). It is the *first* differential balance equation a Process Engineer derives in transport phenomena.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy **Kerala state road intersection in Kochi** with a single-lane entry and a single-lane exit.

- **If the number of vehicles entering per minute equals the number leaving**, the number of vehicles waiting at the signal remains constant — *steady-state continuity*.
- **If more vehicles enter than leave**, the queue grows — *mass accumulation*.
- **If fewer vehicles enter than leave** (perhaps one lane is closed), the queue shrinks — *mass depletion*.

Now substitute "vehicles" with "fluid mass (kg)" and the lane with the "control surface" — you have the **continuity equation**. Mathematically:

$$
\underbrace{\text{Rate of Mass In}}_{\text{Vehicles entering}} - \underbrace{\text{Rate of Mass Out}}_{\text{Vehicles leaving}} + \underbrace{\text{Rate of Generation}}_{\text{New cars appearing}} = \underbrace{\text{Rate of Accumulation}}_{\text{Queue length change}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Involved:**
> - **Density** ($\rho$) — measured in **kg/m³** (SI unit).
> - **Velocity** ($\vec{v}$) — measured in **m/s**.
> - **Mass flow rate** ($\dot{m}$) — measured in **kg/s**.
> - **Volumetric flow rate** ($Q$) — measured in **m³/s**.

### 1.3 The Three Different Forms of Continuity

| Form | Mathematical Nature | Typical Use |
|------|--------------------|------------|
| **Integral (System) Form** | Applied to a fixed finite control volume | Process equipment design, macroscopic mass balances |
| **Differential (Point) Form** | Applied at a fluid particle in space | CFD simulations, transport phenomena derivations |
| **Macroscopic/One-Dimensional Form** | Applied to 1-D flow (pipes) | Piping networks, plug flow reactors (PFR) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

#### Step 1: The Fundamental Conservation Principle

At its core, the continuity equation is a **bookkeeping statement**. For any arbitrary control volume $V$ bounded by a control surface $S$:

$$
\underbrace{\frac{d}{dt} \iiint_{V} \rho \, dV}_{\text{(A) Rate of mass accumulation}} = \underbrace{-\oiint_{S} \rho \, \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS}_{\text{(B) Net mass efflux}} + \underbrace{\iiint_{V} R \, dV}_{\text{(C) Mass generation rate}}
$$

Where:
- $\hat{n}$ = outward unit normal vector to the surface
- $R$ = volumetric rate of mass generation (kg/m³·s)

#### Step 2: Reduction to Incompressible, Non-Reactive Flow

For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho = \text{constant}$) with **no chemical reaction** ($R = 0$):

$$
\iiint_{S} \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS = 0
$$

For an **incompressible fluid**, the volume is conserved, which means:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0
$$

#### Step 3: The Differential Form (Most General)

Using the **Divergence Theorem** (Gauss's Theorem) to convert the surface integral to a volume integral:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = R
$$

For non-reactive flow ($R = 0$):

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0
$$

#### Step 4: Expansion in Rectangular Coordinates

Expanding the divergence operator $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$ in **Cartesian** $(x, y, z)$:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

#### Step 5: Expansion in Cylindrical Coordinates

For **cylindrical** $(r, \theta, z)$ systems (very common in process engineering for pipes, tanks, reactors):

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Equation** | **Physical Meaning** | **Standard Form** |
|---|---|---|
| **General Continuity (Differential)** | Conservation of mass at a point | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ |
| **Incompressible Continuity** | Mass conservation for $\rho = \text{const}$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ |
| **Steady-State Continuity** | No time dependence | $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ |
| **1-D Pipe Flow** | Mass balance across a pipe | $\dfrac{d(\rho u A)}{dz} = 0$ |
| **Integral Form** | Macroscopic balance on a CV | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\iiint_V \rho\, dV = -\oiint_S \rho \vec{v}\cdot \hat{n}\, dS$ |
| **For Reacting System** | Includes generation term | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho \vec{v}) = \sum_j \nu_j M_j r_j$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Mastery Check (KTU 2024 marks often lost here):**
> - $\rho \vec{v}$ = **mass flux** with units of **kg/(m²·s)**.
> - $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$ has units of **kg/(m³·s)**.
> - $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ has units of **kg/(m³·s)**.
> - *Every term in the equation MUST have identical units — verify this on every KTU problem.*

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Reactor Design:** Solves for residence time distributions and concentration profiles.
- **Polymer Processing:** Models extruder flow with continuity of melt.
- **Distillation Columns:** Tracks vapor/liquid flow rates between stages.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** The continuity equation is one of the **Navier–Stokes equations** solved numerically in software like ANSYS Fluent and COMSOL.
- **Pipe Network Analysis:** Bernoulli's equation derivation starts from the continuity equation.
- **Process Simulation Tools:** Aspen Plus, HYSYS, and DWSIM all enforce the continuity equation as a constraint on every unit operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Integral Form of the Continuity Equation (from First Principles)

**Goal:** Derive the macroscopic mass balance over a control volume.

**Step 1 — Define the control volume:**
Consider an arbitrary, fixed region of space $V$ bounded by a closed surface $S$. The outward normal at any point on $S$ is $\hat{n}$.

**Step 2 — Express the total mass inside the volume:**
The mass $M$ contained within $V$ at time $t$ is the integral of density over volume:

$$
M(t) = \iiint_{V} \rho(x,y,z,t) \, dV
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to time (Lagrangian to Eulerian):**
Since the control volume is fixed in space, the time derivative passes *inside* the integral (Reynolds Transport Theorem — fixed CV case):

$$
\frac{dM}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \iiint_{V} \rho \, dV = \iiint_{V} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV
$$

**Step 4 — Express the net mass leaving through the surface:**
The mass flow rate across an elemental area $dS$ with outward normal $\hat{n}$ is $\rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS$. Summing over the entire surface:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{out}} = \oiint_{S} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS
$$

**Step 5 — Apply conservation of mass:**

> *Rate of accumulation = Net rate of mass inflow + Net rate of generation*

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \iiint_{V} \rho \, dV = -\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS + \iiint_{V} R \, dV
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Divergence Theorem:**
The Divergence Theorem converts the closed surface integral into a volume integral:

$$
\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS = \iiint_{V} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV
$$

**Step 7 — Combine the volume integrals:**

$$
\iiint_{V} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) - R \right] dV = 0
$$

**Step 8 — Argue by arbitrariness of $V$:**
Since the integrand must vanish for *any* arbitrary control volume $V$, the bracketed quantity itself must equal zero at every point:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = R}
$$

This is the **general differential form of the continuity equation** — the heart of mass conservation in continuum mechanics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: One-Dimensional Continuity for Flow in a Pipe

**Goal:** Derive the 1-D continuity for a variable-area pipe.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate at a cross-section:**
At axial position $z$, the mass flow rate is:

$$
\dot{m}(z) = \rho(z) \cdot v(z) \cdot A(z)
$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance on a differential slice:**
Consider a thin slice of pipe between $z$ and $z + dz$, with cross-sectional area $A(z)$.

The mass inside the slice is:

$$
dm = \rho A \, dz
$$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation:**

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho A)}{\partial t} dz = \dot{m}(z) - \dot{m}(z+dz)
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side using a Taylor series:**

$$
\dot{m}(z+dz) = \dot{m}(z) + \frac{\partial \dot{m}}{\partial z} dz + \mathcal{O}(dz^2)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho A)}{\partial t} dz = -\frac{\partial \dot{m}}{\partial z} dz
$$

**Step 6 — Final 1-D continuity equation:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial (\rho A)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v A)}{\partial z} = 0}
$$

For **incompressible** steady flow with constant $\rho$:

$$
\boxed{v_1 A_1 = v_2 A_2}
$$

This is the familiar **principle of continuity** taught in fluid mechanics — flow speed increases as cross-section decreases.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Continuity in Cylindrical Coordinates

**Step 1 — Start from the general vector form:**

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the divergence operator in cylindrical coordinates** $(r, \theta, z)$:

$$
\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z}
$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the final equation:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0}
$$

For an axisymmetric flow (no $\theta$ dependence) with only axial velocity $v_z$:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases Summary

| **Special Case** | **Simplified Equation** | **Engineering Scenario** |
|---|---|---|
| Incompressible, 3-D | $\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$ | Water flow in open channels |
| Steady, compressible | $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ | Gas flow in nozzles |
| Steady, incompressible, 1-D | $v_1 A_1 = v_2 A_2$ | Venturi meters |
| Compressible, unsteady, 1-D | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial z} = 0$ | Shock wave analysis |
| Spherical coordinates (radial) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial (r^2 \rho v_r)}{\partial r} = 0$ | Droplet evaporation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Concept Map of Continuity Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conservation of Mass Principle] --> B[Define Control Volume V with Surface S]
    B --> C[Mass inside CV: integral of rho dV]
    C --> D[Time derivative gives accumulation rate]
    B --> E[Net mass flux across S: integral of rho v dot n dS]
    E --> F[Apply Divergence Theorem Gauss Theorem]
    D --> G[Combine volume integrals into single integrand]
    F --> G
    G --> H[General Differential Continuity Equation]
    H --> H1[Rectangular Coordinates xyz form]
    H --> H2[Cylindrical Coordinates r theta z form]
    H --> H3[Spherical Coordinates r theta phi form]
    H1 --> I1[Incompressible: div v equals 0]
    H1 --> I2[Steady State: div rho v equals 0]
    H1 --> I3[1D Pipe: v1 A1 equals v2 A2]
    H2 --> J1[Reactor Cylindrical Vessel Models]
    H3 --> J2[Droplet and Bubble Dynamics]
    I1 --> K[Solved with Navier Stokes for CFD]
    I2 --> L[Process Simulation Aspen HYSYS]
    I3 --> M[Pipe Network Design]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Macroscopic Mass Balance on a Control Volume

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CV[Control Volume V with Surface S]
        direction LR
        IN[Inlet Stream m_dot_in, rho_in, v_in] --> X[Internal Accumulation and Reaction]
        X --> OUT[Outlet Stream m_dot_out, rho_out, v_out]
        REACT[Generation or Consumption term R] --> X
    end
    X -- Accumulation Rate --> ACC[dM over dt]
    X -- Net Outflow --> NET[m_dot_out minus m_dot_in]
    ACC --> EQ[Continuity Equation Statement]
    NET --> EQ
    REACT --> EQ
    EQ --> RESULT[Governing PDE for Process Simulation]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Role of Continuity in Process Modelling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Physical Process Unit Reactor Pipe Distillation Column] --> CV[Identify Control Volume]
    CV --> M1[Apply Mass Conservation Principle]
    M1 --> M2[Derive Integral Continuity Equation]
    M2 --> M3[Apply Divergence Theorem]
    M3 --> M4[Obtain Differential Continuity PDE]
    M4 --> M5[Apply Initial and Boundary Conditions]
    M5 --> M6[Solve Analytical or Numerical Method]
    M6 --> M7[Velocity Field Density Profile]
    M7 --> M8[Feed into Momentum and Energy Equations]
    M8 --> OUT[Complete Process Model]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State the general differential form of the continuity equation for a single-component fluid. Write down its simplified form for an incompressible fluid.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The general differential form of the continuity equation is:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = R
$$

For a non-reacting system ($R = 0$):

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho = \text{constant}$), this reduces to:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the physical significance of the divergence term $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$ appearing in the continuity equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The term $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$ is called the **mass flux divergence**. Its physical significance is as follows:

- It represents the **net rate of mass efflux per unit volume** from an infinitesimal fluid element.
- If $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) > 0$ at a point, it indicates that **more mass is leaving than entering** that region — the region acts as a mass source point in the outflow sense, and density will decrease over time unless replenished.
- If $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) < 0$, mass is **converging into** the region — the region acts as a sink, and density increases.
- The continuity equation states that the time rate of change of density at a point exactly balances this net mass efflux.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Starting from first principles, derive the **integral form** of the continuity equation for a control volume with mass generation. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the divergence theorem, convert the integral form into the **differential form**. Hence write down the continuity equation in **rectangular** and **cylindrical** coordinates. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of the Integral Form (7 marks):**

Consider a fixed control volume $V$ bounded by a closed surface $S$ with outward unit normal $\hat{n}$.

**Step 1:** The mass contained inside the CV at time $t$ is:

$$
M(t) = \iiint_{V} \rho \, dV
$$

**Step 2:** Rate of accumulation of mass within the CV:

$$
\frac{dM}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \iiint_{V} \rho \, dV
$$

**Step 3:** Net mass efflux through the control surface $S$:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{out,net}} = \oiint_{S} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS
$$

**Step 4:** Net rate of mass generation within $V$ due to chemical reaction:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{gen}} = \iiint_{V} R \, dV
$$

**Step 5:** Apply conservation of mass (accumulation = inflow − outflow + generation):

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \iiint_{V} \rho \, dV = -\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS + \iiint_{V} R \, dV
$$

**Assumptions Made:**
1. The control volume is fixed in space (Eulerian description).
2. The fluid behaves as a continuum.
3. The outward normal convention is used (negative sign for outflow).
4. Mass generation is treated as a volumetric source term.

**(b) Conversion to Differential Form (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Since $V$ is fixed, the time derivative can enter the integral:

$$
\iiint_{V} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV = -\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS + \iiint_{V} R \, dV
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to the surface integral:

$$
\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS = \iiint_{V} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV
$$

**Step 3:** Combining:

$$
\iiint_{V} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) - R \right] dV = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Since this holds for *any arbitrary* control volume, the integrand must vanish pointwise:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = R
$$

**Rectangular Coordinates** $(x, y, z)$:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = R
$$

**Cylindrical Coordinates** $(r, \theta, z)$:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = R
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **one-dimensional continuity equation** for flow in a variable-area pipe. Show that for incompressible, steady flow, the relation $v_1 A_1 = v_2 A_2$ holds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An incompressible liquid flows through a pipe that narrows from a diameter of $10$ cm at section 1 to a diameter of $5$ cm at section 2. If the velocity at section 1 is $2$ m/s, calculate: (i) the velocity at section 2, and (ii) the volumetric flow rate in L/s. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of 1-D Continuity (7 marks):**

Consider a pipe with cross-sectional area $A(z)$ varying along the flow direction $z$. Define a differential slice of length $dz$ between positions $z$ and $z + dz$.

**Step 1:** Mass contained in the differential element:

$$
dm = \rho A \, dz
$$

**Step 2:** Mass flow rate at any cross-section:

$$
\dot{m}(z) = \rho v A
$$

**Step 3:** Mass balance on the element (no source/sink):

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho A \, dz)}{\partial t} = \dot{m}(z) - \dot{m}(z+dz)
$$

**Step 4:** Taylor expand the outflow term:

$$
\dot{m}(z+dz) \approx \dot{m}(z) + \frac{\partial \dot{m}}{\partial z} dz
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho A)}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial \dot{m}}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial (\rho v A)}{\partial z}
$$

**Step 6 — Final 1-D continuity equation:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial (\rho A)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v A)}{\partial z} = 0}
$$

**For incompressible** ($\rho = \text{const}$) **and steady** ($\partial/\partial t = 0$) flow:

$$
\frac{\partial (v A)}{\partial z} = 0 \implies v A = \text{constant}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{v_1 A_1 = v_2 A_2}
$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = 10$ cm = $0.10$ m
- $D_2 = 5$ cm = $0.05$ m
- $v_1 = 2$ m/s
- Incompressible liquid

**Part (i): Velocity at section 2**

Areas:

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Applying continuity: $v_1 A_1 = v_2 A_2$:

$$
v_2 = v_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 2 \cdot \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Part (ii): Volumetric flow rate**

$$
Q = v_1 A_1 = 2 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Converting to L/s (1 m³ = 1000 L):

$$
Q = 1.5708 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000 = 15.708 \text{ L/s} \approx 15.71 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $v_2 = 8$ m/s, $Q = 15.71$ L/s.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Missing the negative sign** in the mass efflux term — students often write $+\oiint \rho \vec{v}\cdot\hat{n}\,dS$ instead of $-\oiint \rho \vec{v}\cdot\hat{n}\,dS$. The negative sign is **critical** because $\hat{n}$ is the *outward* normal, and we want net *inflow* on the right-hand side.
> 2. **Forgetting to invoke the Divergence Theorem** during derivation — this step is a **mandatory checkpoint** in KTU's 14-mark answer keys.
> 3. **Skipping the step that argues "arbitrariness of $V$"** to drop the integral and obtain the differential form. This logical transition is worth **at least 1 mark** in KTU 2024 valuation schemes.
> 4. **In cylindrical coordinates, students often forget the $\frac{1}{r}$ and $\frac{1}{r^2}$ Jacobian terms** in front of the partial derivatives — this is a very common error.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** in numerical problems — failing to convert cm to m. KTU examiners actively deduct for this.
> 6. **Assuming incompressibility without stating it** — when applying $\nabla\cdot \vec{v} = 0$, explicitly mention "since the fluid is incompressible ($\rho = \text{constant}$)".

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Continuity Equation**

- ✅ The **continuity equation** is the mathematical statement of the **conservation of mass** for a flowing fluid.
- ✅ **Integral form** is applied to a control volume; **differential form** is applied at a fluid point.
- ✅ The **negative sign** in front of the surface integral is due to the **outward normal convention**.
- ✅ The **Divergence Theorem (Gauss's Theorem)** is the bridge that converts integral to differential form.
- ✅ For an **incompressible fluid**, the continuity equation reduces to the simple elegant form: $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$.
- ✅ For a **1-D, incompressible, steady flow**: $v_1 A_1 = v_2 A_2$ — the foundation of Venturi meters and orifice plates.
- ✅ In **cylindrical coordinates**, do not forget the **$\frac{1}{r}$ factor** in the radial divergence term and the **$\frac{1}{r}$ factor** in the $\theta$-divergence term.
- ✅ The continuity equation is the **first equation solved in CFD codes** (alongside Navier–Stokes).
- ✅ In **process simulation software** (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, DWSIM), the continuity equation is the **default constraint** imposed on every unit operation block.
- ✅ When a chemical reaction is present, **add the volumetric generation term $R$** to the right-hand side.
- ✅ Always **verify dimensional homogeneity** of every term: each term in the continuity equation must have units of **kg/(m³·s)**.
- ✅ The continuity equation is a **first-order PDE** in time — it requires **one initial condition** plus appropriate **boundary conditions** to obtain a unique solution.
- ✅ Common application: residence time $\tau = V/Q$ comes directly from the **steady-state integral continuity equation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equation of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — EQUATION OF MOTION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Equation of Motion (EOM)**: A mathematical statement expressing **Newton's Second Law of Motion** applied to a control mass (system) or control volume, equating the **time rate of change of momentum** to the **sum of all external forces** acting on the system, including body forces (gravity), surface forces (pressure, viscous stress), and applied forces.

Mathematically, the most general scalar-vector form is:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} \;=\; \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(m\vec{v}\right)
$$

For a **constant-mass system** this reduces to the familiar form $F = ma$. For a **control volume (open system)**, the Reynolds Transport Theorem transforms the law into:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} \;=\; \frac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\rho\vec{v}\,dV \;+\; \int_{CS}\!\rho\vec{v}\,(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — PEPOT527 / Module 1**: The Equation of Motion is the *cornerstone* of all transport processes. The same momentum-balance framework, when extended to **mass balance → continuity equation** and **energy balance → energy equation**, becomes the foundation of chemical engineering unit operations, reactor design, and CFD simulation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Tug-of-War on a Particle" Analogy**
> Imagine a tiny fluid "blob" (a particle) being pulled in multiple directions simultaneously:
> - **Gravity** pulls it **down** (like a heavy friend tugging the rope).
> - **Pressure** from the surrounding fluid pushes it (the crowd around the rope pushing sideways).
> - **Viscous friction** drags it backward (the sandpaper on the rope).
> - **Inertia** makes it resist changes in motion (the rope's own mass).
>
> The **Equation of Motion** is simply the "scorecard" that says: *the total pulling force on the blob equals how fast its momentum is changing*. The blob accelerates, decelerates, or stays steady depending on which forces "win" at that instant.

This is precisely how engineers model everything from a sand grain settling in water to fuel injection in a rocket nozzle.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | Standard Earth value used in KTU problems |
| $\rho$ | Fluid density | **kg/m³** | Water at 25 °C ≈ 997 kg/m³ |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity | **Pa·s** | Water at 25 °C ≈ 8.9 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity | **m²/s** | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ |
| $C_D$ | Drag coefficient | dimensionless | Function of $Re_p$ |
| $Re_p$ | Particle Reynolds number | dimensionless | $Re_p = \rho v d_p / \mu$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Levels of the Equation of Motion

A KTU examiner expects you to recognize that the EOM is *not* one equation but a **hierarchy**:

### Level 1 — Point / Particle Form (Newton's Original)

$$
\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = m\,\frac{d\vec{v}}{dt}
$$

Valid for a **lump / discrete particle** of constant mass. This is the starting point for problems involving a **single falling sphere**, droplet, or bubble.

### Level 2 — System Integral Form (Reynolds Transport Theorem applied to momentum)

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\rho\vec{v}\,dV + \int_{CS}\!\rho\vec{v}\,(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this level matters**: In Process Modelling, you often deal with fluids *flowing through* pipes, reactors, and pumps — the boundaries are **not** fixed to mass. The CS integral captures the **net efflux of momentum** carried by the moving fluid.

### Level 3 — Differential Field Form (Cauchy / Navier-Stokes)

For a **Newtonian fluid** with constant $\rho$ and $\mu$:

$$
\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}\right) = -\nabla P + \mu\,\nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g}
$$

This is the incompressible **Navier–Stokes equation** — the most applied form in CFD and process simulation.

## 2.2 The Four Forces Acting on a Fluid Element

| # | Force | Symbol | Origin | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Body force (gravity) | $\rho\vec{g}\,dV$ | Long-range, acts on volume | Downward (typically) |
| 2 | Pressure force | $-\nabla P\,dV$ | Normal surface stress | From high → low $P$ |
| 3 | Viscous force | $\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}\,dV$ | Tangential surface stress | Opposes motion |
| 4 | Inertial term | $\rho\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}\,dV$ | Newton's 2nd law | Direction of acceleration |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Term**
> - **Inertia** appears because mass resists change in motion (Newton's 1st law).
> - **Pressure** arises because molecules in a high-$P$ region collide more frequently with the element.
> - **Viscosity** appears because neighboring fluid layers "drag" the element when there is a velocity gradient.
> - **Gravity** is the only long-range force; it is treated as a *body* force (per unit volume), not a surface force.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Domain / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's 2nd Law (lump) | $F = m\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | Single particle, constant mass |
| 2 | Momentum balance (CV) | $\sum F = \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\rho\vec{v}\,dV + \int_{CS}\!\rho\vec{v}(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA$ | Open systems, reactors, pipes |
| 3 | Navier–Stokes (incompressible, Newtonian) | $\rho\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t}+\vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}\right) = -\nabla P + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g}$ | Fluid flow simulation (CFD) |
| 4 | Falling sphere — Gravity | $F_g = m\,g = \rho_p\,V_p\,g$ | Always present |
| 5 | Falling sphere — Buoyancy | $F_b = \rho_f\,V_p\,g$ | Fluid displaced by particle |
| 6 | Falling sphere — Drag (Stokes) | $F_d = 3\pi\mu d_p\,v$ | $Re_p < 0.1$ (creeping flow) |
| 7 | Falling sphere — Drag (Newton) | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}C_D\,\rho_f\,A_p\,v^{2}$ | $Re_p > 1000$ |
| 8 | Particle Reynolds Number | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_f\,v\,d_p}{\mu}$ | Characterizes flow regime |
| 9 | Terminal velocity (Stokes) | $v_t = \dfrac{(\rho_p-\rho_f)\,g\,d_p^{2}}{18\mu}$ | $Re_p < 0.1$ |
| 10 | Terminal velocity (Newton) | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(\rho_p-\rho_f)\,g\,d_p}{C_D\,\rho_f}}$ | $Re_p > 1000$ |
| 11 | Material derivative | $\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}$ | Converts Eulerian to Lagrangian rate |
| 12 | Mass conservation (continuity) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{v}) = 0$ | Coupled with EOM in any flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip**: Most KTU Module-1 problems on the Equation of Motion are *falling-particle* type — the **Lump form** (Level 1) is tested in Part A, while the **CV form** (Level 2) is tested in Part B. Memorize the *force balance structure*, not just the final answer.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Chemical Process Industry**: Designing **settlers, cyclones, and classifiers** — particle terminal velocity is the governing design parameter.
- **CFD Simulation**: The Navier–Stokes equation is the workhorse of **ANSYS Fluent, COMSOL, OpenFOAM**.
- **Reactor Engineering**: Mixing-tank impeller design uses momentum balance to compute **power number $N_p$**.
- **Piping & Pumps**: Pump-head calculations use the momentum integral form to compute **thrust and pressure drop**.
- **Environmental Engineering**: Modelling **particulate dispersion** in air and water (pollution control).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — General Equation of Motion for a Fluid Element (Cauchy's Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal**: Start from Newton's 2nd law applied to a *differential fluid element* $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$ and arrive at the differential (Cauchy) equation of motion.

### Step 1 — Define the Fluid Element

Consider a small rectangular fluid element of dimensions $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ fixed in space, with centre at point $(x,y,z)$. The mass of the element is $dm = \rho\,dV = \rho\,dx\,dy\,dz$.

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law (Vector Form)

The total force on the element equals mass × acceleration:

$$
\sum \vec{F} = (\rho\,dV)\,\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Three Force Contributions

**Force (a) — Body force (gravity):**

$$
\vec{F}_{\text{body}} = \rho\,\vec{g}\,dV
$$

**Force (b) — Surface (pressure) force:** Pressure $P$ acts on each of the six faces. The *net* force in the $x$-direction is obtained by Taylor expansion. Consider the face at $x + \tfrac{dx}{2}$ where the pressure is:

$$
P\!\left(x+\tfrac{dx}{2}\right) = P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\cdot\frac{dx}{2}
$$

The face at $x - \tfrac{dx}{2}$ has pressure:

$$
P\!\left(x-\tfrac{dx}{2}\right) = P - \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\cdot\frac{dx}{2}
$$

Net pressure force in $x$:

$$
F_{P,x} = \left[P - \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\tfrac{dx}{2}\right]dydz - \left[P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\tfrac{dx}{2}\right]dydz
$$

Simplifying:

$$
F_{P,x} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\,dV
$$

Combined vectorially for all three directions:

$$
\vec{F}_{\text{pressure}} = -\nabla P\,dV
$$

**Force (c) — Viscous (shear) force:** For a Newtonian fluid, the viscous stress tensor $\tau_{ij}$ produces a net force:

$$
\vec{F}_{\text{visc}} = (\nabla\cdot\boldsymbol{\tau})\,dV
$$

For constant viscosity and incompressible flow, this simplifies to $\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}\,dV$.

### Step 4 — Assemble the Force Balance

Adding the three contributions:

$$
\rho\,\vec{g}\,dV \;-\; \nabla P\,dV \;+\; \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}\,dV \;=\; \rho\,dV\,\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}
$$

### Step 5 — Cancel the Common Volume Element

Dividing both sides by $dV$:

$$
\rho\,\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} \;=\; -\nabla P \;+\; \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} \;+\; \rho\,\vec{g}
$$

Expanding the material derivative:

$$
\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}\right) = -\nabla P + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\,\vec{g}
$$

✅ **This is the Navier–Stokes equation — the workhorse of process simulation.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Equation of Motion for a Single Falling Sphere (Terminal Velocity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-tested KTU problem** in Module 1. Master this derivation — both Stokes and Newton regimes.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram & Force Identification

A solid sphere of density $\rho_p$ and diameter $d_p$ falls under gravity through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$. The three forces acting on it are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. Gravity (down):} \quad & F_g = m_p\,g = \rho_p \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{6}d_p^{3} \cdot g \\[4pt]
\text{2. Buoyancy (up):} \quad & F_b = \rho_f \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{6}d_p^{3} \cdot g \\[4pt]
\text{3. Drag (opposes motion):} \quad & F_d = 3\pi\mu d_p\,v \quad \text{(Stokes regime)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law (1-D Vertical Direction, Down Positive)

$$
m_p\,\frac{dv}{dt} = F_g - F_b - F_d
$$

Substituting:

$$
\rho_p\cdot\frac{\pi}{6}d_p^{3}\cdot\frac{dv}{dt} = \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right)\frac{\pi}{6}d_p^{3}\,g - 3\pi\mu d_p\,v
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Terminal Velocity Condition

At terminal velocity, the particle no longer accelerates, so $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$ and $v = v_t$:

$$
0 = \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right)\frac{\pi}{6}d_p^{3}\,g - 3\pi\mu d_p\,v_t
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Terminal Velocity

$$
3\pi\mu d_p\,v_t = \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right)\frac{\pi}{6}d_p^{3}\,g
$$

$$
v_t = \frac{\left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right)\,d_p^{2}\,g}{18\mu}
$$

✅ **Stokes' Law for Terminal Velocity — derived.**

### Step 5 — Generalize to Newton Regime (Higher Re)

For $Re_p > 1000$, the drag force follows:

$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}C_D\,\rho_f\,A_p\,v^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}C_D\,\rho_f\cdot\frac{\pi d_p^{2}}{4}\cdot v^{2}
$$

Setting $F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$ and solving for $v_t$:

$$
v_t = \sqrt{\frac{2\left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right)\,g\,d_p}{C_D\,\rho_f}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Reynolds Transport Theorem Applied to Momentum

> [!NOTE]
> This bridges Newton's law (system) to the control-volume (open system) form — a high-yield KTU concept.

### Step 1 — Start with Newton's Law on a System

For a *system* (fixed mass $m$) at time $t$:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \frac{d}{dt}(m\vec{v}) = \frac{d(m\vec{v})_{\text{sys}}}{dt}
$$

### Step 2 — Recognise the System is Moving with the Fluid

At time $t + dt$, the system has *moved* with the flow. The original control volume $CV$ now contains *different* material. We cannot simply differentiate inside $CV$ — that gives the wrong (Eulerian) rate.

### Step 3 — Apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)

The RTT states that for any extensive property $B$:

$$
\frac{dB_{\text{sys}}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\beta\,\rho\,dV + \int_{CS}\!\beta\,\rho\,(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA
$$

where $\beta = B/m$. For momentum, $B = m\vec{v}$, so $\beta = \vec{v}$ and $\beta\rho = \rho\vec{v}$.

### Step 4 — Substitute into Newton's Law

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\rho\vec{v}\,dV + \int_{CS}\!\rho\vec{v}\,(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA
$$

✅ **General integral form of the equation of motion for an open system.**

### Step 5 — Interpretation of the Two Terms

- **Term 1** $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\rho\vec{v}\,dV$ — *unsteady term*: rate of accumulation of momentum inside $CV$.
- **Term 2** $\int_{CS}\!\rho\vec{v}(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA$ — *convective term*: net efflux of momentum across the control surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **steady flow**, Term 1 = 0 and the equation collapses to $\sum F = $ (momentum efflux − momentum influx). This is what you will use for nozzles, diffusers, and pumps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Derivation Pipeline of the Equation of Motion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Newton's Second Law F equals m dv dt"] --> B["Choose Reference Frame"]
    B --> B1["Lagrangian Lump System"]
    B --> B2["Eulerian Control Volume"]
    B1 --> C1["Apply to Discrete Particle"]
    C1 --> D1["Identify Forces Gravity Buoyancy Drag"]
    D1 --> E1["F net equals m_p dv dt"]
    E1 --> F1["Integrate to Get v t and Position x t"]
    B2 --> C2["Define Control Volume CV and Control Surface CS"]
    C2 --> D2["Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem to Momentum"]
    D2 --> E2["Integral Form Sum F equals d dt integral rho v dV plus integral rho v v dot n dA"]
    E2 --> F2["Take Differential Limit dV to zero"]
    F2 --> G2["Substitute Newtonian Constitutive Relation tau equals mu grad v"]
    G2 --> H2["Differential Navier Stokes Equation"]
    H2 --> I2["Apply Boundary Conditions no slip at wall free surface at open surface"]
    I2 --> J2["Solve Analytically or Numerically CFD"]
    F1 --> K1["Engineering Outputs Terminal Velocity Settling Time"]
    J2 --> L2["Engineering Outputs Velocity Profile Pressure Drop Drag"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map: Forces and Flow Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Equation of Motion F net equals m dv dt"] --> B["Four Force Categories"]
    B --> B1["Body Force rho g V"]
    B --> B2["Pressure Force minus grad P V"]
    B --> B3["Viscous Force mu nabla squared v V"]
    B --> B4["Inertial Term rho Dv Dt V"]
    A --> C["Two Solution Regimes"]
    C --> C1["Stokes Regime Re p less than 0.1"]
    C --> C2["Newton Regime Re p greater than 1000"]
    C --> C3["Intermediate 0.1 to 1000 Use Empirical C D vs Re p"]
    C1 --> D1["v t equals rho p minus rho f g d p squared over 18 mu"]
    C2 --> D2["v t equals sqrt 2 rho p minus rho f g d p over C D rho f"]
    C3 --> D3["Iterate Assume Re compute v t check Re repeat until convergence"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Free-Body Diagram of a Falling Sphere (Text-Based)

```
                     ↑  Buoyancy  F_b = ρ_f · (π/6)d_p³ · g
                     │
                     │
                  ╭──┴──╮
                  │     │   ← Fluid of viscosity μ
                  │  ●  │      density ρ_f
                  │     │      Velocity v (downward)
                  ╰──┬──╯
                     │
                     ↓  Gravity  F_g = ρ_p · (π/6)d_p³ · g
                     ↓
                     ↓  Drag      F_d = 3πμd_p·v  (Stokes)
                     ↓
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The sphere (●) experiences **two body/surface forces** (gravity down, buoyancy up) and **one velocity-dependent resistive force** (drag up, opposing motion). At terminal velocity, the *vector sum* of these three forces is **zero**, which is precisely the equation of motion at steady state.


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State Newton's Second Law of Motion. Write the general form of the equation of motion for a control volume in terms of momentum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Newton's Second Law of Motion states that the net external force acting on a body equals the time rate of change of its linear momentum:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \frac{d(m\vec{v})}{dt}
$$

For an open system (control volume $CV$ with control surface $CS$), the Reynolds Transport Theorem applied to momentum gives the integral form:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\rho\vec{v}\,dV + \int_{CS}\!\rho\vec{v}\,(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA
$$

The **first term** represents the rate of accumulation of momentum inside the CV (unsteady term), and the **second term** represents the net efflux of momentum across the CS (convective term). **[3 marks: 1 for statement + 1 for CV form + 1 for term interpretation]**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the material derivative. Why is it important in the equation of motion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The material derivative (also called the substantial derivative or Lagrangian derivative) gives the time rate of change of a property as experienced by a fluid particle moving through space:

$$
\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}
$$

It is crucial because the equation of motion is a statement about the **acceleration of a fluid particle**, not about the rate of change at a fixed point in space. The local derivative $\partial \vec{v}/\partial t$ gives the rate at a fixed Eulerian point, while the convective term $\vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}$ accounts for the particle's motion into regions of different velocity. Together, they yield the *true* acceleration appearing on the left-hand side of the Navier–Stokes equation. **[3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for the two terms + 1 for significance]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme)**: Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (typically a derivation and a numerical application). Students answer **either Option A or Option B**.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Falling Sphere in Stokes Regime

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Derive the equation of motion for a solid sphere falling under gravity through a viscous Newtonian fluid under creeping-flow conditions ($Re_p < 0.1$). State clearly the assumptions made. **[7 marks]**

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Free-Body Diagram Forces:**

A sphere of density $\rho_p$, diameter $d_p$, falling at velocity $v$ through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$ experiences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gravity:} \quad & F_g = m_p\,g = \rho_p\cdot\frac{\pi}{6}d_p^{3}\cdot g \\[4pt]
\text{Buoyancy:} \quad & F_b = \rho_f\cdot\frac{\pi}{6}d_p^{3}\cdot g \\[4pt]
\text{Drag (Stokes):} \quad & F_d = 3\pi\mu d_p\,v
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumptions Made:**
1. Sphere is rigid and spherical.
2. Fluid is Newtonian and incompressible.
3. Flow is in the **creeping regime** ($Re_p < 0.1$), so inertial terms in the Navier–Stokes equation are negligible and **Stokes' law is valid**.
4. The sphere is far from walls and other particles (no wall/interference effects).
5. Motion is one-dimensional (purely vertical, no horizontal components).
6. No other external forces (electric, magnetic) act on the sphere.

**Newton's Second Law (downward positive):**

$$
m_p\,\frac{dv}{dt} = F_g - F_b - F_d
$$

Substituting and dividing both sides by the particle mass $m_p = \rho_p\cdot\frac{\pi}{6}d_p^{3}$:

$$
\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}{\rho_p} - \frac{18\mu}{\rho_p d_p^{2}}\,v
$$

Define the **characteristic settling time constant**:

$$
\tau_p = \frac{\rho_p d_p^{2}}{18\mu}
$$

Then the equation reduces to the **first-order linear ODE**:

$$
\frac{dv}{dt} + \frac{v}{\tau_p} = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}{\rho_p}
$$

**Terminal Velocity (steady state, $dv/dt = 0$):**

$$
v_t = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g\,d_p^{2}}{18\mu}
$$

This is **Stokes' Law** for terminal settling velocity. **[7 marks: 1 diagram + 2 assumptions + 2 force balance + 1 substitution + 1 final answer]**

---

> **(b)** A glass sphere of density $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³ and diameter $d_p = 100\,\mu\text{m} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m is allowed to settle in air at 25 °C ($\rho_f = 1.184$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s). Determine:
> 1. The terminal velocity $v_t$ (assuming Stokes' law is valid).
> 2. The particle Reynolds number $Re_p$ at $v_t$.
> 3. Comment on whether Stokes' law is **justified**. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, $\rho_f = 1.184$ kg/m³
- $d_p = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s

**1. Terminal Velocity (Stokes' Law):**

$$
v_t = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g\,d_p^{2}}{18\mu}
$$

$$
v_t = \frac{(2500 - 1.184)(9.81)(1.0 \times 10^{-4})^{2}}{18 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

Numerator: $(2498.816)(9.81)(1.0 \times 10^{-8}) = 2.4513 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m

Denominator: $3.33 \times 10^{-4}$ N·s/m

$$
v_t = \frac{2.4513 \times 10^{-4}}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.736\,\text{m/s}
$$

**2. Particle Reynolds Number:**

$$
Re_p = \frac{\rho_f\,v_t\,d_p}{\mu} = \frac{(1.184)(0.736)(1.0 \times 10^{-4})}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
Re_p = \frac{8.714 \times 10^{-5}}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 4.71
$$

**3. Justification of Stokes' Law:**

Stokes' law is valid only for $Re_p < 0.1$. The calculated $Re_p \approx 4.71$ is **~47 times higher** than the upper limit. Therefore, **Stokes' law is NOT justified** for this case. The problem should be re-solved using the **intermediate regime** with empirical $C_D$–$Re_p$ correlations (e.g., Schiller–Naumann: $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p}(1 + 0.15\,Re_p^{0.687})$). **[7 marks: 2 for $v_t$ + 2 for $Re_p$ + 1 for comparison + 1 for comment + 1 for remedy]**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Momentum Balance on a Control Volume (Rocket / Pipe)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Starting from Newton's second law, apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem to derive the **integral form of the equation of motion** for a control volume. Clearly explain the physical meaning of each term. **[7 marks]**

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Newton's 2nd Law on a system of fixed mass:**

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \frac{d(m\vec{v})_{\text{sys}}}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — The system moves with the fluid.** At time $t$ the system coincides with a chosen control volume $CV$; at $t + dt$, it has moved with the flow and *no longer* coincides with $CV$. So we cannot simply differentiate the integral over $CV$.

**Step 3 — Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT):** For any extensive property $B$ with intensive value $\beta = B/m$:

$$
\frac{dB_{\text{sys}}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\beta\,\rho\,dV + \int_{CS}\!\beta\,\rho\,(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA
$$

**Step 4 — For momentum**, $B = m\vec{v}$ and $\beta\rho = \rho\vec{v}$:

$$
\boxed{\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\rho\vec{v}\,dV + \int_{CS}\!\rho\vec{v}\,(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA}
$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}}$: Sum of all surface forces (pressure, viscous) and body forces (gravity) acting on the fluid inside the CV.
- $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\rho\vec{v}\,dV$: **Unsteady term** — rate of accumulation of momentum within the CV.
- $\int_{CS}\!\rho\vec{v}(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA$: **Convective (flux) term** — net rate of momentum leaving the CV through the control surface.

For **steady flow**, the unsteady term vanishes, giving $\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \int_{CS}\rho\vec{v}(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA$. **[7 marks: 2 for Newton's law + 2 for RTT application + 2 for boxed equation + 1 for term meanings]**

---

> **(b)** Water enters a horizontal pipe of diameter 10 cm with a uniform velocity of 2 m/s and pressure 200 kPa. At the outlet (diameter 5 cm), the pressure is 150 kPa. Compute the **anchoring force** required to hold the pipe in place. Use the integral momentum equation. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Given:** Inlet: $D_1 = 0.10$ m, $V_1 = 2$ m/s, $P_1 = 200{,}000$ Pa. Outlet: $D_2 = 0.05$ m, $P_2 = 150{,}000$ Pa. $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. Horizontal pipe, gravity perpendicular to flow → no $x$-component of body force.

**Step 1 — Areas:**
$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^{2} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Outlet velocity (continuity):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$

$$
V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{(7.854 \times 10^{-3})(2)}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.0\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate:**
$$
\dot{m} = \rho A_1 V_1 = (1000)(7.854 \times 10^{-3})(2) = 15.708\,\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply steady-state momentum equation (x-direction):**
$$
\sum F_x = \int_{CS}\rho v_x(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA = \dot{m}(V_2 - V_1)
$$

Pressure forces on the fluid in the $x$-direction:
- At inlet: $+P_1 A_1$ (pressure pushes fluid forward)
- At outlet: $-P_2 A_2$ (pressure pushes fluid backward on the control surface)

The anchoring force $F_A$ is the force the support exerts on the pipe:

$$
P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2 + F_A = \dot{m}(V_2 - V_1)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerical values:**

$$
P_1 A_1 = (200{,}000)(7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 1570.8\,\text{N}
$$

$$
P_2 A_2 = (150{,}000)(1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) = 294.52\,\text{N}
$$

$$
\dot{m}(V_2 - V_1) = (15.708)(8.0 - 2.0) = 94.25\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $F_A$:**

$$
F_A = \dot{m}(V_2 - V_1) - P_1 A_1 + P_2 A_2
$$

$$
F_A = 94.25 - 1570.8 + 294.52 = -1182.0\,\text{N}
$$

The **negative sign** indicates the force acts in the $-x$ direction (opposite to flow). The magnitude of the anchoring force is **≈ 1182 N** and it must oppose the direction of flow to keep the pipe in place. **[7 marks: 1 for areas + 1 for continuity + 1 for mass flow + 2 for momentum equation setup + 2 for numerical evaluation]**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Equation-of-Motion Problems**
> 1. **Forgetting the buoyancy term** in falling-particle problems. Many students write $m\,dv/dt = mg - F_d$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong drag formula** for the regime: using Stokes' drag ($3\pi\mu d_p v$) when $Re_p > 0.1$ is a *fatal* error — full 7 marks can be lost.
> 3. **Not checking the Reynolds number** after calculating $v_t$. Examiners specifically look for a "comment on validity" line. Without it, lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Sign errors in the CV momentum equation**: pressure at outlet pushes *backward* on the fluid — many students add it instead of subtracting. Use a clear sign convention and stick to it.
> 5. **Confusing local derivative and material derivative**: in $\partial \vec{v}/\partial t + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}$, the second term is **convective acceleration**, not viscous. Misnaming it loses marks.
> 6. **Forgetting units** — the answer to a numerical problem without a unit (m/s, N) is treated as **incomplete** and penalised.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Equation of Motion (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ The **Equation of Motion** is Newton's 2nd law applied to a system or control volume.
>
> ✅ **Three forms** must be remembered:
>    - **Lump/Particle**: $F = m\dfrac{dv}{dt}$
>    - **Integral (CV)**: $\sum F = \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\int_{CV}\!\rho\vec{v}\,dV + \int_{CS}\!\rho\vec{v}(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA$
>    - **Differential (Navier–Stokes)**: $\rho\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t}+\vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}\right) = -\nabla P + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g}$
>
> ✅ The **material derivative** $\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}$ converts Eulerian field data to the *true* particle acceleration.
>
> ✅ **Stokes' Law** is valid **only for $Re_p < 0.1$**: $F_d = 3\pi\mu d_p v$.
>
> ✅ **Newton's Law** is valid **for $Re_p > 1000$**: $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}C_D \rho_f A_p v^{2}$.
>
> ✅ Terminal velocity is reached when the **net force is zero** ($dv/dt = 0$).
>
> ✅ For a **horizontal CV momentum problem** (pipes, nozzles), ignore gravity in the flow direction; include pressure forces and the anchoring force.
>
> ✅ Always **verify $Re_p$** after computing $v_t$ and state the regime explicitly.
>
> ✅ The **Reynolds Transport Theorem** is the bridge that converts a system (closed) equation into a control-volume (open) equation.
>
> ✅ The equation of motion is **coupled** with the **continuity equation** (mass conservation) — both must be solved together for any real flow problem.
>
> ✅ Standard unit check: $F$ in **N**, $v$ in **m/s**, $P$ in **Pa (N/m²)**, $\rho$ in **kg/m³**, $\mu$ in **Pa·s**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transport Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Process Modelling & Simulation (PEPOT527)**, *Transport Equations* are the fundamental **partial differential equations (PDEs)** that mathematically describe the conservation of three key physical quantities — **mass, momentum, and energy** — within a control volume of a flowing fluid or a reacting mixture. They form the mathematical skeleton of every chemical, mechanical, and process engineering simulation (CFD, Aspen, COMSOL, MATLAB PDE solvers).

The **general transport equation** (also called the *equation of change*) for any property $\phi$ is:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_{\phi}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (**kg/m³**)
- $\mathbf{v}$ = velocity vector (**m/s**)
- $\Gamma$ = diffusion coefficient (**kg/m·s** for momentum, **W/m·K** for heat, **m²/s** for mass)
- $S_{\phi}$ = volumetric source term

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to derive the **Continuity (Mass), Momentum (Navier–Stokes), Energy, and Species Continuity** equations from a shell balance and recognize that they are *all* special cases of the single general transport equation shown above.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "River Highway" Intuition

Imagine a **busy city highway** where cars constantly enter, exit, accelerate, and slow down. Now substitute "cars" with "fluid molecules", "highway" with a "control volume", and "speed" with "transport properties":

- **Cars entering & exiting** → **Convective transport** ($\nabla \cdot \rho \mathbf{v} \phi$)
- **Cars spreading into different lanes** → **Diffusive transport** ($\nabla \cdot \Gamma \nabla \phi$)
- **Cars generated or removed in accidents** → **Source/sink term** ($S_{\phi}$)
- **The traffic officer's logbook** → **Conservation equation** (inflow − outflow + generation − consumption = accumulation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The **left-hand side** of every transport equation tracks *what is being carried in by bulk flow*, while the **right-hand side** tracks *what spreads by molecular-level diffusion plus any internal sources/sinks*. This identical structure is the unifying beauty of transport phenomena.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Transport Equations

| Property Transported ($\phi$) | Conservation Law | Resulting Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Mass (1) | Continuity | Continuity Equation |
| Momentum ($\mathbf{v}$) | Newton's 2nd Law | Navier–Stokes Equation |
| Energy ($T$ or $h$) | 1st Law of Thermodynamics | Energy Equation |
| Species Mass ($C_A$) | Mass balance on A | Species Continuity |

Physical constants always appearing: **density $\rho$**, **viscosity $\mu$**, **thermal conductivity $k$**, **heat capacity $C_p$**, **binary diffusivity $D_{AB}$**, and **pressure $P$**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conservation Principle — The Universal Foundation

Every transport equation is a verbal statement converted into mathematics:

$$\text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{Rate of In (convection + diffusion)} - \text{Rate of Out} + \text{Rate of Generation}$$

In differential form for an **infinitesimal fluid element** of volume $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} dV = - \oint_{S} (\rho \phi \mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n})\,dS - \oint_{S} (\mathbf{q}_{\phi} \cdot \mathbf{n})\,dS + \int_{V} S_{\phi}\,dV$$

Applying the **Gauss Divergence Theorem** ($\oint_S \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{S} = \int_V \nabla\cdot \mathbf{F}\,dV$) on the surface integrals yields the PDE form on the right-hand side.

### 2.2 Why "Shell Balance" Is the KTU Favourite Method

The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests the **shell (or differential) balance approach**:

1. **Draw** a thin shell of thickness $dx$ in the direction of transport.
2. **Write** the flux entering the left face: $W_{\phi}\big|_{x}$.
3. **Write** the flux leaving the right face: $W_{\phi}\big|_{x+dx}$.
4. **Subtract** and divide by $dx$ as $dx \to 0$.
5. **Set** the result equal to the rate of accumulation + generation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum mastery set** required for a full 14-mark derivation in KTU ESE. Memorize the *form* (not the numbers) — the substitution principle is universal.

| # | Equation Name | Final Form (incompressible flow, constant $\rho$, $\mu$, $k$, $D_{AB}$) | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Transport Equation** | $\dfrac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho \mathbf{v} \phi) = \nabla\cdot(\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_{\phi}$ | Universal PDE template |
| 2 | **Continuity Equation** | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$ | Conservation of mass |
| 3 | **Continuity (incompressible)** | $\nabla\cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ | Mass conservation for liquids |
| 4 | **Navier–Stokes (x-momentum)** | $\rho \dfrac{D v_x}{Dt} = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^{2} v_x + \rho g_x$ | Newton's 2nd law per unit volume |
| 5 | **Energy Equation** | $\rho C_p \dfrac{D T}{Dt} = k \nabla^{2} T + \Phi + \dot{Q}$ | 1st Law of Thermodynamics |
| 6 | **Species Continuity (A)** | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(C_A \mathbf{v}) = D_{AB} \nabla^{2} C_A + R_A$ | Mass balance on species A |
| 7 | **Viscous Dissipation Function** | $\Phi = \mu \left[ 2\left(\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}\right)^{2} + 2\left(\dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y}\right)^{2} + 2\left(\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial x}+\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}\right)^{2} + \ldots \right]$ | Heat generated by viscous shear |
| 8 | **Material Derivative** | $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + v_x \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} + v_z \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}$ | Rate following a fluid particle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the *workhorses* of CFD codes like **ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, COMSOL, and STAR-CCM+**. Every combustion reactor, distillation column, heat exchanger, and bioreactor in industry is simulated by discretizing (FVM/FEM) one or more of these PDEs. Mastering them is the gateway to **R&D roles in simulation, design, and process safety**.

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions — The "Killer Step" in KTU Valuation

A PDE without BCs is incomplete. The four canonical BCs:

| Type | Form | Physical Example |
|---|---|---|
| Dirichlet | $\phi = \phi_{wall}$ | No-slip at wall: $v_x = 0$ |
| Neumann | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0$ | Insulated wall: $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial y} = 0$ |
| Robin (Convective) | $-k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n} = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Heat loss to ambient |
| Periodic | $\phi(L) = \phi(0)$ | Cyclic flow in pipes |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

**Step 1 — Choose a differential fluid element** fixed in space (Eulerian control volume) with dimensions $dx, dy, dz$.

**Step 2 — Mass flux entering** through face at $x$ (area $dy\,dz$):

$$\dot{m}_{x} = \rho v_x \big|_{x} \, dy\, dz$$

**Step 3 — Mass flux leaving** through face at $x + dx$:

$$\dot{m}_{x+dx} = \rho v_x \big|_{x+dx} \, dy\, dz$$

**Step 4 — Net x-direction outflow** using Taylor expansion ($v_x\big|_{x+dx} \approx v_x\big|_x + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} dx$):

$$\text{Net out}_x = \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} \right] dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 5 — Similarly for y and z directions** and sum the three contributions:

$$\text{Total net out} = \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} \right] dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 6 — Rate of mass accumulation** in the element:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 7 — Apply conservation** (Accumulation = In − Out, or net out = 0 since mass is not generated/destroyed in non-nuclear reactions):

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx\, dy\, dz &= -\left[ \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} \right] dx\, dy\, dz
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Cancel $dx\, dy\, dz$ and use vector notation**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0\;}
\end{aligned}$$

For **incompressible flow** ($\rho = $ constant), this reduces to $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Navier–Stokes Equation (x-Momentum)

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law** to the same fluid element:

$$\text{Mass} \times \text{Acceleration} = \sum \text{Forces}$$

$$\rho\, dx\, dy\, dz \cdot \frac{D v_x}{Dt} = \sum F_x$$

**Step 2 — Identify the four forces**:

**(a) Pressure force** — net on the two x-faces:

$$F_{P,x} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} dx\, dy\, dz$$

**(b) Viscous (shear) force** — net shear stress $\tau_{yx}$ on top and bottom faces plus $\tau_{zx}$ on front and back faces:

$$F_{\text{visc},x} = \left[ \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} \right] dx\, dy\, dz$$

**(c) Gravity force** in the x-direction:

$$F_{g,x} = \rho g_x \, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**(d) No other body forces** are considered.

**Step 3 — Substitute Newton's law of viscosity** for a Newtonian fluid:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{xx} &= -2\mu \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \tfrac{2}{3}\mu(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) \\
\tau_{yx} &= -\mu \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial x} \right) \\
\tau_{zx} &= -\mu \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial x} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Insert into viscous force** and combine (assuming constant $\mu$ and incompressible flow so $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$):

$$F_{\text{visc},x} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial z^{2}} \right) dx\, dy\, dz = \mu \nabla^{2} v_x \, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 5 — Equate and divide** by $dx\, dy\, dz$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho \frac{D v_x}{Dt} &= -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^{2} v_x + \rho g_x
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Expand the material derivative** explicitly:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\rho \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^{2} v_x + \rho g_x\;}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Many students lose marks by forgetting the **convective acceleration terms** $v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}$, etc. These nonlinear terms are what make the Navier–Stokes equations notoriously *hard to solve analytically* and is the very reason **CFD** exists!

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Equation

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics** to the fluid element:

$$\text{Rate of accumulation of internal energy} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - \dot{W}_{\text{by fluid}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{gen}}$$

**Step 2 — Accumulation term**:

$$\rho C_p \frac{D T}{Dt} dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 3 — Net heat inflow by conduction** (Fourier's law $\mathbf{q} = -k \nabla T$):

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = k \nabla^{2} T \, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 4 — Viscous dissipation** — mechanical energy converted irreversibly to heat $\Phi$:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{visc}} = \mu \Phi \, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 5 — External volumetric heat source** $\dot{Q}$ (e.g., chemical reaction, electrical heating):

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{ext}} = \dot{Q} \, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 6 — Combine and divide** by the volume:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\rho C_p \frac{D T}{Dt} = k \nabla^{2} T + \mu \Phi + \dot{Q}\;}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Species Continuity Equation for A

**Step 1 — Mass balance on species A** in the element:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx\, dy\, dz = \text{Net convective in} + \text{Net diffusive in} + R_A \, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 2 — Convective net in**:

$$- \nabla \cdot (C_A \mathbf{v}) \, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 3 — Diffusive (Fick's law) net in**:

$$- \nabla \cdot (-D_{AB} \nabla C_A) \, dx\, dy\, dz = D_{AB} \nabla^{2} C_A \, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 4 — Rate of generation** by reaction: $R_A$ (mol/m³·s, +ve for generation, −ve for consumption).

**Step 5 — Final form**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (C_A \mathbf{v}) = D_{AB} \nabla^{2} C_A + R_A\;}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.5 The Unification — All Four Equations in One Table

| Property $\phi$ | Diffusivity $\Gamma$ | Source $S_\phi$ | Resulting PDE |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Continuity |
| $v_x$ | $\mu$ | $-\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \rho g_x$ | Navier–Stokes (x) |
| $T$ | $k$ | $\mu \Phi + \dot{Q}$ | Energy |
| $C_A$ | $\rho D_{AB}$ | $R_A$ | Species continuity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This unification is the heart of the KTU Module 1 question pattern.** If you can reproduce this table from memory, the 14-mark derivations become trivial substitutions.
