## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Map of Transport Equations (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Conservation Principle] --> B[Choose phi]
    B --> C1[phi equals 1: Mass]
    B --> C2[phi equals v: Momentum]
    B --> C3[phi equals T: Energy]
    B --> C4[phi equals C_A: Species]
    C1 --> D1[Continuity Equation]
    C2 --> D2[Navier Stokes Equation]
    C3 --> D3[Energy Equation]
    C4 --> D4[Species Equation]
    D1 --> E[General Transport PDE]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    F --> G[Discretization FVM or FEM]
    G --> H[CFD Simulation in ANSYS or COMSOL]
    H --> I[Industrial Design Decision]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Equation Derivation

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[Step 1: Draw Shell] --> step2[Step 2: Identify Fluxes]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Apply Conservation]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Apply Constitutive Law]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Expand Material Derivative]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Simplify and Write Final PDE]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: State Boundary Conditions]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Verify with Limiting Cases]
```

### 4.3 Inter-Coupling of the Four Transport Phenomena

```mermaid
graph TD
    NSE[Navier Stokes Momentum] -->|velocity field| ENE[Energy Equation]
    NSE -->|velocity field| SPE[Species Equation]
    SPE -->|heat of reaction| ENE
    ENE -->|buoyancy natural convection| NSE
    CON[Continuity] -->|mass conservation| NSE
    CON --> ENE
    CON --> SPE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Insight:** The arrows in the diagram above show that the four equations are *coupled* — temperature affects density (and hence momentum in natural convection), species concentration changes the heat of reaction (Soret, Dufour effects), and velocity is required to solve species and energy. This **nonlinear coupling** is precisely what makes chemical process simulation a *grand challenge* problem.

### 4.4 Coordinate System Variants — Cylindrical and Spherical

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with alternate forms:

- **Rectangular** $(x, y, z)$ — used in Cartesian pipes and ducts.
- **Cylindrical** $(r, \theta, z)$ — used in pipe flow and annular heat exchangers.
- **Spherical** $(r, \theta, \phi)$ — used in droplet evaporation, packed-bed catalysis.

For example, the **continuity equation in cylindrical coordinates** is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the general transport equation for a property $\phi$ in a fluid. Identify the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The general transport equation is:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_{\phi}$$

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\partial(\rho \phi)/\partial t$ | Transient (accumulation) term — rate of change of $\phi$ per unit volume |
| $\nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v} \phi)$ | Convective transport — bulk flow carrying $\phi$ |
| $\nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi)$ | Diffusive transport — molecular-level spreading |
| $S_\phi$ | Volumetric source/sink term — generation or consumption |

The **Continuity, Navier–Stokes, Energy, and Species Continuity** equations are obtained by setting $\phi = 1$, $v_x$, $T$, and $C_A$ respectively, with the appropriate $\Gamma$ and $S_\phi$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Eulerian and Lagrangian frames of reference. Which one is used in deriving the transport equations and why?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Lagrangian frame** — observer moves *with* the fluid particle; tracks the history of an individual molecule.
- **Eulerian frame** — observer is *stationary* in space; measures properties at fixed points through which the fluid flows.

Transport equations are derived in the **Eulerian frame** because:
1. The control volume (or shell) is *fixed in space*, making the math simpler.
2. Most industrial measurements (thermocouples, pressure gauges) are at *fixed locations*, matching the Eulerian viewpoint.
3. The convective term $\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla$ then appears naturally in the **material derivative** to account for the movement of the fluid through the fixed observer.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **continuity equation** in rectangular coordinates for a compressible fluid using the **shell balance** method. Clearly state the underlying assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the **first law of thermodynamics**, derive the **energy equation** for a Newtonian fluid with constant thermal conductivity $k$ and constant viscosity $\mu$, including the **viscous dissipation** term. State the final PDE and explain each term. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. No nuclear reactions (mass is conserved).
2. The control volume is a differential fluid element of size $dx\, dy\, dz$ fixed in space.
3. Continuous fluid with well-defined density $\rho$ and velocity $\mathbf{v} = (v_x, v_y, v_z)$.

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate *in* through the face at $x$ is:
$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{in}} = (\rho v_x)\big|_{x} \, dy\, dz$$

**Step 2:** Mass flow rate *out* through the face at $x+dx$ is:
$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{out}} = (\rho v_x)\big|_{x+dx} \, dy\, dz = (\rho v_x)\big|_{x} \, dy\, dz + \frac{\partial(\rho v_x)}{\partial x} dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 3:** Net *out* in the x-direction (per unit volume):
$$\left(\dot{m}_{x,\text{out}} - \dot{m}_{x,\text{in}}\right)/dV = \frac{\partial(\rho v_x)}{\partial x}$$

**Step 4:** Summing over the three directions:
$$\text{Net out per volume} = \frac{\partial(\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho v_z)}{\partial z}$$

**Step 5:** Mass accumulation in the element:
$$\text{Accumulation} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 6:** Equate (In − Out = Accumulation, rearranged as Out − In = −Accumulation):

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[\frac{\partial(\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho v_z)}{\partial z}\right] dx\, dy\, dz &= -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx\, dy\, dz
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7:** Cancel the volume element and write in vector form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0\;}
\end{aligned}$$

For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho$ = const), this simplifies to $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the fluid element $dV = dx\, dy\, dz$:

$$\text{Rate of change of internal energy} = \dot{Q}_{\text{net in}} - \dot{W}_{\text{by pressure and viscous forces}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{visc dissipation}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{ext source}}$$

**Step 2:** Accumulation of internal energy (per unit volume, with $C_p$ assumed constant):
$$\text{Accumulation} = \rho C_p \frac{D T}{Dt}$$

**Step 3:** Net heat inflow by **conduction** (Fourier's law $\mathbf{q} = -k \nabla T$):
Using the same shell-balance logic as for mass, the net conductive inflow per unit volume is $k \nabla^2 T$.

**Step 4:** **Viscous dissipation** — irreversible conversion of mechanical work into heat:
$$\mu \Phi = \mu \left[ 2\left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}\right)^2 + 2\left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y}\right)^2 + 2\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial x}+\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y}+\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial z}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}+\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial x}\right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 5:** External volumetric source $\dot{Q}$ (W/m³) — e.g., Joule heating, chemical reaction heat.

**Step 6:** Final PDE (energy equation):

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\rho C_p \frac{D T}{Dt} = k \nabla^{2} T + \mu \Phi + \dot{Q}\;}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Term-by-term meaning:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\rho C_p DT/Dt$ | Energy carried with the fluid element (transient + convective) |
| $k \nabla^2 T$ | Heat conducted in by molecular diffusion |
| $\mu \Phi$ | Heat generated by viscous friction (always positive, raises T) |
| $\dot{Q}$ | External heat source per unit volume |

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **Navier–Stokes equation** in the x-direction for an incompressible Newtonian fluid of constant viscosity $\mu$, using Newton's second law and the shell balance approach. State the final vector form of the equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the **general transport equation**, show that the **continuity, momentum, energy, and species equations** are special cases. Clearly identify the values of $\phi$, $\Gamma$, and $S_\phi$ for each case. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's second law to the fluid element:

$$\rho \frac{D v_x}{Dt} \, dV = \sum F_x$$

**Step 2:** The three forces on the element are:
- **Pressure force** in x: $F_P = -(\partial P/\partial x) dV$
- **Viscous force** in x: $F_\mu = \mu \nabla^2 v_x \, dV$ (after applying Newton's law of viscosity for a Newtonian fluid)
- **Body force** (gravity): $F_g = \rho g_x \, dV$

**Step 3:** Equate:

$$\rho \frac{D v_x}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^{2} v_x + \rho g_x$$

**Step 4:** Expand the material derivative $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + \mathbf{v}\cdot \nabla$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\rho \left[ \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} \right] = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial z^{2}} \right) + \rho g_x\;}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Vector form** (combining x, y, z components):

$$\boxed{\;\rho \frac{D \mathbf{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}\;}$$

**Note:** The term $\nabla^2 \mathbf{v}$ is shorthand for applying the Laplacian to each Cartesian component of velocity separately.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

The general transport equation is:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_{\phi}$$

| Case | Property $\phi$ | Diffusivity $\Gamma$ | Source $S_\phi$ | Resulting Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = 1$ (mass) | $\Gamma = 0$ | $S_\phi = 0$ | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$ |
| 2 | $\phi = v_x$ (momentum) | $\Gamma = \mu$ | $S_\phi = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \rho g_x$ | $\rho \dfrac{D v_x}{Dt} = \mu \nabla^2 v_x - \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \rho g_x$ |
| 3 | $\phi = T$ (energy) | $\Gamma = k/C_p$ (or $k$ depending on formulation) | $S_\phi = \mu \Phi/C_p + \dot{Q}/C_p$ | $\rho C_p \dfrac{DT}{Dt} = k \nabla^2 T + \mu \Phi + \dot{Q}$ |
| 4 | $\phi = C_A$ (species) | $\Gamma = \rho D_{AB}$ | $S_\phi = R_A$ | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(C_A \mathbf{v}) = D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A + R_A$ |

This proves that **all four conservation laws are special cases of one unified PDE**, which is why commercial CFD codes can use a *single solver template* (e.g., the SIMPLE algorithm in ANSYS Fluent) for any combination of these phenomena.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Valuation (read carefully!):**
> 1. **Forgetting the convective acceleration terms** ($v_x \partial v_x/\partial x$ etc.) in the Navier–Stokes derivation — a single missing term = **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing the material derivative $D/Dt$ with the partial derivative $\partial/\partial t$** — they are *not* the same. The material derivative *includes* convection.
> 3. **Dropping the viscous dissipation $\mu \Phi$ in the energy equation** — KTU examiners often check this specifically; omission = **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Missing the negative sign in Newton's law of viscosity** $\tau = -\mu \, d v/dy$ — sign errors propagate everywhere.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., Newtonian, incompressible, constant $\mu$, no-slip) — KTU's *Course Outcome CO1* and *CO2* explicitly require this; missing them costs **1 to 2 marks**.
> 6. **Failing to write the final equation in *vector* form** after the component form — KTU board examiners want the unified compact form for the last step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **general transport equation** is the *unified template* from which all four laws emerge.
- **Continuity** is obtained with $\phi = 1$, $\Gamma = 0$, $S_\phi = 0$ — the *simplest* case but also the *most fundamental*.
- **Momentum equation** requires Newton's law of viscosity to close the problem (constitutive equation).
- **Energy equation** requires Fourier's law of conduction to close the problem.
- **Species equation** requires Fick's law of diffusion to close the problem.
- Always expand the **material derivative** $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + \mathbf{v}\cdot \nabla$ before writing the final PDE.
- The **nonlinear convective terms** are what distinguish fluid mechanics from solid mechanics and are the *root* of turbulence.
- For **incompressible** flow, set $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ — this is a *constraint*, not an equation you derive separately.
- **Cylindrical and spherical** coordinate forms are required for problems involving pipes, drops, and particles — the KTU 2024 syllabus lists them under Module 1.
- **Boundary conditions** must be physically consistent: no-slip at walls, zero-gradient (Neumann) at symmetry planes, periodic at cyclic boundaries.
- **Limiting case verification** — always test your derived equation against known limits (e.g., steady-state, 1D, fully developed) to catch sign or term errors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for KTU PEPOT527 Module 1:** *"Accumulation = Convective in − Convective out + Diffusive in − Diffusive out + Source."* — If you remember only one sentence, remember this. Every transport equation in this course is a *variation on this theme*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations of State and Equilibrium States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equations of State and Equilibrium States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: EQUATIONS OF STATE AND EQUILIBRIUM STATES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

### A. Equation of State (EoS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **Equation of State (EoS)** is a thermodynamic constitutive equation that mathematically relates the macroscopic state variables of a pure substance or mixture — specifically **Pressure ($P$)**, **Volume ($V$)**, and **Temperature ($T$)** — along with composition. It provides a closed-form algebraic or implicit functional relationship that uniquely defines the thermodynamic state of a system at equilibrium.

The most general implicit form is:

$$f(P, V, T, n_1, n_2, ..., n_k) = 0$$

For a pure component, this reduces to the **PVT relationship**:

$$f(P, V, T) = 0$$

### B. Equilibrium State

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> An **Equilibrium State** of a thermodynamic system is a stationary state in which the macroscopic properties (P, T, composition) are **spatially uniform** (or follow a defined distribution) and **temporally invariant** with respect to time, such that no net driving force exists for any spontaneous change in the system.

A system is in true thermodynamic equilibrium when **all three** of the following sub-equilibria are simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Mechanical Equilibrium** → Uniform pressure ($P_1 = P_2 = ...$)
2. **Thermal Equilibrium** → Uniform temperature ($T_1 = T_2 = ...$)
3. **Chemical / Phase Equilibrium** → Uniform chemical potential ($\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta} = ...$)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Model:**

Think of an **Equation of State** as a **"recipe card"** for a gas. Imagine you have a sealed pressure cooker on your stove. The recipe card (EoS) tells you: *"If I set the burner to temperature $T$ and the cooker has volume $V$, then the pressure inside will be $P$."* The recipe (EoS) is **universal** — it works for the same gas anywhere in the universe, as long as the state variables are known. Different gases (oxygen, nitrogen, steam) have **different recipe cards** (different EoS parameters).

> **Equilibrium Analogy — The "Balanced Seesaw":**
> Imagine a perfectly balanced seesaw with three children sitting on it — one representing pressure, one temperature, and one chemical composition. The seesaw is **stationary** (not moving, not changing). If you push one child down (disturb one variable), the seesaw tilts, and the system is no longer in equilibrium — it will spontaneously adjust (e.g., flow of heat, expansion, or reaction) until balance is restored. Equilibrium is that "perfect balance point" where **no further spontaneous change is possible**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants (Standard Metrics)

The following fundamental constants are used in deriving and applying Equations of State:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $\mathbf{R = 8.314 \, J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP):** $\mathbf{T = 273.15 \, K}$, $\mathbf{P = 101.325 \, kPa}$
- **Critical Constants** (substance-specific): $P_c$, $T_c$, $V_c$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $\mathbf{k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, J \cdot K^{-1}}$
- **Avogadro's Number:** $\mathbf{N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, mol^{-1}}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Equations of State

Equations of State are broadly classified into four families based on their mathematical complexity and physical fidelity:

### A. Ideal Gas Equation of State
- The **simplest and most fundamental** EoS.
- Assumes: **point particles**, **no intermolecular forces**, **elastic collisions**.
- Valid only at **low pressures** and **high temperatures** where gases behave ideally.

### B. Cubic Equations of State
- Account for **finite molecular volume** (repulsive forces) and **intermolecular attraction** (van der Waals forces).
- Named "cubic" because they yield a **cubic polynomial** in compressibility factor $Z$ or molar volume $V$.
- Examples: **Van der Waals (vdW)**, **Redlich-Kwong (RK)**, **Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK)**, **Peng-Robinson (PR)**.

### C. Virial Equation of State
- A **series expansion** in either $P$ or $1/V$ derived from statistical mechanics.
- Infinitely expandable; truncated forms (2nd, 3rd virial) are widely used.
- **Theoretically rigorous** for moderate densities.

### D. Multi-Constant & Reference Fluid Equations
- **Benedict-Webb-Rubin (BWR)**, **Lee-Kesler**, **SAFT (Statistical Associating Fluid Theory)**.
- Used for **liquids**, **polymers**, **electrolytes**, and **supercritical fluids**.

---

## 2.2 Logic Flow — Why We Need an EoS

1. **Step 1 — State Postulate:** A pure substance in equilibrium is fully defined by **two independent intensive properties** (e.g., $P$ and $T$).
2. **Step 2 — Missing Link:** Knowing $P$ and $T$ is not enough to find $V$ (or density) without a **closure relationship** — this is the EoS.
3. **Step 3 — Constitutive Nature:** The EoS is **NOT derived from the First or Second Law**; it is a **constitutive equation** (property of the specific substance).
4. **Step 4 — Engineering Utility:** EoS enables calculation of **thermodynamic derivatives** like $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$, fugacity, enthalpy/entropy departures — all needed for process simulation.

---

## 2.3 Equilibrium — Deeper Taxonomy

| Sub-Type | Driving Force for Departure | Equilibrium Criterion | Math Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Pressure gradient | $dP = 0$ (no net force) | $P^{\alpha} = P^{\beta}$ |
| **Thermal** | Temperature gradient | $dT = 0$ (no net heat flow) | $T^{\alpha} = T^{\beta}$ |
| **Chemical** | Reaction affinity | $dG = 0$ (no net reaction) | $\sum \nu_i \mu_i = 0$ |
| **Phase** | Chemical potential difference | $dG = 0$ at constant $T, P$ | $\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta}$ |
| **Diffusive (Multicomponent)** | Concentration gradient | Chemical potential equality | $\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Criterion for Equilibrium (KTU Key Result):**
> For a closed system at constant $T$ and $P$, the equilibrium state is the one that **minimizes the total Gibbs free energy** $G$ of the system. This is the **variational principle** of equilibrium thermodynamics.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM-READY FORMULA TABLE** — Memorize these for ESE/Module tests.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Applicability / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ideal Gas EoS** | $PV = nRT$ | Low $P$, high $T$, dilute gases |
| 2 | **Van der Waals EoS** | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$ | Moderate $P$, real gases |
| 3 | **Redlich-Kwong EoS** | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V - b} - \dfrac{a}{T^{0.5} V(V+b)}$ | Non-polar gases & light hydrocarbons |
| 4 | **Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK)** | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V - b} - \dfrac{a(T)}{V(V+b)}$ | Vapour-liquid equilibrium (VLE) |
| 5 | **Peng-Robinson (PR)** | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V - b} - \dfrac{a(T)}{V(V+b) + b(V - b)}$ | Hydrocarbons, CO₂, H₂S |
| 6 | **Virial EoS (in Z)** | $Z = 1 + B'P + C'P^2 + ...$ | Gases below critical $T$ |
| 7 | **Virial EoS (in 1/V)** | $Z = 1 + \dfrac{B}{V} + \dfrac{C}{V^2} + ...$ | Statistical mechanics basis |
| 8 | **Compressibility Factor** | $Z = \dfrac{PV}{nRT}$ | Measure of non-ideality |
| 9 | **Mechanical Eq. Criterion** | $P^{\alpha} = P^{\beta}$ | Multi-phase systems |
| 10 | **Thermal Eq. Criterion** | $T^{\alpha} = T^{\beta}$ | Heat transfer absence |
| 11 | **Phase Eq. Criterion** | $\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta}$ | Multi-component, multi-phase |
| 12 | **Chemical Eq. Criterion** | $\sum_i \nu_i \mu_i = 0$ | Reactive systems |
| 13 | **Fugacity Equality (alt. form)** | $f_i^{\alpha} = f_i^{\beta}$ | Practical VLE calculations |
| 14 | **Maxwell Relation (1)** | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | From $dG = -SdT + VdP$ |
| 15 | **Maxwell Relation (2)** | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | From $dH = TdS + VdP$ |

> **CRITICAL:** Note the Peng-Robinson denominator — $\left[V(V+b) + b(V-b)\right]$ is **NOT** the same as $V^2 + 2bV - b^2$ miswritten. Be precise in derivations.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cubic EoS (PR, SRK):** Backbone of refinery simulation (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, PRO/II) for distillation columns, reactors, and compressors.
- **Virial EoS:** Used in **cryogenic gas processing** (LNG, air separation) where second virial coefficient is sufficient.
- **Equilibrium Criteria:** Foundation of **flash calculations**, **reactor design** (equilibrium conversion), and **distillation column** modeling (VLE).
- **Process Simulation:** Without an EoS, **no property package** can be built in any commercial process simulator.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Ideal Gas Equation of State

### Starting Assumptions (Kinetic Theory)
- Gas consists of a **large number of identical particles** in **random motion**.
- The **volume of particles is negligible** compared to the container volume.
- **No intermolecular forces** except during perfectly elastic collisions.
- **Average kinetic energy** is proportional to absolute temperature.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Pressure on a wall:**
Consider a single molecule of mass $m$ moving with velocity components $(v_x, v_y, v_z)$ in a cubic container of side $L$.

The change in momentum upon elastic collision with a wall perpendicular to the $x$-axis is:

$$\Delta p_x = m v_x - (-m v_x) = 2 m v_x$$

**Step 2 — Frequency of collisions:**
Time between successive collisions with the same wall:

$$\Delta t = \dfrac{2L}{v_x}$$

Force exerted by ONE molecule on the wall:

$$F_1 = \dfrac{\Delta p_x}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{2 m v_x}{2L / v_x} = \dfrac{m v_x^2}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Total force from $N$ molecules:**
Sum over all $N$ molecules and take the **time-averaged** value:

$$F = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{m v_{i,x}^2}{L} = \dfrac{m}{L} \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_{i,x}^2 = \dfrac{N m \langle v_x^2 \rangle}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Relate to mean square speed:**
By isotropy, $\langle v_x^2 \rangle = \langle v_y^2 \rangle = \langle v_z^2 \rangle = \dfrac{1}{3} \langle c^2 \rangle$, where $\langle c^2 \rangle$ is the mean-square speed.

**Step 5 — Pressure = Force / Area:**
Wall area is $L^2$, so:

$$P = \dfrac{F}{L^2} = \dfrac{N m \langle c^2 \rangle}{3 L^3}$$

**Step 6 — Volume of container:**
$V = L^3$, giving:

$$P = \dfrac{N m \langle c^2 \rangle}{3 V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV = \dfrac{1}{3} N m \langle c^2 \rangle$$

**Step 7 — Relate to temperature:**
The mean kinetic energy per molecule from kinetic theory is $\dfrac{1}{2} m \langle c^2 \rangle = \dfrac{3}{2} k_B T$.

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{1}{3} N m \langle c^2 \rangle = N k_B T$$

**Step 8 — Final result (Ideal Gas EoS):**
Let $n = N / N_A$, so $N k_B = n R$:

$$\boxed{PV = nRT}$$

This is the **Ideal Gas Equation of State**, valid for all ideal gases regardless of chemical identity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Van der Waals Equation of State

### Starting Point
The ideal gas EoS: $P_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{RT}{V - b}$. Van der Waals introduced **two corrections** to account for non-ideality.

### Correction 1 — Repulsive Forces (Excluded Volume)
Real molecules occupy **finite volume**. The available volume for molecular motion is **not** $V$ but $(V - nb)$, where $b$ is the **co-volume** (≈ 4 times the actual molecular volume).

### Correction 2 — Attractive Forces (Internal Pressure)
Intermolecular attractions **reduce** the pressure exerted on the walls. The pressure correction is proportional to the **square of the number density** $\left(\dfrac{n}{V}\right)^2$, giving an attractive term $\dfrac{a n^2}{V^2}$.

### Final Cubic Form (in $V$)

$$\boxed{\left(P + \dfrac{a n^2}{V^2}\right)\left(\dfrac{V}{n} - b\right) = RT}$$

Or, in terms of **molar volume** $\bar{V} = V/n$:

$$\left(P + \dfrac{a}{\bar{V}^2}\right)\left(\bar{V} - b\right) = RT$$

### Step-by-Step Expansion to Cubic

$$\left(P + \dfrac{a}{\bar{V}^2}\right)\left(\bar{V} - b\right) = RT$$

Expand the left side:

$$P \bar{V} - P b + \dfrac{a}{\bar{V}} - \dfrac{a b}{\bar{V}^2} = RT$$

Multiply throughout by $\bar{V}^2$:

$$P \bar{V}^3 - P b \bar{V}^2 + a \bar{V} - a b = R T \bar{V}^2$$

Rearrange into **standard cubic polynomial form**:

$$\boxed{P \bar{V}^3 - (P b + R T) \bar{V}^2 + a \bar{V} - a b = 0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **cubic in $\bar{V}$** — hence the name "cubic equation of state." For a given $P$ and $T$, the **three roots** correspond physically to: (i) liquid root, (ii) unstable root, (iii) vapour root.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equilibrium Criteria from the Second Law

### Starting Point: Second Law of Thermodynamics
For an **isolated system**, the entropy must satisfy:

$$dS_{\text{isolated}} \geq 0 \quad \text{(equality at equilibrium)}$$

### Derivation for Constant T, P (Closed System)

For a closed system exchanging only heat and work with surroundings, the combined entropy change is:

$$dS_{\text{universe}} = dS_{\text{system}} + dS_{\text{surroundings}} \geq 0$$

At constant $T$ and $P$, surroundings exchange heat $dQ = -dH$ (since $H$ changes with heat at constant $P$):

$$dS_{\text{surroundings}} = -\dfrac{dH}{T}$$

Therefore:

$$dS_{\text{system}} - \dfrac{dH}{T} \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T dS - dH \geq 0$$

Substitute $G = H - TS$, so $dG = dH - TdS - SdT$. At constant $T$ ($dT = 0$):

$$dG = dH - TdS$$

Substituting into the inequality:

$$-dG \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{dG \leq 0}$$

**Interpretation:** At constant $T$ and $P$, **any spontaneous process decreases Gibbs free energy**, and **equilibrium is reached when $G$ is minimized** ($dG = 0$, $d^2G > 0$).

### General Equilibrium Conditions

| System Constraint | Equilibrium Criterion |
|---|---|
| Isolated ($U, V, N$ constant) | $dS = 0$, $S$ is maximum |
| Constant $T, V$ (canonical) | $dA = 0$, Helmholtz $A$ is minimum |
| Constant $T, P$ (Gibbs) | $dG = 0$, Gibbs $G$ is minimum |
| Constant $S, P$ (enthalpy) | $dH = 0$, $H$ is minimum |
| Constant $S, V$ (internal energy) | $dU = 0$, $U$ is minimum |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maxwell Relations from EoS

Starting from the **exact differential** of Gibbs free energy:

$$dG = -S \, dT + V \, dP$$

By the **Euler reciprocity condition** (mixed partials of a state function are equal):

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial P}\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

Since $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T = V$:

$$\boxed{\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P}$$

This is the **Maxwell relation** that allows us to compute **entropy changes from PVT data** obtained from an EoS — a critical link in process simulation.

### Application: Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas
For an ideal gas, $V = \dfrac{RT}{P}$, so $\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{R}{P}$.

Therefore:

$$\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\dfrac{R}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta S = -R \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$$

This result is **derived directly from the EoS**, demonstrating the EoS's central role.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Equation of State." Why is it classified as a constitutive equation rather than a fundamental law of thermodynamics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **Equation of State (EoS)** is a mathematical relationship that interconnects the macroscopic state variables **Pressure (P), Volume (V), and Temperature (T)** of a pure substance or mixture, providing a closure relation that uniquely determines the thermodynamic state.

> It is termed a **constitutive equation** because it is **not derivable from the First or Second Law of Thermodynamics** (which are universal). Instead, the EoS depends on the **specific molecular nature** of the substance — its size, shape, polarity, and intermolecular forces. Different substances (e.g., water vs. methane) require different EoS, with substance-specific constants (e.g., $a$, $b$ in van der Waals; $P_c$, $T_c$ in corresponding states). **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State and explain the three conditions that must be simultaneously satisfied for a system to be in complete thermodynamic equilibrium.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A system is in complete thermodynamic equilibrium when **all three** sub-equilibria hold:

1. **Mechanical Equilibrium:** Net force on every movable boundary is zero; equivalently, pressure is uniform throughout the system and across all phases: $P^{\alpha} = P^{\beta} = P$. **(1 mark)**

2. **Thermal Equilibrium:** No net heat flow between any two parts; the system has a single uniform temperature: $T^{\alpha} = T^{\beta} = T$. **(1 mark)**

3. **Chemical (or Phase) Equilibrium:** No net chemical reaction occurs, and no net mass transfer occurs between phases. For each species $i$, the chemical potential is uniform: $\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta}$, or equivalently, fugacities are equal: $f_i^{\alpha} = f_i^{\beta}$. **(1 mark)**

Failure of any one criterion means the system is in a **non-equilibrium (constrained)** state.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 📌 INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE of the following

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the **Ideal Gas Equation of State** from kinetic theory, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the Ideal Gas Law, derive the **Van der Waals Equation of State**, including the physical significance of the constants $a$ and $b$. Compute the **critical constants** ($P_c$, $V_c$, $T_c$) for a van der Waals gas. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Ideal Gas EoS from Kinetic Theory** **[7 marks]**

**Assumptions** [1 mark]:
- Gas comprises a large number of identical, **point-mass molecules**.
- The **volume of molecules is negligible** compared to the container.
- **No intermolecular forces** act except during collisions.
- All collisions (molecule-molecule, molecule-wall) are **perfectly elastic**.
- The motion is completely **random**, and the gas is isotropic.

**Derivation Steps** [6 marks]:

Consider $N$ molecules, each of mass $m$, in a cube of side $L$, total volume $V = L^3$.

**Step 1:** For one molecule striking a wall perpendicular to the $x$-axis with velocity component $v_x$, the change in momentum per collision is $\Delta p_x = 2 m v_x$.

**Step 2:** Time between successive collisions with the same wall is $\Delta t = \dfrac{2L}{v_x}$.

**Step 3:** Force from one molecule: $F_1 = \dfrac{2 m v_x}{2L / v_x} = \dfrac{m v_x^2}{L}$.

**Step 4:** Total force from $N$ molecules (time-averaged):
$$F = \dfrac{N m \langle v_x^2 \rangle}{L}$$

**Step 5:** By isotropy: $\langle v_x^2 \rangle = \dfrac{1}{3} \langle c^2 \rangle$, where $c$ is molecular speed.

**Step 6:** Pressure $P = F / L^2$:
$$P = \dfrac{N m \langle c^2 \rangle}{3 V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV = \dfrac{1}{3} N m \langle c^2 \rangle$$

**Step 7:** From kinetic theory, $\dfrac{1}{2} m \langle c^2 \rangle = \dfrac{3}{2} k_B T$, so:
$$PV = N k_B T = n R T$$

**Result:** $\boxed{PV = nRT}$ — Ideal Gas EoS.

---

**Part (b) — Van der Waals EoS and Critical Constants** **[7 marks]**

**Starting point:** $P = \dfrac{RT}{\bar{V}}$ for an ideal gas. Two corrections: [1 mark for stating corrections]

- **Excluded volume:** Available volume is $(\bar{V} - b)$, where $b$ is the covolume (≈ 4× actual molecular volume per mole). This accounts for the **finite size** of molecules (repulsion).
- **Internal pressure:** Intermolecular attractions reduce the wall pressure by $\dfrac{a}{\bar{V}^2}$, where $a$ is the attraction parameter.

**Substituting these corrections** [1 mark]:

$$P = \dfrac{RT}{\bar{V} - b} - \dfrac{a}{\bar{V}^2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\left(P + \dfrac{a}{\bar{V}^2}\right)\left(\bar{V} - b\right) = RT}$$

**Physical significance of constants** [1 mark]:
- $a$ (Pa·m⁶/mol²) — measures the strength of **intermolecular attractive forces**.
- $b$ (m³/mol) — measures the **excluded volume** per mole due to finite molecular size.

**Critical Constant Derivation** [4 marks]:

The critical point is where $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \bar{V}}\right)_T = 0$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial \bar{V}^2}\right)_T = 0$ (inflection point of the isotherm).

Starting from the explicit form:
$$P = \dfrac{RT}{\bar{V} - b} - \dfrac{a}{\bar{V}^2}$$

**First derivative:**
$$\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \bar{V}}\right)_T = -\dfrac{RT}{(\bar{V} - b)^2} + \dfrac{2a}{\bar{V}^3} = 0$$

**Second derivative:**
$$\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial \bar{V}^2}\right)_T = \dfrac{2RT}{(\bar{V} - b)^3} - \dfrac{6a}{\bar{V}^4} = 0$$

At the critical point $\bar{V} = \bar{V}_c$ and $T = T_c$. Setting up the two equations and dividing to eliminate $RT_c$:

$$\dfrac{2a / \bar{V}_c^3}{2a / \bar{V}_c^4} = \dfrac{RT_c/(\bar{V}_c - b)^2}{6a / \bar{V}_c^4}$$

After simplification:

$$\bar{V}_c = 3b$$

Substituting back into the first derivative condition:

$$T_c = \dfrac{8a}{27 R b}$$

Substituting both into the EoS:

$$P_c = \dfrac{a}{27 b^2}$$

**Final critical constant relations** [boxed result]:

$$\boxed{V_c = 3b, \quad P_c = \dfrac{a}{27 b^2}, \quad T_c = \dfrac{8a}{27 R b}}$$

**Universal compressibility factor at critical point:**
$$Z_c = \dfrac{P_c V_c}{R T_c} = \dfrac{(a/27b^2)(3b)}{R(8a/27Rb)} = \dfrac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

> Note: Real gases have $Z_c \approx 0.27$–$0.29$, so vdW overestimates $Z_c$ — a known limitation.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define **thermodynamic equilibrium**. With the aid of suitable mathematical expressions, derive the **equilibrium criteria** for a closed system at constant temperature and pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **Maxwell relations**? Starting from the exact differential of the Gibbs free energy, derive the four Maxwell relations and explain their **utility in process simulation** with one example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Equilibrium Definition and Derivation** **[7 marks]**

**Definition** [1 mark]:
Thermodynamic equilibrium is the state of a system in which all macroscopic properties are spatially uniform (or systematically distributed) and do not change with time, so that no net driving force exists for any spontaneous change (heat flow, mass flow, or chemical reaction).

**Derivation using Second Law** [6 marks]:

For an **isolated system**, the Second Law requires:
$$dS_{\text{isolated}} \geq 0 \quad \text{with equality at equilibrium}$$

For a closed system in thermal contact with a reservoir at temperature $T$ and mechanical contact at pressure $P$, the universe is the system plus the reservoir. The reservoir's entropy change when the system exchanges heat $dQ = dH - VdP = dH$ (at constant $P$) is:

$$dS_{\text{reservoir}} = -\dfrac{dH_{\text{system}}}{T}$$

The total entropy change:
$$dS_{\text{total}} = dS_{\text{system}} - \dfrac{dH_{\text{system}}}{T} \geq 0$$

Multiplying by $T$:
$$T \, dS_{\text{system}} - dH_{\text{system}} \geq 0$$

Substituting the definition of Gibbs free energy $G = H - TS$, the differential at constant $T$ gives $dG = dH - T dS$, hence:
$$-dG \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{dG \leq 0 \text{ (spontaneous process)}}$$

At equilibrium:
$$\boxed{dG = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad d^2G > 0 \text{ (stable equilibrium)}}$$

This means the **Gibbs free energy is minimized** at constant $T$ and $P$.

**Equilibrium Criteria for a Spontaneous Change at constant T, P:**
$$\Delta G_{\text{system}} \leq 0$$

For a multi-phase, multi-component system, the phase equilibrium condition becomes:
$$\mu_i^{\alpha}(T, P) = \mu_i^{\beta}(T, P) = \mu_i^{\gamma}(T, P) = ...$$

For a chemical reaction $0 = \sum_i \nu_i A_i$, the chemical equilibrium condition is:
$$\sum_i \nu_i \mu_i = 0$$

---

**Part (b) — Maxwell Relations and Their Utility** **[7 marks]**

**Definition** [1 mark]:
Maxwell relations are a set of equations that equate mixed second partial derivatives of thermodynamic potentials. They arise from the **equality of mixed partials** of an exact differential (Euler's reciprocity condition) and allow **non-measurable quantities** (like entropy) to be calculated from **measurable PVT data**.

**Derivation** [4 marks]:

Four fundamental potentials with their differentials:

$$dU = T dS - P dV$$
$$dH = T dS + V dP$$
$$dA = -S dT - P dV$$
$$dG = -S dT + V dP$$

**Apply the reciprocity condition** $\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \, \partial y} = \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y \, \partial x}$ to each:

| Potential | First Derivatives | Maxwell Relation |
|---|---|---|
| $U(S, V)$ | $T = (\partial U/\partial S)_V$, $P = -(\partial U/\partial V)_S$ | $\boxed{\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V}$ |
| $H(S, P)$ | $T = (\partial H/\partial S)_P$, $V = (\partial H/\partial P)_S$ | $\boxed{\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P}$ |
| $A(T, V)$ | $S = -(\partial A/\partial T)_V$, $P = -(\partial A/\partial V)_T$ | $\boxed{\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V}$ |
| $G(T, P)$ | $S = -(\partial G/\partial T)_P$, $V = (\partial G/\partial P)_T$ | $\boxed{\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P}$ |

**Utility in Process Simulation** [2 marks]:

Maxwell relations are the **bridge** between an EoS (which gives PVT data) and **calorimetric quantities** (entropy, enthalpy). For example, to compute the entropy change of a real gas from state 1 to state 2:

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{C_p}{T} dT - \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

The integrand $\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ is obtained from the chosen EoS (e.g., differentiating van der Waals EoS with respect to $T$). This is how simulators like **Aspen HYSYS** calculate real-gas entropy and enthalpy departures.

**Example — Entropy change of an ideal gas from Maxwell relation:**
For an ideal gas, $V = RT/P$:
$$\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{R}{P}$$

Therefore:
$$\Delta S = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \left(-\dfrac{R}{P}\right) dP = -R \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$$

This is a textbook result, but the **method** is what matters for process simulation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing constitutive vs. fundamental:** Many students wrongly claim that the Ideal Gas EoS is "derived from the First Law." It is **NOT** — it is constitutive, derivable from kinetic theory or empirical observation. **Loss: 2 marks** in 3-mark definitions.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Maxwell relation signs:** The most common error is writing $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = +\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ (missing the negative sign). **Loss: 1 mark per relation.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting the assumptions in derivations:** KTU examiners allocate **1 full mark** for stating assumptions in the kinetic-theory derivation. Skipping this step = direct 1-mark loss.
>
> 4. **Sign error in $\left(P + a/\bar{V}^2\right)$ term:** Students sometimes write $P - a/\bar{V}^2$ instead of $+\ a/\bar{V}^2$ in the van der Waals equation. The $a$ term **adds** to pressure because it represents the *missing* pull from intermolecular attractions.
>
> 5. **Van der Waals cubic form:** Writing $\left(P - \dfrac{a}{\bar{V}^2}\right)\left(\bar{V} - b\right) = RT$ instead of the correct form. **Verify the sign.**
>
> 6. **Critical constant error:** Forgetting that $V_c$ is the **molar** critical volume. $V_c = 3b$ is per mole; do not multiply by $n$ unless explicitly asked.
>
> 7. **Phase equilibrium criterion:** Writing $f_i^{\alpha} = P$ (vapor pressure) instead of $f_i^{\alpha} = f_i^{\beta}$ (fugacity equality between phases).
>
> 8. **Unit mismatches:** When using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), ensure pressure is in **Pa** and volume in **m³** — not bar or litres — unless using $R = 0.08314$ bar·L/(mol·K).

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- ✅ **EoS** = P-V-T constitutive closure relation: $f(P, V, T) = 0$
- ✅ **Ideal Gas EoS:** $PV = nRT$
- ✅ **Compressibility factor:** $Z = \dfrac{PV}{nRT}$ (1 for ideal, ≠1 for real)
- ✅ **Equilibrium** = stationary, time-invariant state with zero net driving force
- ✅ **Variational Principle:** At constant T, P, equilibrium ↔ $G$ is **minimized**

### 🔑 Key Equations to Memorize
- ✅ **Van der Waals:** $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{\bar{V}^2}\right)\left(\bar{V} - b\right) = RT$
- ✅ **Critical Constants of vdW:** $V_c = 3b$, $P_c = \dfrac{a}{27b^2}$, $T_c = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}$, $Z_c = 3/8 = 0.375$
- ✅ **Four Maxwell Relations** (signs critical)
- ✅ **Phase Eq.:** $\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta}$ ↔ $f_i^{\alpha} = f_i^{\beta}$
- ✅ **Chemical Eq.:** $\sum \nu_i \mu_i = 0$

### 🔑 Classification Snapshot
- ✅ **Ideal Gas → Cubic → Virial → Multi-constant → Theoretical (statistical)**
- ✅ **Equilibrium types: Mechanical / Thermal / Chemical / Phase**
- ✅ **Cubic EoS PR is preferred for hydrocarbons; SRK for VLE; BWR for dense fluids.**

### 🔑 Engineering Relevance
- ✅ EoS is the **backbone of all process simulators** (Aspen, HYSYS, PRO/II).
- ✅ Equilibrium criteria enable **flash calculations, distillation design, reactor analysis.**
- ✅ Maxwell relations convert **PVT data (from EoS) into entropy/enthalpy values.**

### 🔑 Numerical Constants to Remember
- ✅ $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- ✅ $R = 0.08314 \, \text{bar} \cdot \text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- ✅ STP: $T = 273.15$ K, $P = 101.325$ kPa
- ✅ vdW $Z_c = 0.375$ (real gases: 0.27–0.29)

> 🎯 **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** Always **state assumptions** before derivations, **draw a P-V isotherm** when discussing cubic EoS, and **mention the physical significance** of every constant introduced — examiners reward conceptual clarity with bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Kinetics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chemical Kinetics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Kinetics** is the branch of physical chemistry and chemical engineering that quantifies the **rate at which chemical species are consumed or produced** in a reacting system, together with the **mathematical relationships (rate laws)** that connect these rates to the operating variables of the process — namely, **concentration, temperature, pressure, and catalyst activity**.

In the context of **Process Modelling & Simulation (PEPOT527)**, chemical kinetics is the *physical-chemical kernel* of every reactor model. Without an accurate rate expression, no amount of sophisticated numerical solution of mass, energy, or momentum balances will produce meaningful predictions. The rate law closes the system of differential (or algebraic) equations that constitute the mathematical model of the reactor.

### 1.1.1 Rate of Reaction — Precise Definition

For the elementary reaction stoichiometry:

$$\nu_A A + \nu_B B \longrightarrow \nu_R R + \nu_S S$$

the **rate of disappearance of A** per unit volume is rigorously defined as:

$$-r_A \;=\; -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt} \;=\; k\,C_A^{\alpha}\,C_B^{\beta}$$

where:
- $N_A$ is the moles of species A, $V$ is the reactor volume, $t$ is time,
- $k$ is the **rate constant** (with units that depend on the overall order),
- $C_A,\,C_B$ are the concentrations of A and B,
- $\alpha,\,\beta$ are the **partial orders** with respect to A and B.

The **overall order** of the reaction is $n = \alpha + \beta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention (KTU convention):** $-r_A$ denotes the *rate of disappearance* of A (always positive when A is consumed). The *rate of formation* of product R is written as $r_R$ and is positive when R is produced. Stoichiometry links them: $-r_A/\nu_A = r_R/\nu_R = r$ (the *reaction rate*).

### 1.1.2 Molecularity vs. Order of Reaction

| Property | Molecularity | Order of Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Number of molecules colliding in a single elementary step | Sum of exponents in the empirical rate law |
| Allowed values | Integers only (1, 2, 3) | Any real or fractional number |
| Determined by | Reaction mechanism (microscopic) | Experiment (macroscopic) |
| Applicable to | Elementary reactions only | Any reaction, elementary or not |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Pot" Model

Imagine a restaurant kitchen where a chef is boiling rice:

| Chemical Kinetics Term | Kitchen Analogy |
|---|---|
| Concentration $C_A$ | Amount of uncooked rice in the pot |
| Rate constant $k$ | Power of the stove (high $k$ = strong flame) |
| Activation energy $E$ | "Stubbornness" of rice grains to absorb water |
| Temperature $T$ | Heat setting on the stove |
| Order of reaction $n$ | How the rate scales with the rice amount (linear? quadratic?) |
| Catalyst | Pressure-cooker lid — lowers the energy barrier without being consumed |

If you **double the rice** (concentration) and the order is **first order**, the cooking rate doubles linearly. If it is **second order**, quadrupling the rice makes the rate *quadruple* — analogous to rice grains needing to find each other in the pot. The **activation energy** is like the minimum water temperature required before rice begins to soften — below it, the reaction is negligibly slow.

This analogy clarifies *why* chemical kinetics is central to reactor modelling: the rate expression tells the simulation engine **how fast molecules are being converted** under any given set of conditions — the fundamental engine of every process model.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A good chemical engineer does not memorize rate laws — they internalize that *the rate expression is the heart of the model*. A perfect mass balance with the wrong $k$ produces perfectly wrong answers.

## 1.3 Why Chemical Kinetics Matters in Process Modelling

A process model integrates three sub-models:
1. **Mass balances** (conservation of mass),
2. **Energy balances** (conservation of energy),
3. **Rate laws** (chemical kinetics — the *source/sink* term).

Without (3), the balance equations are *inert* — the closed system of ODEs/PDEs has no driving force. The Arrhenius dependence of $k$ on $T$ also makes kinetics the **coupling point** between mass and energy balances in non-isothermal reactors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Module 1 of PEPOT527 establishes the rate expression framework that later modules use to build full reactor models (CSTR, PFR, batch) and to set up ODE systems for numerical simulation (Euler, RK4, etc.). Mastery of this module is **prerequisite** for Modules 2–5.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Elementary vs. Non-Elementary Reactions

- **Elementary reaction:** Occurs in a single molecular event. The rate law is **directly derivable from the stoichiometry** (molecularity = order).
- **Non-elementary reaction:** Proceeds through a sequence of elementary steps. The observed rate law is the result of the **rate-determining step** and any **pre-equilibria**; order may not match stoichiometric coefficients.

Example — the classic non-elementary case:

$$H_2 + Br_2 \longrightarrow 2\,HBr$$

Stoichiometry suggests a second-order rate law, but experimentally the rate is:

$$r_{HBr} = \frac{k_1\,C_{H_2}\,C_{Br_2}^{1/2}}{k_2 + C_{HBr}/C_{Br_2}}$$

This half-order dependence on $Br_2$ reveals a **radical chain mechanism** — a non-trivial reminder that order must be determined experimentally.

## 2.2 Classification of Reactions by Order

### 2.2.1 Zero-Order Reactions
The rate is **independent of concentration**:

$$-r_A = k \quad\Longrightarrow\quad [k] = \text{mol}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

Physically: occurs when the surface is saturated (heterogeneous catalysis at high coverage) or when a photochemical reaction is limited by light absorption.

### 2.2.2 First-Order Reactions
$$-r_A = k\,C_A \quad\Longrightarrow\quad [k] = \text{s}^{-1}$$

Radioactive decay, many unimolecular decompositions.

### 2.2.3 Second-Order Reactions
$$-r_A = k\,C_A^2 \quad\text{or}\quad -r_A = k\,C_A\,C_B$$
$$[k] = \text{L}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

Bimolecular elementary reactions.

### 2.2.4 $n^{\text{th}}$-Order Reactions
$$-r_A = k\,C_A^n$$

The order $n$ is **determined from experimental data**, not from stoichiometry.

## 2.3 Temperature Dependence — The Arrhenius Equation

The rate constant $k$ varies with absolute temperature $T$ according to:

$$k(T) = A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{R\,T}\right)$$

where:
- $A$ = **pre-exponential (frequency) factor** — same units as $k$,
- $E$ = **activation energy** — typically **40–250 kJ/mol** for most chemical reactions,
- $R$ = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (universal gas constant).

### 2.3.1 Two-Temperature Form (commonly used in KTU problems)

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick rule of thumb:** Near room temperature, a **10 K rise** typically **doubles** the rate of many reactions (valid for $E \approx 50$ kJ/mol).

### 2.3.2 Physical Origin of the Arrhenius Equation

From **Transition State Theory (TST)**:

$$k = \kappa\,\frac{k_B T}{h}\,\frac{Q^{\ddagger}}{Q_A Q_B}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_0}{RT}\right)$$

where $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, $h$ is Planck's constant, and $Q$ denotes partition functions. The Arrhenius form is recovered when $T$ variations in $Q$ are weak compared to the exponential.

## 2.4 Integrated Rate Laws — Constant-Volume Batch Reactor

The starting differential equation for a constant-volume batch reactor is:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -r_A = -k\,C_A^n$$

| Order $n$ | Differential Form | Integrated Form | Half-Life $t_{1/2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | $-\dfrac{dC_A}{dt}=k$ | $C_A = C_{A0} - k\,t$ | $\dfrac{C_{A0}}{2k}$ |
| **1** | $-\dfrac{dC_A}{dt}=k\,C_A$ | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) = k\,t$ | $\dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ |
| **2** | $-\dfrac{dC_A}{dt}=k\,C_A^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{C_A} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}} = k\,t$ | $\dfrac{1}{k\,C_{A0}}$ |
| **$n$** | $-\dfrac{dC_A}{dt}=k\,C_A^n$ | $\dfrac{1}{n-1}\!\left(C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n}\right) = k\,t$ | $\dfrac{2^{n-1}-1}{(n-1)\,k\,C_{A0}^{n-1}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Half-life behaviour** is a powerful diagnostic for order determination in KTU problems:
> - $t_{1/2}$ independent of $C_{A0}$ → **first order**
> - $t_{1/2} \propto 1/C_{A0}$ → **second order**
> - $t_{1/2} \propto C_{A0}$ → **zero order**

## 2.5 Methods of Analysing Experimental Data

### 2.5.1 Integral Method
1. **Assume** an order $n$.
2. **Integrate** the rate law.
3. **Plot** the appropriate concentration function vs. $t$ — if linear, the assumed order is correct.

### 2.5.2 Differential Method
1. Plot $C_A$ vs. $t$ and **measure slopes** $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt}$ at several points.
2. Compute $-r_A = -\dfrac{dC_A}{dt}$ at each point.
3. Take logarithms: $\ln(-r_A) = \ln k + n \ln C_A$.
4. Plot $\ln(-r_A)$ vs. $\ln C_A$ → slope = $n$, intercept = $\ln k$.

### 2.5.3 Half-Life Method
Using $t_{1/2} \propto C_{A0}^{1-n}$ for $n \ne 1$, plot $\ln t_{1/2}$ vs. $\ln C_{A0}$ → slope = $1-n$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet for KTU End-Semester Examination (Module 1)**

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-r_A = k\,C_A^n\,C_B^m$ | General power-law rate expression |
| 2 | $k = A\,\exp(-E/RT)$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence |
| 3 | $\ln(k_2/k_1) = \dfrac{E}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-temperature Arrhenius |
| 4 | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$ | Linear form — plot $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ |
| 5 | $C_A = C_{A0} - k\,t$ (0th order) | Integrated form, $n=0$ |
| 6 | $C_A = C_{A0}\exp(-k\,t)$ (1st order) | Integrated form, $n=1$ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{C_A} = \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}} + k\,t$ (2nd order) | Integrated form, $n=2$ |
| 8 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Half-life, 1st order |
| 9 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k\,C_{A0}}$ | Half-life, 2nd order |
| 10 | $X = \dfrac{C_{A0} - C_A}{C_{A0}}$ | Conversion of A |
| 11 | $t = C_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ | Batch time for given $X$ |
| 12 | $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ | PFR volume for given $X$ |
| 13 | $V = \dfrac{F_{A0}\,X}{-r_A}\big\vert_{X}$ | CSTR volume for given $X$ |
| 14 | $r_{\text{overall}} = -\dfrac{1}{\nu_A}\dfrac{dC_A}{dt}$ | Reaction rate from species |
| 15 | $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A$ (PFR/Batch) | ODE form (constant $V$) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Sizing:** The integrated rate law gives the residence time $\tau$ needed for a target conversion — directly used in designing commercial reactors.
- **Process Safety:** The strong temperature dependence of $k$ (Arrhenius) underlies **thermal runaway** analysis in exothermic reactions.
- **Process Optimisation:** The order of reaction governs how sensitive conversion is to concentration changes — used to choose between CSTR, PFR, or recycle configurations.
- **Process Control:** The half-life and time-constant data feed directly into **PID controller tuning** for batch reactors.
- **Catalyst Development:** Activation energy $E$ is a key metric for comparing catalysts — a good catalyst *lowers* $E$ without changing $A$ significantly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Integrated First-Order Rate Law (Constant-Volume Batch)

**Starting point — the species balance on a constant-volume batch reactor:**

For a closed system with no inflow/outflow, the mole balance on species A is:

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A\,V$$

Since $V$ is constant, $N_A = C_A V$, so $V\,dC_A/dt = r_A V$, i.e.:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A$$

The rate of disappearance is $-r_A = k\,C_A$ (first-order), so:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k\,C_A$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $t=0$, $C_A = C_{A0}$ to $t$, $C_A = C_A$:**

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k\int_0^t dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate both integrals:**

$$\ln C_A \big\vert_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -k\,t$$

$$\ln C_A - \ln C_{A0} = -k\,t$$

**Step 4 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) = k\,t \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad C_A = C_{A0}\,\exp(-k\,t)\;}$$

**Sanity checks:**
- At $t=0$: $C_A = C_{A0}\exp(0) = C_{A0}$ ✓
- As $t \to \infty$: $C_A \to 0$ (complete conversion) ✓
- Half-life: $C_A = C_{A0}/2$ gives $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Integrated Second-Order Rate Law

**Starting ODE (second order, single reactant):**

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k\,C_A^2$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $C_{A0}$ to $C_A$:**

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} C_A^{-2}\,dC_A = -k\int_0^t dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate (recall $\int x^{-2}dx = -x^{-1}$):**

$$\left[-\frac{1}{C_A}\right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -k\,t$$

$$-\frac{1}{C_A} + \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = -k\,t$$

**Step 4 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k\,t\;}$$

**Half-life derivation:** Set $C_A = C_{A0}/2$:

$$\frac{2}{C_{A0}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k\,t_{1/2} \;\Longrightarrow\; t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k\,C_{A0}}$$

This explicitly shows $t_{1/2} \propto 1/C_{A0}$ — the diagnostic signature of a **second-order** reaction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Arrhenius Two-Temperature Form

**Start from the Arrhenius equation evaluated at two temperatures:**

$$k_1 = A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{R\,T_1}\right) \quad\text{and}\quad k_2 = A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{R\,T_2}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Take the ratio (eliminates $A$):**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{\exp(-E/RT_2)}{\exp(-E/RT_1)} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite the sign:**

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\;}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-typical problem):**
Given $E = 80{,}000$ J/mol, $T_1 = 600$ K, $T_2 = 700$ K, find $k_2/k_1$.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{80{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{600} - \frac{1}{700}\right)$$

$$= 9622.0 \times (1.6667 \times 10^{-3} - 1.4286 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$= 9622.0 \times 2.3810 \times 10^{-4} = 2.291$$

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{2.291} \approx 9.88$$

**Interpretation:** Raising the temperature from 600 K to 700 K makes the reaction **nearly 10 times faster** — a vivid illustration of the strong temperature sensitivity of reaction rates.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Conversion Form of the Batch Reactor Design Equation

**Start with the mole balance and substitute conversion:**

The conversion of A is defined as:

$$X = \frac{N_{A0} - N_A}{N_{A0}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N_A = N_{A0}(1-X)$$

For a constant-volume system, $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$ and $dC_A = -C_{A0}\,dX$.

The mole balance $dC_A/dt = -r_A$ becomes:

$$-C_{A0}\frac{dX}{dt} = -r_A \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{-r_A}{C_{A0}}$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables:**

$$dt = C_{A0}\,\frac{dX}{-r_A}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $X=0$ at $t=0$ to $X$ at time $t$:**

$$\boxed{\;t = C_{A0}\int_0^{X} \frac{dX}{-r_A}\;}$$

**Application — first-order in a batch reactor:**

$$-r_A = k\,C_A = k\,C_{A0}(1-X)$$

$$t = C_{A0}\int_0^{X} \frac{dX}{k\,C_{A0}(1-X)} = \frac{1}{k}\int_0^{X} \frac{dX}{1-X} = -\frac{1}{k}\ln(1-X)$$

$$\boxed{\;t = -\frac{\ln(1-X)}{k}\;}$$

This is the **batch reactor design equation for a first-order reaction** — heavily tested in KTU papers.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Generalised $n^{\text{th}}$-Order Integrated Law

**Start:** $-r_A = k\,C_A^n$, with $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$.

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{k\,C_{A0}^{n}(1-X)^{n}}{C_{A0}} = k\,C_{A0}^{n-1}(1-X)^{n}$$

Separate and integrate:

$$t = \frac{1}{k\,C_{A0}^{n-1}}\int_0^{X} \frac{dX}{(1-X)^{n}}$$

Using $\displaystyle\int \frac{dX}{(1-X)^{n}} = \frac{(1-X)^{1-n}}{n-1}$ for $n \ne 1$:

$$t = \frac{1}{k\,C_{A0}^{n-1}}\left[\frac{(1-X)^{1-n} - 1}{n-1}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\;t = \frac{(1-X)^{1-n} - 1}{(n-1)\,k\,C_{A0}^{n-1}}\quad (n \ne 1)\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence check:** As $X \to 1$ (full conversion), $t \to \infty$ for $n < 1$ (rate does not slow down) and $t \to$ a finite value for $n > 1$ (rate slows as reactants are consumed). For $n = 1$, recover $t = -\ln(1-X)/k$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm for Determining Reaction Order

| Step | Action | Diagnostic Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Measure $C_A$ vs. $t$ from batch experiment | Tabulate data |
| 2 | Compute $-r_A = -\Delta C_A / \Delta t$ at each $t$ | Get $r$ values |
| 3 | Compute $\ln C_A$ and $\ln(-r_A)$ | Linearise |
| 4 | Plot $\ln(-r_A)$ vs. $\ln C_A$ | Slope = $n$ |
| 5 | If slope ≈ 0, 1, 2 → zero, first, second order | Confirm |
| 6 | If non-integer → empirical $n^{\text{th}}$ order | Report $n$ |
| 7 | From intercept, $k = \exp(\text{intercept})$ | Compute $k$ |
| 8 | Repeat at another $T$, fit Arrhenius | Compute $E$, $A$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always plot *and* report the slope + intercept; do not skip the table of $C_A$, $t$, $\ln C_A$, $\ln(-r_A)$ — marks are awarded for showing the complete data reduction.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ is carried out in a constant-volume batch reactor. Initial concentration $C_{A0} = 2.0$ mol/L. After 10 min, $C_A = 0.5$ mol/L. Determine (a) the rate constant $k$, and (b) the time for 75% conversion.

**Solution (a):** Use integrated first-order law:

$$k = \frac{1}{t}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) = \frac{1}{10}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{2.0}{0.5}\right) = \frac{\ln 4}{10} = \frac{1.3863}{10} = 0.1386\;\text{min}^{-1}$$

**Solution (b):** For $X = 0.75$, $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X) = 2.0 \times 0.25 = 0.5$ mol/L.

$$t = -\frac{\ln(1-X)}{k} = -\frac{\ln(0.25)}{0.1386} = \frac{1.3863}{0.1386} = 10\;\text{min}$$

(As expected — 75% conversion gives $C_A = 0.5$, same as the data point.)

**Solution (c) — half-life:** $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k = 0.693/0.1386 = 5.0$ min.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that reaching 75% conversion takes exactly **two half-lives** — a defining property of first-order reactions. This is a classic KTU concept-test question.

---

## 3.8 Construction of ODE Models from Rate Laws (Module-1 Bridge to Simulation)

The rate law becomes an **ODE source term**. For a constant-volume batch reactor:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k(T)\,C_A^{\,n}\;}$$

For a non-isothermal batch, the energy balance closes the system:

$$\boxed{\;\rho\,C_p\,\frac{dT}{dt} = -\Delta H_r\,(-r_A)\,V - U\,A\,(T - T_{\text{cool}})\;}$$

The **simulation task** (introduced in PEPOT527) is to integrate this coupled ODE system numerically. Common methods:
- **Euler explicit** (introductory, Module 2)
- **Runge–Kutta 4th order (RK4)** (standard workhorse, Module 3)
- **Stiff solvers** (BDF, implicit) for systems with widely separated time scales (Module 4)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mapping:** Module 1 supplies the **physical model** (the rate law). Modules 2–4 supply the **numerical methods** to solve the resulting ODEs. Module 5 supplies **parameter estimation** (fitting $k$, $E$, $A$ to data).

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow Diagram — From Mechanism to Process Model

This mermaid diagram maps the conceptual flow of how chemical kinetics fits into process modelling and simulation, exactly as introduced in Module 1 of PEPOT527.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Definition] --> B[Stoichiometry]
    B --> C[Propose Reaction Mechanism]
    C --> D[Select Rate Law Form]
    D --> E[Determine Order and k from Data]
    E --> F[Fit Arrhenius Parameters E and A]
    F --> G[Formulate Rate Expression]
    G --> H[Couple with Mass and Energy Balances]
    H --> I[Set Up ODE Model of Reactor]
    I --> J[Apply Numerical Solver]
    J --> K[Validate Against Plant Data]
    K --> L[Final Process Model]
    K --> M[Refine Parameters]
    M --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Start at node **A** (process definition) and progress downward.
- The **feedback loop** (M → E) represents **iterative parameter refinement** — a key concept in PEPOT527.
- Node **G** is the *deliverable* of Module 1; node **J** is the *deliverable* of Module 3.

---

## 4.2 Algorithm Diagram — Determining Reaction Order from Experimental Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start with Concentration vs Time Data] --> B[Compute Slopes to Obtain rA]
    B --> C[Take Natural Log of rA and CA]
    C --> D[Plot ln rA versus ln CA]
    D --> E{Is the Plot Linear?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Slope gives Order n]
    E -->|No| G[Try Other Rate Law Forms]
    F --> H[Intercept gives ln k]
    H --> I[Compute k and Report Order]
    G --> A
    I --> J[Repeat at Other Temperatures]
    J --> K[Plot ln k versus 1 over T]
    K --> L[Slope gives E over R]
    L --> M[Compute Activation Energy E and A]
    M --> N[Arrhenius Model Complete]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **decision diamond** (E) is the diagnostic test for the power-law model.
- Node **J** initiates the **temperature sweep** that yields Arrhenius parameters.
- Node **N** is the *terminal* state — a fully parameterised kinetic model.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Kinetic Modelling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        I1[Concentration Data]
        I2[Temperature Data]
        I3[Time Data]
    end

    subgraph ModelEngine
        M1[Rate Law Module]
        M2[Arrhenius Module]
        M3[ODE Solver Module]
    end

    subgraph Output
        O1[Rate Constant k]
        O2[Activation Energy E]
        O3[Predicted Concentration Profile]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M2
    I3 --> M1
    I3 --> M3
    M1 --> O3
    M2 --> O2
    M3 --> O3
    M1 --> O1
    M2 --> O1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left sub-graph (Input)** is the experimental data layer.
- The **centre sub-graph (ModelEngine)** is where the kinetic model is implemented in simulation software.
- The **right sub-graph (Output)** is the *deliverable* — a fully parameterised, simulation-ready kinetic model.

---

## 4.4 Concept Map — Order of Reaction vs. Diagnostic Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Assume Reaction Order n] --> B{Which Order?}
    B -->|n = 0| C0[Plot CA vs t]
    B -->|n = 1| C1[Plot ln CA vs t]
    B -->|n = 2| C2[Plot 1/CA vs t]
    B -->|n general| CG[Plot 1 over CA to the n-1 vs t]
    C0 --> D0{Linear?}
    C1 --> D1{Linear?}
    C2 --> D2{Linear?}
    CG --> DG{Linear?}
    D0 -->|Yes| E0[Confirmed n = 0, slope equals negative k]
    D0 -->|No| A
    D1 -->|Yes| E1[Confirmed n = 1, slope equals negative k]
    D1 -->|No| A
    D2 -->|Yes| E2[Confirmed n = 2, slope equals k]
    D2 -->|No| A
    DG -->|Yes| EG[Confirmed order n, slope equals k]
    DG -->|No| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The "test-and-iterate" structure (the loop from decision back to node A) reflects the empirical, hypothesis-driven nature of kinetic parameter estimation — a concept that is fundamental to PEPOT527's approach.
