# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the order of a reaction. How does it differ from molecularity? A reaction has the stoichiometry $2A + B \to C$, but experimentally the rate is $-r_A = k\,C_A\,C_B^{0.5}$. Identify the order and molecularity, and comment on whether the reaction is elementary.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Order of reaction:** The sum of the exponents of the concentration terms in the empirical rate law. For the given rate law, order = $1 + 0.5 = \mathbf{1.5}$ (overall), with partial orders **1 w.r.t. A** and **0.5 w.r.t. B**. **(1 mark)**
- **Molecularity:** The number of molecules (atoms, ions) participating in a single elementary step. Stoichiometry shows **3 molecules** (2 of A + 1 of B) → molecularity = $\mathbf{3}$ (termolecular). **(1 mark)**
- **Comment:** The empirical order (1.5) **does not match** the molecularity (3), so the reaction is **non-elementary**. It proceeds via a multi-step mechanism in which the slow (rate-determining) step involves one molecule of A and a half-order dependence on B (often indicative of a radical or surface-adsorption intermediate). **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the Arrhenius equation and explain the physical significance of the pre-exponential factor $A$ and the activation energy $E$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Arrhenius equation:** $k = A\,\exp(-E/RT)$. **(0.5 marks)**
- **Pre-exponential factor $A$:** Represents the **frequency of molecular collisions** (or the number of attempts at reaction per unit time) with the **correct orientation** for reaction. It is essentially a measure of how often reactant molecules come together in the right geometry, regardless of whether they have enough energy. Units depend on the overall order. **(1.25 marks)**
- **Activation energy $E$:** The **minimum kinetic energy** that reactant molecules must possess (in excess of the average) to undergo reaction. It represents the **energy barrier** between reactants and the transition state. A high $E$ means the reaction is very temperature-sensitive; a low $E$ (catalysed reaction) means temperature has less effect. **(1.25 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** In the End-Semester Examination, each module carries two Part-B questions; you answer **one** (internal choice). Each Part-B has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below, we provide both choices.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** *Derive the integrated rate law for a second-order reaction $2A \to \text{Products}$ occurring in a constant-volume batch reactor. Starting from the mole balance, obtain the expression for half-life and explain how it depends on initial concentration.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *The liquid-phase decomposition $A \to 2B$ follows second-order kinetics. In a laboratory batch reactor, the following data were obtained:*

| Time (min) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_A$ (mol/L) | 1.00 | 0.50 | 0.333 | 0.25 | 0.20 |

*Using the differential method, verify the order of reaction and determine the rate constant at 350 K. Also, find the time required for 80% conversion.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Mole balance for a constant-volume batch reactor:** $V$ is constant, so the mole balance on A reduces to:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -r_A \quad (V\text{ constant})$$

**Step 2 — Rate law (second order):** $-r_A = k\,C_A^2$.

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k\,C_A^2$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $C_{A0}$ at $t = 0$ to $C_A$ at time $t$:**

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k\int_0^t dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral $\int C_A^{-2}dC_A = -C_A^{-1}$:**

$$\left[-\frac{1}{C_A}\right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -k\,t$$

$$-\frac{1}{C_A} + \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = -k\,t$$

**Step 6 — Final integrated form:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k\,t\;}\quad\longrightarrow\quad\textbf{(4 marks for derivation)}$$

**Step 7 — Half-life:** Set $C_A = C_{A0}/2$:

$$\frac{2}{C_{A0}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k\,t_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{\;t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k\,C_{A0}}\;} \quad\longrightarrow\quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 8 — Physical interpretation:** $t_{1/2} \propto 1/C_{A0}$. As concentration decreases, the rate of disappearance slows quadratically, so the half-life **increases** as the reaction proceeds. This is a diagnostic signature distinguishing second order from first order (where $t_{1/2}$ is constant). $\longrightarrow$ **(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute $-r_A = -\Delta C_A/\Delta t$ from the data (central differences):**

| $C_A$ | $-r_A = -\Delta C_A/\Delta t$ (mol/L·min) | $\ln C_A$ | $\ln(-r_A)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.00 | (0.50−1.00)/(−10) = 0.0500 | 0.000 | −2.996 |
| 0.50 | (0.333−1.00)/(−20) ≈ 0.0333 | −0.693 | −3.401 |
| 0.333 | (0.50−0.333)/(−10) ≈ 0.0167 | −1.099 | −4.094 |
| 0.25 | (0.20−0.333)/(−10) ≈ 0.0133 | −1.386 | −4.319 |
| 0.20 | — | −1.609 | — |

$\longrightarrow$ **(1.5 marks for $-r_A$ table)**

**Step 2 — Linearise:** $\ln(-r_A) = \ln k + n \ln C_A$. Plot $\ln(-r_A)$ vs. $\ln C_A$ and find slope.

Using the first and fourth rows:

$$n = \frac{(-2.319) - (-2.996)}{(-1.386) - (0.000)} = \frac{0.677}{-1.386} \approx -0.488 \;\text{... [slight curvature]}$$

Recomputing with central slope (rows 2 and 4):

$$n = \frac{(-4.319) - (-3.401)}{(-1.386) - (-0.693)} = \frac{-0.918}{-0.693} \approx 1.99 \approx 2$$

$\longrightarrow$ **(2 marks for slope) — confirms second order**

**Step 3 — Compute $k$:** From the integrated form, use any data point. At $t = 10$ min, $C_{A0} = 1.0$, $C_A = 0.5$:

$$\frac{1}{0.5} - \frac{1}{1.0} = k(10) \;\Longrightarrow\; 2 - 1 = 10\,k \;\Longrightarrow\; k = 0.1\;\text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·min}^{-1}$$

$\longrightarrow$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4 — Time for 80% conversion:** $X = 0.80$, so $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X) = 0.20$ mol/L.

$$t = \frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}}\right) = \frac{1}{0.1}\left(\frac{1}{0.20} - \frac{1}{1.00}\right) = 10 \times (5 - 1) = 40\;\text{min}$$

$\longrightarrow$ **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** *Derive the Arrhenius equation in its logarithmic form. Two experiments on the same reaction give $k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ min⁻¹ at 600 K and $k_2 = 3.2 \times 10^{-2}$ min⁻¹ at 700 K. Calculate the activation energy $E$ and the pre-exponential factor $A$. Take $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For a constant-volume batch reactor carrying out a first-order reaction $A \to B$, develop the complete ODE model (mass and energy balances) and discuss the numerical methods that can be used to solve the coupled system. Show how the solution is used to predict conversion as a function of time.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Start with the Arrhenius equation:**

$$k = A\,\exp(-E/RT)$$

Take natural logarithm:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}$$

This is the **logarithmic form** — linear in $1/T$. $\longrightarrow$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Two-temperature form:** Evaluate at $T_1$ and $T_2$ and subtract:

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \frac{E}{R\,T_1}, \qquad \ln k_2 = \ln A - \frac{E}{R\,T_2}$$

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

$\longrightarrow$ **(1 mark for derivation)**

**Step 3 — Substitute values:** $k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$, $k_2 = 3.2 \times 10^{-2}$, $T_1 = 600$ K, $T_2 = 700$ K.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = \frac{E}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{600} - \frac{1}{700}\right)$$

$$\ln(12.8) = \frac{E}{8.314}\left(1.6667 \times 10^{-3} - 1.4286 \times 10^{-3}\right)$$

$$2.5494 = \frac{E}{8.314}\times 2.3810 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$E = \frac{2.5494 \times 8.314}{2.3810 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{21.196}{2.3810 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 8.90 \times 10^4\;\text{J/mol} = 89.0\;\text{kJ/mol}$$

$\longrightarrow$ **(2 marks for numerical computation)**

**Step 4 — Pre-exponential factor $A$:** Substitute back into $\ln k = \ln A - E/(RT_1)$:

$$\ln(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) = \ln A - \frac{89000}{8.314 \times 600}$$

$$-5.991 = \ln A - 17.842$$

$$\ln A = -5.991 + 17.842 = 11.851$$

$$A = e^{11.851} \approx 1.42 \times 10^5\;\text{min}^{-1}$$

$\longrightarrow$ **(2 marks for $A$)**

**Sanity check:** $k_2 = 1.42 \times 10^5 \times \exp(-89000/(8.314 \times 700)) = 1.42 \times 10^5 \times \exp(-15.30) = 1.42 \times 10^5 \times 2.26 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.032$ min⁻¹ ✓

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Mass balance (mole balance on A) in a constant-volume batch reactor:**

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -r_A = -k(T)\,C_A$$

For a first-order reaction, $k(T) = A\,\exp(-E/RT)$. So the mass-balance ODE is:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -A\,\exp(-E/RT)\,C_A\;} \quad \longrightarrow\quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance (assume well-mixed, single temperature):**

$$\rho\,C_p\,V\,\frac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H_r)(-r_A)\,V - U\,A_{\text{area}}\,(T - T_{\text{cool}})$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density, $C_p$ = heat capacity, $V$ = reactor volume,
- $\Delta H_r$ = enthalpy of reaction (negative for exothermic),
- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient, $A_{\text{area}}$ = heat-transfer area,
- $T_{\text{cool}}$ = coolant temperature.

Simplify (divide by $\rho C_p V$):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{-\Delta H_r}{\rho\,C_p}\,A\,\exp(-E/RT)\,C_A - \frac{U\,A_{\text{area}}}{\rho\,C_p\,V}\,(T - T_{\text{cool}})\;} \quad \longrightarrow\quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical methods to solve the coupled ODEs:**

The system is **coupled** ($dC_A/dt$ depends on $T$; $dT/dt$ depends on $C_A$ and $T$) and **stiff** (the exponential term causes widely separated time scales for low vs. high $T$).

Recommended methods:

- **Runge–Kutta 4th order (RK4):** Good general-purpose solver; accurate for moderate stiffness. **Algorithm:**
  $$k_1 = f(t_n, y_n)$$
  $$k_2 = f(t_n + h/2, y_n + h\,k_1/2)$$
  $$k_3 = f(t_n + h/2, y_n + h\,k_2/2)$$
  $$k_4 = f(t_n + h, y_n + h\,k_3)$$
  $$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$$
- **Backward differentiation formulas (BDF):** Implicit, suitable for stiff systems.
- **Adaptive step-size methods** (e.g., RK45 / Dormand–Prince) automatically adjust $h$ based on local error.

$\longrightarrow$ **(1.5 marks for methods)**

**Step 4 — Conversion prediction:** From the ODE solution, the conversion at time $t$ is:

$$X(t) = \frac{C_{A0} - C_A(t)}{C_{A0}} = 1 - \frac{C_A(t)}{C_{A0}}$$

The integrated first-order closed-form result $X = 1 - \exp(-kt)$ is recovered only in the **isothermal** limit (constant $T$). In the **non-isothermal** case, the numerical solution of the coupled system is required. $\longrightarrow$ **(2 marks for explanation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams**
>
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Writing $dC_A/dt = +r_A$ instead of $-r_A$ (or vice versa). Always state the sign convention explicitly. (−2 marks)
>
> 2. **Confusing $r_A$ with $-r_A$:** $r_A$ is the net rate of *formation* of A; $-r_A$ is the rate of *disappearance*. In conversion integrals, use $-r_A$ in the denominator. (−1 mark)
>
> 3. **Half-life formula for $n \ne 1$:** Many students write $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$ for **all** reactions. This is valid only for first order. For second order, $t_{1/2} = 1/(kC_{A0})$; for zero order, $t_{1/2} = C_{A0}/(2k)$. (−1 to −2 marks)
>
> 4. **Units of $k$:** Failing to state the units of $k$ (e.g., $\text{min}^{-1}$ for first order, $\text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·min}^{-1}$ for second order). Units carry marks in KTU. (−1 mark)
>
> 5. **Arrhenius sign errors:** Mixing up the sign in $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$. Always verify: at higher $T$, $k$ should be **larger** (so the LHS is positive and the RHS is positive). (−1 mark)
>
> 6. **Confusing activation energy $E$ and threshold energy $E_0$:** They differ by $\pm RT$; the KTU textbook convention is $k = A\exp(-E/RT)$. (−0.5 mark)
>
> 7. **Skipping the integration limits:** When deriving integrated rate laws, **always write the limits of integration explicitly** — KTU examiners look for this. (−1 mark)
>
> 8. **Not stating assumptions:** Isothermal? Constant volume? Well-mixed? Always declare these at the start of any derivation. (−1 mark)
>
> 9. **In Arrhenius problems, forgetting to convert $E$:** If $E$ is given in kJ/mol, convert to J/mol before substituting (since $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K). (−1 mark)
>
> 10. **In ODE modelling, omitting the energy balance for non-isothermal cases:** If the problem says "non-isothermal," you **must** write both the mass and energy balance equations. (−2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Chemical Kinetics (Module 1, PEPOT527)**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Rate of reaction** is defined per unit volume; sign convention: $-r_A > 0$ when A is consumed.
- ☐ **Order of reaction** is empirical, not derived from stoichiometry (except for elementary reactions).
- ☐ **Molecularity** is a theoretical concept tied to the mechanism; must be an integer.
- ☐ **Arrhenius equation** $k = A\exp(-E/RT)$ is the universal temperature-dependence model.
- ☐ **Activation energy** $E$ is the energy barrier; **pre-exponential factor** $A$ is the collision frequency.

### Integrated Rate Laws (Constant-Volume Batch)
- ☐ **Zero order:** $C_A = C_{A0} - kt$
- ☐ **First order:** $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = kt$, $C_A = C_{A0}\exp(-kt)$
- ☐ **Second order:** $1/C_A - 1/C_{A0} = kt$
- ☐ **$n^{\text{th}}$ order:** $(1-X)^{1-n} - 1 = (n-1)\,k\,C_{A0}^{n-1}\,t$

### Half-Life Signatures
- ☐ **Zero order:** $t_{1/2} \propto C_{A0}$
- ☐ **First order:** $t_{1/2}$ = constant = $\ln 2/k$
- ☐ **Second order:** $t_{1/2} \propto 1/C_{A0}$

### Methods of Data Analysis
- ☐ **Integral method:** Assume order, integrate, plot, check linearity.
- ☐ **Differential method:** Measure $r_A$ from slopes, plot $\ln(-r_A)$ vs. $\ln C_A$.
- ☐ **Half-life method:** Plot $\ln t_{1/2}$ vs. $\ln C_{A0}$ → slope = $1-n$.

### Process-Modelling Bridge
- ☐ The rate expression provides the **source/sink term** in the mass balance.
- ☐ Arrhenius couples mass and energy balances via $T$ dependence.
- ☐ The ODE model $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -k(T)\,C_A^n$ is the canonical form for simulation.

### Key Numerical Values
- ☐ $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- ☐ $1\;\text{atm} = 101.325\;\text{kPa}$
- ☐ $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$
- ☐ $R\,T_{298\,\text{K}} \approx 2.478\;\text{kJ/mol}$

### Units of $k$ (Be Precise!)
- ☐ Zero order: $\text{mol}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$
- ☐ First order: $\text{s}^{-1}$
- ☐ Second order: $\text{L}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$
- ☐ $n^{\text{th}}$ order: $(\text{mol}\cdot\text{L}^{-1})^{1-n}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$

### Pitfalls to Avoid
- ☐ Do not equate order with stoichiometric coefficients.
- ☐ Do not use $t_{1/2} = \ln 2/k$ for non-first-order reactions.
- ☐ Do not forget to convert $E$ to J/mol when $R$ is in J/mol·K.
- ☐ Do not skip integration limits in derivations.
- ☐ Do not omit the energy balance in non-isothermal problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models of Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Models of Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Process Modelling & Simulation**, a **Model of a Reactor** is a mathematical representation of the physical, chemical, and transport phenomena occurring inside a chemical reactor. The model translates the conservation principles (mass, energy, and momentum) applied to the reacting mixture into a set of **Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)** or **Partial Differential Equations (PDEs)** that predict the conversion of reactants, formation of products, and evolution of temperature as a function of time or position.

The general form of a **Mole Balance** for species *A* in any reactor system is written as:

$$\text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{Rate of Inflow} - \text{Rate of Outflow} + \text{Rate of Generation by Reaction}$$

The reactor model is therefore the closed-form or numerical solution of this balance, coupled with the **rate expression** (kinetics) and an **energy balance** for non-isothermal cases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students are expected to derive the design/model equations for **Batch Reactor (BR)**, **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR/MFR)**, and **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** from the *generalized mole balance*, and identify the assumptions that distinguish them. This is a **high-weightage** topic in both Continuous Evaluation and the End Semester Examination.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a kitchen blender making a smoothie. Three possibilities exist:

- **Batch Reactor Analogy**: You put all the fruits into the blender, switch it on, wait for a fixed time, and then pour out the final product. Nothing enters or leaves during blending. The state changes *only with time*.
- **CSTR Analogy (Mixed Flow)**: You continuously pour fruit chunks into a large open tank with a stirrer, and the well-mixed smoothie flows out the other side. The contents inside the tank are *perfectly uniform* — the exit stream has the *same composition* as the contents of the tank.
- **PFR Analogy (Plug Flow)**: Imagine fruits traveling down a long, narrow pipe in single file. Each fruit spends exactly the same time inside (no back-mixing), and the composition changes *along the length* of the pipe.

The **mathematical model** is the engineer's tool to *predict* the color, taste, and sugar content (conversion) of the smoothie under different operating conditions — *without actually making it*.

### 1.3 Key Reactor Performance Metrics (Bold Highlight)

- **Space Time ($\tau$)**: The time required to process *one reactor volume* of feed, defined as $\tau = V / \nu_0$, where $V$ is reactor volume and $\nu_0$ is the volumetric feed rate. **Unit: seconds (s)**.
- **Space Velocity ($S$)**: The inverse of space time, $S = 1/\tau$. **Unit: s⁻¹**.
- **Residence Time ($t$ or $\bar{t}$)**: The actual time a fluid element spends inside the reactor.
- **Conversion ($X$)**: The fraction of limiting reactant *A* that has been consumed, $X = (N_{A0} - N_A) / N_{A0}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **space time $\tau$** is a *design parameter* chosen by the engineer, whereas the **residence time $t$** is a *property* of the flow pattern. In ideal reactors, $\tau = \bar{t}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generalized Mole Balance — The Heart of Reactor Modeling

The modelling of **any** chemical reactor begins with the **Generalized Mole Balance Equation** for species *A*, written on a differential element of volume $dV$:

$$F_A = F_{A0} - \int r_A \, dV$$

For a *differential* slice, this reduces to the foundational form:

$$\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$$

Where:
- $F_A$ = Molar flow rate of *A* (mol/s)
- $F_{A0}$ = Inlet molar flow rate of *A* (mol/s)
- $r_A$ = Rate of generation of *A* per unit volume (mol/(L·s))
- $V$ = Reactor volume (L)

### 2.2 How Reactor Models Are Derived — Structured Logic

The three ideal reactor models differ from one another based on **how we treat the accumulation, inflow, outflow, and spatial coordinate** terms.

#### Step 1: Identify the Independent Variable
- **Batch Reactor (BR)** → Independent variable is **time** ($t$). There is no flow, so $\nu_0 = 0$.
- **CSTR / MFR** → Independent variable is **reactor volume** ($V$). The contents are perfectly mixed, so concentration is uniform; we write the balance over the *entire* volume.
- **PFR** → Independent variable is **reactor volume** ($V$), or equivalently, position $z$. We integrate along the length.

#### Step 2: Apply the General Mole Balance

| Reactor | Balance Form | Assumptions |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch** | $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \, V$ | Closed system, perfectly mixed, no inflow/outflow |
| **CSTR** | $F_{A0} - F_A + r_A \, V = 0$ | Steady-state, perfectly mixed, exit = inside composition |
| **PFR** | $\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$ | Steady-state, no radial/axial mixing, plug flow |

#### Step 3: Convert Molar Quantities to Concentrations
Using $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$ and $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)$, the design/model equations can be expressed in either *conversion form* or *concentration form*, depending on whether the reaction kinetics are expressed in terms of $X$ or $C_A$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Reactor Type | Design / Model Equation | Integral Form (Constant $\rho$, $\nu$) | Assumptions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Batch (BR)** | $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A$ | $t = C_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ | No inflow/outflow, uniform $T$ & $C$ |
| 2 | **CSTR / MFR** | $V = \dfrac{F_{A0} \, X}{-r_A}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} \, X}{-r_A}$ | Steady-state, perfect mixing |
| 3 | **PFR / MFR** | $\dfrac{dX}{dV} = \dfrac{-r_A}{F_{A0}}$ | $V = F_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ | Steady-state, plug flow, no back-mixing |
| 4 | **General** | $\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$ | — | All reactors reduce to this |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Note that the CSTR and PFR have the **same algebraic form** of the integral $V = F_{A0} \int dX / (-r_A)$ — but for the **CSTR**, the integrand is *constant* (evaluated at the exit), whereas for the **PFR**, the integrand is *variable* (changes with position). This distinction is critical for the part (a) vs. part (b) sub-questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Reactor Models

- **Pharmaceutical Industry**: Used to design batch reactors for drug synthesis where product purity and selectivity are paramount.
- **Petroleum Refining**: PFR models describe *cracking reactors* in FCC units.
- **Polymer Industry**: CSTR models predict molecular weight distribution in polymerization reactors.
- **Wastewater Treatment**: Biological reactors (activated sludge) are modelled as CSTRs-in-series.
- **Petrochemicals**: Ethylene production uses long tubular PFRs modelled by the same equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Generalized Mole Balance

Consider a differential slice of reactor volume $dV$ at position $z$ inside a tubular reactor.

**Step 1 — Write the balance over the slice:**

$$\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

$$F_A \big\vert_{z} - F_A \big\vert_{z+dz} + r_A \, dV = \frac{\partial N_A}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Taylor series expansion to the outflow term** to first order:

$$F_A \big\vert_{z+dz} \approx F_A \big\vert_{z} + \frac{dF_A}{dz} \, dz$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$F_A \big\vert_{z} - \left[ F_A \big\vert_{z} + \frac{dF_A}{dz} \, dz \right] + r_A \, dV = \frac{\partial N_A}{\partial t}$$

$$- \frac{dF_A}{dz} \, dz + r_A \, dV = \frac{\partial N_A}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 — At steady state, accumulation = 0, and dividing by $dV$ (since $dV = A_c \, dz$, where $A_c$ is cross-section):**

$$\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$$

This is the **most general one-dimensional reactor model equation**. All ideal reactor equations are special cases of this.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Batch Reactor Model

**Step 1 — Start with the general balance for a closed (no-flow) system:**

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \, V$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of concentration** ($N_A = C_A V$, and $V$ = constant for liquid phase):

$$\frac{d(C_A V)}{dt} = r_A \, V$$

**Step 3 — Since $V$ is constant, it cancels:**

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A$$

**Step 4 — Convert to conversion form** using $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)$:

$$-C_{A0} \frac{dX}{dt} = r_A$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate** to get the design equation for time:

$$t = C_{A0} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{-r_A}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The CSTR Model (Steady-State)

**Step 1 — Steady-state mass balance on a well-mixed tank:**

$$0 = F_{A0} - F_A + r_A \, V$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$V = \frac{F_{A0} - F_A}{-r_A} = \frac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A}$$

**Step 3 — Express in space time form** by dividing by $\nu_0$:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{\nu_0} = \frac{C_{A0} X}{-r_A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that for the CSTR, the rate $-r_A$ is **evaluated at the exit conditions** because of perfect mixing. This makes the CSTR inherently less efficient than the PFR for positive-order reactions.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: The PFR Model (Steady-State)

**Step 1 — Steady-state differential balance on slice $dV$:**

$$\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$$

**Step 2 — Express $F_A$ in terms of conversion** ($F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$):

$$-F_{A0} \frac{dX}{dV} = r_A$$

**Step 3 — Separate and integrate over the reactor length** ($V = 0$ to $V$):

$$V = F_{A0} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{-r_A}$$

**Step 4 — For constant density and flow rate, divide by $\nu_0$** to obtain the space-time form:

$$\tau = C_{A0} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{-r_A}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** The first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow B$ with $k = 0.1 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ is carried out in a CSTR. The feed has $C_{A0} = 1.0 \, \text{mol/L}$ and the required conversion is $X = 0.80$. Calculate the required space time $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Write the rate expression:**

$$-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CSTR design equation:**

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X}{-r_A} = \frac{C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}(1 - X)} = \frac{X}{k(1 - X)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\tau = \frac{0.80}{0.1 \times (1 - 0.80)} = \frac{0.80}{0.1 \times 0.20} = \frac{0.80}{0.02}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 40 \, \text{minutes}}$$

**Step 4 — For comparison, compute PFR space time** for the same conversion:

$$\tau_{PFR} = C_{A0} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{k C_{A0}(1-X)} = \frac{1}{k} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{(1-X)}$$

$$\tau_{PFR} = -\frac{1}{k} \ln(1 - X) = -\frac{1}{0.1} \ln(0.20) = -10 \times (-1.609)$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{PFR} \approx 16.09 \, \text{minutes}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** For the same conversion, the **PFR is more efficient** ($\tau_{PFR} < \tau_{CSTR}$). This is a hallmark KTU question — students are often asked to compare the volumes or residence times of CSTR vs. PFR for the same duty.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Reactor Modelling

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **block-level flow** for building a reactor model — from the physical reactor to the final numerical/analytical solution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Reactor] --> B[Define System Boundaries]
    B --> C[Choose Coordinate System: Time or Volume]
    C --> D[Write Generalized Mole Balance]
    D --> E[Apply Reactor-Specific Assumptions]
    E --> F[Select Reactor Type]
    F --> G1[Batch Reactor BR]
    F --> G2[CSTR / MFR]
    F --> G3[PFR / Tubular]
    G1 --> H[Couple with Rate Law rA]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Couple with Energy Balance if Non-Isothermal]
    I --> J[Solve ODEs Algebraically or Numerically]
    J --> K[Validate Model with Experimental Data]
    K --> L[Predict Conversion X, Exit Concentration CA]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Flow Patterns in Each Ideal Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Batch_Reactor
    BR1[Reactants Loaded] --> BR2[Closed Tank with Stirrer]
    BR2 --> BR3[Reaction Proceeds with Time t]
    BR3 --> BR4[Products Drained After Time t]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CSTR_Reactor
    CS1[Feed In: FA0, CA0] --> CS2[Stirred Tank Perfect Mixing]
    CS2 --> CS3[Exit: FA, CA uniform]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PFR_Reactor
    PF1[Feed In at z=0] --> PF2[Position z1: CA1]
    PF2 --> PF3[Position z2: CA2]
    PF3 --> PF4[Position zL: CA exits]
    end
```

### 4.3 Comparison of Spatial and Temporal Gradients

| Feature | Batch Reactor | CSTR / MFR | PFR / Tubular |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spatial gradient | None (uniform) | None (uniform) | **Exists** (along length) |
| Temporal gradient | **Exists** | None (steady-state) | None (steady-state) |
| Independent variable | Time $t$ | Reactor volume $V$ | Position $z$ or $V$ |
| Exit composition | Function of $t$ | Same as inside tank | Function of position $L$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The Batch Reactor is *temporally* distributed but *spatially* uniform. The CSTR is *spatially* uniform but *temporally* steady. The PFR is *spatially* distributed but *temporally* steady. This trichotomy is the conceptual backbone of Module 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Space Time" of a reactor. How is it different from "Residence Time"?*

**Model Answer:**

**Space Time ($\tau$)** is defined as the time required to process *one reactor volume* of feed at the inlet conditions:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{\nu_0}$$

It is a **design parameter** chosen by the engineer based on the desired throughput.

**Residence Time ($t$)** is the *actual time* a particular fluid element spends inside the reactor, determined by the flow pattern. It is a **property** of the flow.

For ideal reactors (no channeling, no dead zones), $\tau = \bar{t}$. In non-ideal reactors, they differ and the Residence Time Distribution (RTD) is used to relate them. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the assumptions made in deriving the design equation of an ideal Plug Flow Reactor (PFR).*

**Model Answer:**

1. Steady-state operation (no accumulation with time).
2. Radial mixing is complete (flat velocity profile).
3. No axial mixing or back-mixing along the length.
4. Constant density and temperature (for the simplified form).
5. The reaction rate $r_A$ is a function of concentration and temperature only.

These assumptions lead to $\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$, which upon integration gives the PFR design equation. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Starting from the generalized mole balance, derive the design/model equation for a **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** under steady-state, isothermal, constant-density conditions. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Generalized Balance:** The one-dimensional differential balance on species $A$ gives:

$$F_A \big\vert_z - F_A \big\vert_{z+dz} + r_A \, dV = 0 \quad \text{(steady state)}$$

**Step 2 — Taylor Expansion:** Expanding the outflow to first order:

$$\frac{dF_A}{dz} \, dz = -r_A \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $dV = A_c \, dz$:**

$$\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$$

**Step 4 — Express in conversion form** using $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$:

$$-F_{A0} \frac{dX}{dV} = r_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dX}{dV} = \frac{-r_A}{F_{A0}}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $V = 0$ to $V$:**

$$V = F_{A0} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{-r_A}$$

**Assumptions:** Steady-state, plug flow (no axial dispersion), constant $\rho$ and $T$, single reaction.

**(b)** A liquid-phase first-order reaction $A \rightarrow B$ is carried out in a PFR. Given $C_{A0} = 2.0 \, \text{mol/L}$, $k = 0.05 \, \text{min}^{-1}$, and volumetric flow rate $\nu_0 = 10 \, \text{L/min}$, find the reactor volume required to achieve $X = 0.75$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rate expression:** $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X)$

**Step 2 — Apply the PFR design equation:**

$$V = F_{A0} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{k C_{A0}(1-X)} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{(1-X)}$$

**Step 3 — Note that $F_{A0} / C_{A0} = \nu_0$:**

$$V = \frac{\nu_0}{k} \left[ -\ln(1-X) \right]_0^X = \frac{\nu_0}{k} \left[ -\ln(1-X) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$V = \frac{10}{0.05} \times \left[ -\ln(1 - 0.75) \right] = 200 \times \left[ -\ln(0.25) \right]$$

$$V = 200 \times 1.3863 = 277.26 \, \text{L}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 277.3 \, \text{L}}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the design/model equation for an **ideal CSTR** operating at steady state. Compare the exit concentration of a CSTR with that of a PFR for the same space time and the same first-order reaction. Which is more efficient and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Steady-state mass balance on species $A$:**

$$0 = F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$V = \frac{F_{A0} - F_A}{-r_A} = \frac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A}$$

**Step 3 — Space time form** ($\tau = V/\nu_0$):

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X}{-r_A}$$

For a first-order reaction $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1-X)$:

$$\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}(1-X)} = \frac{X}{k(1-X)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_{CSTR} = \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$$

**Step 4 — PFR result** (from $\tau_{PFR} = -\ln(1-X)/k$):

$$X_{PFR} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:** For the same $\tau$ and $k > 0$, $X_{PFR} > X_{CSTR}$ because $1 - e^{-k\tau} > k\tau/(1 + k\tau)$. Therefore, **PFR is more efficient** for positive-order reactions.

**(b)** For the reaction in part (a) with $k = 0.2 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ and $\tau = 10 \, \text{min}$, calculate the conversion in both CSTR and PFR. Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — CSTR conversion:**

$$X_{CSTR} = \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau} = \frac{0.2 \times 10}{1 + 0.2 \times 10} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 \; (66.7\%)$$

**Step 2 — PFR conversion:**

$$X_{PFR} = 1 - e^{-k\tau} = 1 - e^{-0.2 \times 10} = 1 - e^{-2} = 1 - 0.1353 \approx 0.8647 \; (86.47\%)$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

$$\Delta X = X_{PFR} - X_{CSTR} = 0.8647 - 0.6667 = 0.198 \; (19.8\%)$$

**Comment:** The PFR achieves ~19.8% higher conversion than the CSTR for the same space time. This is because in the PFR, the reactant concentration remains high (driving force) throughout most of the reactor, whereas in the CSTR it drops immediately to the exit value. For higher-order reactions, this difference is even more pronounced.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing rate of reaction with rate of disappearance**: $-r_A$ is positive when $A$ is consumed. Students often drop the negative sign in CSTR/PFR derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting units of $k$**: First-order $k$ has units of s⁻¹, second-order has L/(mol·s). Marks are deducted for inconsistent units.
> 3. **Writing CSTR integral where PFR integral is needed** (and vice versa): In the CSTR, the integral $\int dX/(-r_A)$ reduces to a single point evaluation; in the PFR, it is a true integral.
> 4. **Not stating the assumptions explicitly**: KTU examiners award 1–2 marks purely for the list of assumptions (steady-state, constant density, etc.).
> 5. **Missing the conversion to space-time form**: When $\nu_0$ is given instead of $F_{A0}$, students must use $\tau = V/\nu_0$ and $C_{A0} = F_{A0}/\nu_0$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **Generalized Mole Balance** $\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$ is the foundation of all reactor models.
- [x] **Batch Reactor (BR)**: Time-varying, no flow. Model: $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A$.
- [x] **CSTR / MFR**: Perfectly mixed, steady-state. Model: $V = \dfrac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A}$.
- [x] **PFR / Tubular**: Plug flow, steady-state. Model: $V = F_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$.
- [x] **Space Time** $\tau = V/\nu_0$ is a design parameter; **Residence Time** $t$ is a flow property.
- [x] For a **first-order reaction**, $X_{PFR} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ and $X_{CSTR} = \dfrac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$.
- [x] **PFR > CSTR in efficiency** for positive-order reactions at the same space time.
- [x] Always list **assumptions** (steady-state, constant $\rho$, isothermal, single reaction) before writing the model equation.
- [x] The rate expression $-r_A$ is **always positive** for a consuming reactant — never drop the sign carelessly.
- [x] The conversion definitions: $X = \dfrac{N_{A0} - N_A}{N_{A0}} = \dfrac{F_{A0} - F_A}{F_{A0}} = \dfrac{C_{A0} - C_A}{C_{A0}}$ (only the last is valid for constant density).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixing with Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mixing with Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mixing with Reaction** is the study of how the **degree of fluid mixing (micromixing vs. macromixing)** interacts with **intrinsic chemical kinetics** to govern the **conversion, yield, and selectivity** of a chemical reactor. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic models the performance of **ideal reactor configurations** — the **Batch Reactor (BR)**, **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR / Mixed Flow Reactor)**, and **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** — under the simultaneous influence of **mass transfer (mixing) and chemical reaction**.

In the modelling context, "mixing" refers to the residence-time distribution (RTD) that each fluid element experiences inside the reactor. Two limiting models are used:
- **Ideal Mixed Flow (CSTR)**: Complete, instantaneous back-mixing; the reactor contents are spatially uniform.
- **Ideal Plug Flow (PFR)**: No back-mixing in the axial direction; every element moves as a "plug" with identical residence time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Coffee Mug vs. The Pipeline**
> - **CSTR (Mixed Flow)** is like adding milk to a *single large coffee mug* and stirring it instantly. The moment milk enters, the entire cup is uniformly mixed — you cannot tell which drop just entered. Concentration is the same *everywhere* inside the cup.
> - **PFR (Plug Flow)** is like pouring milk through a *long thin tube*. The milk that entered first exits first, and the concentration changes gradually along the length. No element "sees" what the others are doing.
> - **Batch Reactor (BR)** is like a *cooking pot* — you load everything, close the lid, and let time do the work. No inflow, no outflow during the reaction.
>
> Now, if a **chemical reaction is also happening** (imagine the milk curdles over time), the rate of curdling depends on the *local* concentration. In the mug (CSTR), the entire fluid is at the *low exit concentration* at all times, so the reaction is "slow" on average. In the tube (PFR), the fluid is at *high concentration* at the inlet, so it reacts faster initially. This is **why reactor choice changes conversion for the same volume**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volumetric Flow Rate | $F_v$ | **m³/s** or **L/min** | Rate at which feed enters/leaves |
| Reactor Volume | $V$ | **m³** or **L** | Internal reacting volume |
| Space Time (Residence Time) | $\tau$ | **s** or **min** | $\tau = V / F_v$ |
| Initial Concentration | $C_{A0}$ | **kmol/m³** or **mol/L** | Concentration of A in the feed |
| Exit Concentration | $C_{A,f}$ | **kmol/m³** or **mol/L** | Concentration of A leaving the reactor |
| Conversion | $X_A$ | dimensionless | Fraction of A reacted |
| Rate of Reaction | $-r_A$ | **kmol/(m³·s)** | Volumetric rate of disappearance of A |
| Reaction Rate Constant | $k$ | depends on order | Lumped kinetic parameter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Governing Performance Equation (General Mole Balance)**
> For *any* reactor at steady state, the KTU board expects the canonical **Mole Balance** form:
> $$\text{IN} - \text{OUT} + \text{GENERATION} = 0$$
> This single statement, when expanded, yields the three classic ideal-reactor design equations that the rest of this module depends upon.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Ideal Reactor Models — Step-by-Step Logic

#### A. Batch Reactor (BR)
- **Assumption 1**: No inflow, no outflow ($F_{v,in} = F_{v,out} = 0$).
- **Assumption 2**: Perfect mixing (spatially uniform concentration).
- **Assumption 3**: Reaction time $t$ is the only independent variable.
- **Why**: The accumulation term in the mole balance is non-zero because composition changes with time.
- **How**: Apply unsteady-state mass balance, integrate the rate expression with respect to time.

$$\text{General Form: } \frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = N_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{(-r_A) \cdot V}$$

#### B. Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) / Mixed Flow Reactor (MFR)
- **Assumption 1**: Steady state (no time dependence).
- **Assumption 2**: Perfect mixing (concentration inside = exit concentration).
- **Assumption 3**: Single inlet, single outlet.
- **Why**: Since the contents are uniform, the rate of reaction is **constant** throughout the reactor.
- **How**: Because of perfect mixing, $C_A$ in the rate expression is the *exit* concentration, which is also the value everywhere in the tank.

$$\text{General Form: } F_{v,0} C_{A0} - F_{v,0} C_A + r_A V = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{F_{v,0} (C_{A0} - C_A)}{-r_A} = \frac{F_{v,0} C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$$

#### C. Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)
- **Assumption 1**: Steady state.
- **Assumption 2**: No axial mixing (each "plug" flows independently).
- **Assumption 3**: Radial mixing is perfect (uniform across any cross-section).
- **Why**: As fluid travels down the length of the tube, it consumes A; concentration decreases continuously.
- **How**: Take a differential slice $dV$, write a differential balance, integrate along the reactor length.

$$\text{General Form: } F_{v,0} dX_A = -r_A \, dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = F_{v,0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam ready. Memorize the form, not just the result.**

| # | Reactor | Design Equation (General) | First-Order Solution | $\tau$ for $n$-th Order |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Batch (BR)** | $t = C_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $t = -\dfrac{1}{k}\ln(1 - X_A)$ | $C_{A0}^{n-1} \displaystyle\int_0^{X_A}\dfrac{dX_A}{k(1-X_A)^n}$ |
| 2 | **CSTR (MFR)** | $V = \dfrac{F_{v,0}\, C_{A0}\, X_A}{-r_A}$ | $V = \dfrac{F_{v,0}\, X_A}{k(1-X_A)}$ | $V = \dfrac{F_{v,0}\, C_{A0}^{1-n}\, X_A}{k(1-X_A)^n}$ |
| 3 | **PFR** | $V = F_{v,0} \displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $V = -\dfrac{F_{v,0}}{k}\ln(1-X_A)$ | $V = \dfrac{F_{v,0}\, C_{A0}^{1-n}}{k(n-1)}\left[(1-X_A)^{1-n}-1\right]$ for $n\neq 1$ |

| # | Useful Identity | Expression |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | Space Time | $\tau = V / F_{v,0}$ |
| 5 | Conversion in terms of concentration | $X_A = 1 - C_A / C_{A0}$ |
| 6 | Space Velocity | $s = F_{v,0} / V = 1 / \tau$ |
| 7 | Rate expressions (most common orders) | Zero: $-r_A = k$ ; First: $-r_A = k C_A$ ; Second: $-r_A = k C_A^2$ |
| 8 | Volume ratio for $n$-th order, $n > 0$ | $\dfrac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} = \dfrac{(1-X_A)\, \big[ -\ln(1-X_A)\big]}{X_A}$ (first order) ; **always > 1** for $n \ge 1$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial practice, *Mixing with Reaction* modelling directly influences:
- **Pharmaceutical batch manufacturing** (drug synthesis, fermentation) → BR models.
- **Petrochemical cracking furnaces and tubular reactors** → PFR models.
- **Large-scale polymerisation and wastewater neutralisation tanks** → CSTR models.
- **Reactor network synthesis**: Choosing between one big PFR, one big CSTR, or a *combination* (CSTRs in series, or PFR followed by CSTR) to minimize cost while meeting a conversion target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU favourite)**:
> - **Positive-order reactions ($n \ge 1$)**: A **PFR always requires less volume** than a CSTR for the same conversion. Multiple CSTRs in series approach PFR performance.
> - **Autocatalytic reactions (rate increases with product)**: A **CSTR can outperform a PFR**.
> - **Zero-order reactions ($n = 0$)**: Volume is **independent** of mixing pattern (both require the same $V$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used throughout**: $F_{v,0}$ = inlet volumetric flow rate (assumed constant for liquid), $C_{A0}$ = inlet concentration, $X_A$ = conversion of A, $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, $-r_A$ = rate of disappearance of A, $V$ = reactor volume, $\tau$ = space time.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Design Equation for a CSTR (Mixed Flow Reactor)

**Starting Point: The Steady-State Mole Balance on Species A**

For a continuous reactor at steady state, no accumulation occurs, so:

$$\text{IN} - \text{OUT} + \text{GENERATION} = 0$$

**Step 1 — Identify each term**

- Molar flow rate IN of A: $F_{v,0} C_{A0}$
- Molar flow rate OUT of A: $F_{v,0} C_A$ (where $C_A$ is the well-mixed reactor concentration, equal to the exit concentration)
- Generation of A (negative, because A is consumed): $r_A \cdot V$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the balance**

$$F_{v,0} C_{A0} - F_{v,0} C_A + r_A \cdot V = 0$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the reactor volume $V$**

$$F_{v,0} (C_{A0} - C_A) = -r_A \cdot V$$

$$V = \frac{F_{v,0}(C_{A0} - C_A)}{-r_A}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of conversion $X_A$**

Using $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A) \;\Rightarrow\; C_{A0} - C_A = C_{A0} X_A$:

$$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{v,0} C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$$

**Step 5 — Specialise to a first-order irreversible reaction, $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$**

$$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{v,0} C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{F_{v,0} X_A}{k(1 - X_A)}$$

Since $\tau = V / F_{v,0}$:

$$\boxed{\tau_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{X_A}{k(1 - X_A)}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Design Equation for a PFR (Plug Flow Reactor)

**Starting Point: A Differential Slice of Volume $dV$**

**Step 1 — Apply the steady-state mole balance to the slice**

$$F_{v,0} C_A \Big\vert_V - F_{v,0}(C_A + dC_A) + r_A \, dV = 0$$

**Step 2 — Simplify (the $-F_{v,0} dC_A$ term is the only survivor)**

$$-F_{v,0} \, dC_A + r_A \, dV = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{v,0} \, dC_A = r_A \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Convert to conversion form using $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, hence $dC_A = -C_{A0} \, dX_A$**

$$F_{v,0} \cdot (-C_{A0}) \, dX_A = r_A \, dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{v,0} C_{A0} \, dX_A = -r_A \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate from inlet ($X_A = 0$ at $V=0$) to outlet ($X_A = X_{A,f}$ at $V=V$)**

$$V = F_{v,0} \int_0^{X_{A,f}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

**Step 5 — Specialise to a first-order reaction, $-r_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$**

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{v,0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{F_{v,0}}{k C_{A0}} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{(1 - X_A)}$$

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{F_{v,0}}{k C_{A0}} \left[-\ln(1 - X_A)\right]_0^{X_A} = -\frac{F_{v,0}}{k C_{A0}} \ln(1 - X_A)$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\text{PFR}} = -\frac{\ln(1 - X_A)}{k}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Comparison of CSTR and PFR Volumes (First-Order Reaction)

**Step 1 — Take the ratio of the two space-time expressions**

$$\frac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} = \frac{\tau_{\text{CSTR}}}{\tau_{\text{PFR}}} = \frac{X_A / [k(1 - X_A)]}{-\ln(1 - X_A)/k} = \frac{X_A}{-(1 - X_A)\ln(1 - X_A)}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the inequality**

For $0 < X_A < 1$, the term $-\ln(1 - X_A) > X_A$ (Taylor expansion: $-\ln(1 - X_A) = X_A + X_A^2/2 + X_A^3/3 + \dots > X_A$).

Therefore:

$$\frac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} = \frac{X_A}{(1 - X_A)\big[-\ln(1 - X_A)\big]} > 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (Always True for Positive-Order Reactions)**: A single CSTR requires a **larger volume** than a single PFR to achieve the same conversion. This is the central comparison the KTU board tests.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — $n$-CSTRs-in-Series for a First-Order Reaction

For $N$ equal-volume CSTRs in series, the overall conversion $X_{A,N}$ is found by the **recursive** relation:

$$X_{A,i} = \frac{\tau_i k}{1 + \tau_i k} \quad \text{applied successively, or equivalently:}$$

$$(1 - X_{A,N}) = \frac{1}{(1 + \tau_i k)^N}$$

where $\tau_i = V_i / F_{v,0}$ is the space time of **one** tank in the series.

**Step 1 — Solve for $\tau_i$**

$$1 + \tau_i k = (1 - X_{A,N})^{-1/N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_i = \frac{(1 - X_{A,N})^{-1/N} - 1}{k}$$

**Step 2 — Total space time of the system**

$$\tau_{\text{total}} = N \tau_i = \frac{N \left[ (1 - X_{A,N})^{-1/N} - 1 \right]}{k}$$

**Step 3 — Limiting behaviour**

- As $N \to 1$: $\tau_{\text{total}} \to X_A / [k(1 - X_A)]$ — recovers single CSTR.
- As $N \to \infty$: $\tau_{\text{total}} \to -\ln(1 - X_A) / k$ — recovers PFR. This proves the central KTU result: **a train of CSTRs approaches PFR behaviour as $N \to \infty$**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Reactor Choice vs. Reaction Order

| Reaction Order | Best Single Reactor | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| $n = 0$ (zero order) | Either — same $V$ | $-r_A = k$, independent of $C_A$ |
| $n = 1$ (first order) | **PFR** (smaller) | Concentration is highest in PFR, where it matters most |
| $n > 1$ (higher order) | **PFR** (strongly preferred) | Rate depends strongly on $C_A$ → keep $C_A$ high |
| Autocatalytic ($n = -1$) | **CSTR** (smaller) | Rate is highest when $C_A$ is *low* (product-rich) |
| Parallel reactions with different orders | **Match mixing to order of desired reaction** | See Section 3.6 |

### 3.6 Effect of Mixing on Selectivity (Qualitative Derivation)

For a **parallel reaction network**:
$$A \xrightarrow{k_1,\; n_1} R \;\text{(desired)} \quad ; \quad A \xrightarrow{k_2,\; n_2} S \;\text{(undesired)}$$

The instantaneous selectivity is:

$$\frac{dC_R}{dC_S} = \frac{k_1 C_A^{n_1}}{k_2 C_A^{n_2}} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} C_A^{\,n_1 - n_2}$$

- If $n_1 > n_2$: high $C_A$ favours R → choose **PFR** (keeps $C_A$ high).
- If $n_1 < n_2$: low $C_A$ favours R → choose **CSTR** (keeps $C_A$ uniformly low).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Result**: For a **series reaction** $A \to R \to S$, the **PFR gives maximum $R$** at short residence times, while the **CSTR gives maximum $R$** at long residence times, but the CSTR's peak $R$ concentration is **always lower** than the PFR's peak. Thus PFR is preferred for series selectivity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams depict the **functional architecture** of the three ideal reactors and their RTD / mixing behaviour. These are the figures most commonly expected in KTU theory answers.

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Three Ideal Reactor Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BR["Batch Reactor BR - Perfect Mixing Unsteady State"]
        direction LR
        FeedBR["Inlet Feed A0 t=0"] -->|Loaded Once| TankBR["Well Mixed Tank C uniform V"]
        TankBR -->|After Time t| ProdBR["Product A converted to X"]
    end

    subgraph CSTR["CSTR MFR - Perfect Mixing Steady State"]
        direction LR
        FeedC["Inlet Feed A0 F0"] -->|Continuous| TankC["Well Mixed Tank C uniform exit"]
        TankC -->|Continuous| ProdC["Product A reduced C equal to inside"]
    end

    subgraph PFR["PFR - No Axial Mixing Steady State"]
        direction LR
        FeedP["Inlet Feed A0 F0"] -->|Continuous| TubeP["Tubular Reactor Concentration falls along L"]
        TubeP -->|Continuous| ProdP["Product A minimum C at exit"]
    end

    classDef nodeA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef nodeB fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef nodeC fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class FeedBR,TankBR,ProdBR nodeA
    class FeedC,TankC,ProdC nodeB
    class FeedP,TubeP,ProdP nodeC
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Mole Balance and RTD Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Feed Stream In: F0 and CA0"] --> B{"Reactor Type Selection"}
    B --"Unsteady BR" --> C["Differential dNA by dt equals rA times V"]
    B --"Steady CSTR" --> D["Exit C equals Internal C - Algebraic Solution"]
    B --"Steady PFR" --> E["Integral dCA along V - ODE Solution"]
    C --> F["Integrate rA versus XA to get Time t"]
    D --> G["Evaluate rA at Exit Concentration only"]
    E --> H["Integrate rA versus XA along Length"]
    F --> I["Final Output: Conversion XA and Volume V"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{"Compare with Target XA target and V available"}
    J --"OK" --> K["Reactor Validated"]
    J --"Not OK" --> L["Modify V or Switch Reactor Type"]

    classDef srcNode fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef outNode fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef dcdNode fill:#ff6f00,stroke:#000000,color:#000000

    class A,B,I,J srcNode
    class C,D,E,F,G,H dcdNode
    class K,L outNode
```

### 4.3 Comparison Tree — When to Use Which Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Reaction Kinetics Known"] --> Q1{"Reaction Order n"}
    Q1 --"n greater than 0" --> Q2{"Need Maximum Selectivity"}
    Q1 --"n less than 0 autocatalytic" --> R1["Choose CSTR MFR"]
    Q1 --"n equals 0" --> R2["Either CSTR or PFR - Volume Same"]
    Q2 --"Yes series A to R to S" --> R3["Choose PFR for Peak R"]
    Q2 --"Yes parallel desired high order" --> R4["Choose PFR for High CA"]
    Q2 --"Yes parallel desired low order" --> R5["Choose CSTR for Low CA"]
    Q2 --"No simple conversion" --> R6{"Volume Minimisation"}
    R6 --"Yes" --> R7["Choose PFR Single"]
    R6 --"Cost Constraint" --> R8["Use N CSTRs in Series Approaching PFR"]
    Q1 --"n greater than 0" --> R6

    classDef defStyle fill:#004d40,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef optStyle fill:#e65100,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef leafStyle fill:#283593,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    class S,Q1,Q2,R6 defStyle
    class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5,R7,R8 optStyle
    class R6 leafStyle
```

### 4.4 Multiple CSTRs-in-Series Architecture (Approaching PFR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0["Inlet F0 CA0"] --> S1["CSTR 1 Volume V1 tau1 X1"]
    S1 --> S2["CSTR 2 Volume V2 tau2 X2"]
    S2 --> S3["CSTR 3 Volume V3 tau3 X3"]
    S3 --> SN["CSTR N Volume VN tauN XN"]
    SN --> P["Outlet CA final XA final"]

    classDef tankNode fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef lineNode fill:#000000,stroke:#000000,color:#000000

    class S1,S2,S3,SN tankNode
    class F0,P lineNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: As $N \to \infty$ with constant total volume, the train of $N$ equal CSTRs approaches the performance of a single PFR of the same total volume. This is the **MKTU board's "limit theorem"** and is the conceptual reason why stirred-tank cascades are used industrially when PFR construction is impractical.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme Convention for PEPOT527)**: Part A = 3 marks each (short answer / 1-mark concept + 2-mark explanation). Part B = 14 marks with internal choice; typical split is (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** What is meant by an **ideal CSTR**? State its key design equation for a first-order irreversible reaction $A \rightarrow$ Products.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **ideal CSTR** (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor / Mixed Flow Reactor) is a steady-state reactor in which the contents are **perfectly and instantaneously mixed**, so the concentration inside the reactor is **spatially uniform** and **equal to the exit concentration** at all times (1 mark). It assumes constant density, single inlet and single outlet, and steady flow (0.5 mark).
>
> For a first-order irreversible reaction $A \rightarrow$ Products, the rate law is $-r_A = k C_A$. Substituting into the steady-state mole balance $V = F_{v,0} C_{A0} X_A / (-r_A)$ gives:
> $$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{v,0} \, X_A}{k (1 - X_A)} \quad \text{or} \quad \tau_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{X_A}{k(1-X_A)} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** State the **design equation of a PFR** for an $n$-th order reaction. Why is a PFR always more efficient (smaller volume) than a CSTR for a positive-order reaction?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The PFR design equation for an $n$-th order reaction $-r_A = k C_A^n$ is:
> $$V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{v,0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{k \, C_{A0}^n \, (1 - X_A)^n} = \frac{F_{v,0} \, C_{A0}^{1-n}}{k(n-1)} \left[ (1 - X_A)^{1-n} - 1 \right] \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$
>
> For positive-order reactions ($n \geq 1$), the rate of reaction is **higher at higher concentrations**. A PFR maintains **high $C_A$ throughout most of its length** (only at the exit does $C_A$ drop to its final value), whereas a CSTR instantaneously drops $C_A$ to the **final exit value everywhere**. Since the average rate of reaction is higher in a PFR, the volume required to achieve a given $X_A$ is **smaller** (1.5 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU ESE paper, you must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the design equation of a **CSTR** and a **PFR** for an irreversible first-order reaction $A \rightarrow$ Products. Show that for the **same conversion** and **same feed conditions**, the volume of a single CSTR is **always larger** than that of a single PFR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — CSTR Design Equation Derivation (3 marks)**

Steady-state mole balance on A:
$$F_{v,0} C_{A0} - F_{v,0} C_A + r_A \cdot V = 0$$

Substituting $-r_A = k C_A$ and $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$:
$$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{v,0} C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{F_{v,0} X_A}{k(1 - X_A)}$$

Hence, $\tau_{\text{CSTR}} = X_A / [k(1 - X_A)]$.

**Step 2 — PFR Design Equation Derivation (2 marks)**

Differential balance on a slice: $F_{v,0} dC_A = r_A \, dV$. Converting to $X_A$:
$$V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{v,0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = -\frac{F_{v,0}}{k C_{A0}} \ln(1 - X_A)$$

Hence, $\tau_{\text{PFR}} = -\ln(1 - X_A)/k$.

**Step 3 — Volume Comparison (2 marks)**

$$\frac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} = \frac{X_A/[k(1 - X_A)]}{-\ln(1 - X_A)/k} = \frac{X_A}{-(1 - X_A)\ln(1 - X_A)}$$

Using the Taylor series $-\ln(1 - X_A) = X_A + X_A^2/2 + X_A^3/3 + \cdots > X_A$ for $0 < X_A < 1$, the denominator $(1 - X_A)[-\ln(1 - X_A)] < X_A$, hence the ratio is **strictly greater than 1**. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** A liquid-phase first-order reaction $A \rightarrow$ Products is carried out in a **CSTR** and a **PFR** independently. The feed concentration is $C_{A0} = 2.5$ mol/L, the rate constant is $k = 0.40$ min$^{-1}$, the desired conversion is $X_A = 0.80$, and the volumetric flow rate is $F_{v,0} = 500$ L/min. Calculate the **reactor volume** required in each case, and the **percentage volume saving** achieved by switching from the CSTR to the PFR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — CSTR Volume (3 marks)**

$$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{v,0} X_A}{k(1 - X_A)} = \frac{500 \times 0.80}{0.40 \times (1 - 0.80)} = \frac{400}{0.08} = 5000 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — PFR Volume (3 marks)**

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = -\frac{F_{v,0}}{k}\ln(1 - X_A) = -\frac{500}{0.40}\ln(0.20) = -1250 \times (-1.6094) = 2011.8 \text{ L}$$

**Step 3 — Volume Saving (1 mark)**

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{V_{\text{CSTR}} - V_{\text{PFR}}}{V_{\text{CSTR}}} \times 100 = \frac{5000 - 2011.8}{5000} \times 100 = 59.76\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**:
> - Students commonly forget to **convert the flow rate to consistent units** before plugging into the formula. Always check that $F_{v,0}$, $V$, and $1/k$ carry the same time unit.
> - The **logarithm** in the PFR equation is natural log ($\ln$). Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$ is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> - A common mistake is to compute the percentage saving as $(V_{\text{PFR}} / V_{\text{CSTR}}) \times 100$; the **correct** form is the *reduction* relative to the original CSTR volume.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** For a **series reaction** $A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S$ (both first-order, irreversible), derive expressions for the **maximum concentration of the intermediate R** in (i) a PFR and (ii) a CSTR. Compare the two results and comment on which reactor gives the **higher yield of R**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — PFR Analysis (3 marks)**

The rate expressions are:
$$r_A = -k_1 C_A, \quad r_R = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_R, \quad r_S = k_2 C_R$$

Differential balances (with $\tau$ as the local "time" coordinate along the PFR):
$$\frac{dC_A}{d\tau} = -k_1 C_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_A = C_{A0} e^{-k_1 \tau}$$
$$\frac{dC_R}{d\tau} = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_R = k_1 C_{A0} e^{-k_1 \tau} - k_2 C_R$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Using integrating factor $e^{k_2 \tau}$:
$$C_R(\tau) = \frac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 \tau} - e^{-k_2 \tau}\right)$$

To find the **maximum** of $C_R$, set $dC_R / d\tau = 0$:
$$-k_1 e^{-k_1 \tau_{\max}} + k_2 e^{-k_2 \tau_{\max}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{\max, \text{PFR}} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1}$$

Substituting back:
$$C_{R,\max}^{\text{PFR}} = C_{A0}\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right)^{k_1/(k_2 - k_1)}$$

**Step 2 — CSTR Analysis (3 marks)**

At steady state, applying the mole balance on R:
$$0 = F_{v,0} C_{R0} - F_{v,0} C_R + V(k_1 C_A - k_2 C_R)$$

With $C_{R0} = 0$ and $C_A = C_{A0}/(1 + k_1 \tau)$ (CSTR solution for first-order A):
$$C_R = \frac{k_1 \tau \, C_A}{1 + k_2 \tau} = \frac{k_1 \tau \, C_{A0}}{(1 + k_1 \tau)(1 + k_2 \tau)}$$

To find the maximum, set $dC_R / d\tau = 0$:
$$\tau_{\max, \text{CSTR}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{k_1 k_2}}$$

Substituting:
$$C_{R,\max}^{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{C_{A0}}{\left(\sqrt{k_1/k_2} + 1\right)\left(\sqrt{k_2/k_1} + 1\right)} = \frac{C_{A0}}{\left(\sqrt{k_1} + \sqrt{k_2}\right)^2 / \sqrt{k_1 k_2}}$$

After simplification:
$$C_{R,\max}^{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{C_{A0}\sqrt{k_1 k_2}}{\left(\sqrt{k_1} + \sqrt{k_2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison (1 mark)**

The **PFR always produces a higher $C_{R,\max}$** than the CSTR for the same $k_1$, $k_2$, and $C_{A0}$, because the PFR allows the desired reaction to proceed at the high initial $C_A$ before significant depletion of A occurs. Therefore, **PFR is preferred for series-reaction selectivity**. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** Two equal-volume CSTRs are to be used **in series** to carry out a first-order reaction with $k = 0.25$ min$^{-1}$. If the feed flow rate is $F_{v,0} = 100$ L/min and the overall desired conversion is $X_A = 0.90$, determine (i) the **volume of each tank** in the series, and (ii) the **total volume** that would be required if a **single CSTR** were used. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — N-CSTRs-in-Series Formula Recall (1 mark)**

For $N$ equal-volume CSTRs in series with a first-order reaction, the exit concentration is:
$$C_{A,N} = \frac{C_{A0}}{(1 + k \tau_i)^N} \quad \text{where} \quad \tau_i = V_i / F_{v,0}$$

Equivalently: $1 - X_{A,N} = (1 + k \tau_i)^{-N}$.

**Step 2 — Solve for $\tau_i$ (3 marks)**

$$1 - X_{A,N} = 0.10 = (1 + k \tau_i)^{-2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1 + k \tau_i)^2 = 10$$
$$1 + k \tau_i = \sqrt{10} = 3.1623 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k \tau_i = 2.1623$$
$$\tau_i = \frac{2.1623}{0.25} = 8.649 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of each tank and total volume (1 mark)**

$$V_i = \tau_i \cdot F_{v,0} = 8.649 \times 100 = 864.9 \text{ L} \approx 865 \text{ L}$$
$$V_{\text{total, series}} = 2 \times 865 = 1730 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4 — Single CSTR Volume (2 marks)**

$$\tau_{\text{single}} = \frac{X_A}{k(1 - X_A)} = \frac{0.90}{0.25 \times 0.10} = \frac{0.90}{0.025} = 36 \text{ min}$$
$$V_{\text{single CSTR}} = 36 \times 100 = 3600 \text{ L}$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark)**

The two-CSTR-in-series arrangement requires **1730 L**, which is **only 48%** of the 3600 L needed for a single CSTR. This empirically confirms the KTU principle that **stirred-tank cascades approach PFR performance** and dramatically reduce reactor volume for high-conversion, positive-order reactions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**:
> - Do not confuse $(1 + k\tau_i)^N$ with $1 + (k\tau_i)^N$. The correct form is $(1 + k\tau_i)^N$.
> - The exit concentration $C_{A,N}$ from $N$ tanks in series is **not** simply $C_{A0} - N \cdot r_A \tau_i$. You **must** use the recursive or $(1 + k\tau_i)^N$ formula.
> - The single-CSTR answer is **always larger** than the total series volume when $N \geq 2$. If you compute the opposite, recheck your algebra.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Mole Balance is the Mother Equation**: Every ideal-reactor design equation comes from $\text{IN} - \text{OUT} + \text{GEN} = 0$ (steady) or $dN/dt = \text{IN} - \text{OUT} + \text{GEN}$ (unsteady, batch).
- ✅ **Three Reactor Equations — Memorize the Form**:
  - BR: $t = C_{A0} \int dX_A / (-r_A)$
  - CSTR: $V = F_{v,0} C_{A0} X_A / (-r_A)$ (algebraic — evaluate rate at exit)
  - PFR: $V = F_{v,0} \int dX_A / (-r_A)$ (integral — rate varies along length)
- ✅ **First-Order Closed-Form Results** (high-yield, often given 3 marks directly):
  - BR: $t = -\ln(1 - X_A)/k$
  - CSTR: $\tau = X_A / [k(1 - X_A)]$
  - PFR: $\tau = -\ln(1 - X_A)/k$
  - $N$-CSTRs: $\tau_{\text{total}} = N \cdot \left[(1 - X_A)^{-1/N} - 1\right]/k$
- ✅ **Mixing Rule for Positive-Order Reactions**: $V_{\text{PFR}} < V_{\text{CSTR}} < V_{\text{series of 2 CSTRs}} \dots \to V_{\text{PFR}}$ as $N \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Autocatalytic Reactions** (rate increases with product): **CSTR is better** than PFR.
- ✅ **Selectivity Principle**:
  - Series $A \to R \to S$: **PFR gives higher peak $C_R$**.
  - Parallel reactions with desired product having **higher order**: **PFR** (keep $C_A$ high).
  - Parallel reactions with desired product having **lower order**: **CSTR** (keep $C_A$ low).
- ✅ **Space Time vs. Residence Time**: For liquids (constant density), $\tau = V/F_{v,0}$ is **numerically equal** to the mean residence time. For gases, account for density change.
- ✅ **Unit Consistency is Non-Negotiable**: $F_{v,0}$ in L/min, $V$ in L, $k$ in min$^{-1}$ for first order. Mixed units cost 1–2 marks.
- ✅ **Logarithm Convention**: All $\ln$ in the PFR and BR equations are **natural logarithms** (base $e$). Using $\log_{10}$ will silently give the wrong answer.
- ✅ **Limiting-Case Theorem**: $N$ equal CSTRs in series $\to$ PFR as $N \to \infty$. This is **the most-tested conceptual point** in KTU viva and short-answer questions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Warning**: The single most common mistake in this module is **forgetting to substitute $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$** before integrating. If you integrate with $C_A$ left as a symbol, you will get stuck. Always convert to $X_A$ **first**, then perform the integration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
