Each node uses alphanumeric identifiers and clean text labels, with no embedded formatting characters inside the labels. The block diagrams precisely map the *cause-effect* relationships between steam flow, jacket dynamics, heat transfer, vessel dynamics, and output temperature — the four pillars of any process modelling exercise. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define a steam jacketed vessel and state the role of the jacket in process heating. List any two key modelling assumptions used in its dynamic simulation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A steam jacketed vessel is a closed process container surrounded by an outer *jacket* through which saturated steam is circulated to indirectly heat the contents. The jacket enables uniform, contamination-free heat transfer from the condensing steam to the process fluid by using the latent heat released during condensation. Two key modelling assumptions are: 1. The contents of the vessel are **perfectly mixed**, so a single temperature `T` describes the entire charge. 2. The steam in the jacket is **saturated at constant pressure**, so the steam temperature `T_s` remains fixed throughout the heating operation. *(Examiner allocates ~ 1 mark per assumption and 1 mark for the definition.)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: What is the thermal time constant of a steam jacketed vessel? Write its expression and explain its physical significance.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The thermal time constant `τ` of a steam jacketed vessel is defined as: $$\tau = \frac{M \cdot C_p}{U \cdot A}$$ Physically, `τ` represents the time required for the temperature difference $(T_s - T)$ to decay to approximately **36.8%** of its initial value (i.e., by a factor of $1/e$). A larger `τ` means the vessel heats up slowly (large mass or low heat transfer), while a smaller `τ` means rapid heating. *(~ 1 mark for the formula, 2 marks for the explanation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each module question is **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carrying 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. The model solutions below are *complete and exhaustive*, suitable for self-study. --- ### ❖ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]` **A(a) [7 Marks]:** A steam-jacketed batch vessel contains 800 kg of an oil (specific heat C_p = 2.5 kJ/kg·K) at an initial temperature of 25 °C. Saturated steam at 3.0 bar (T_s = 133.5 °C, λ = 2164 kJ/kg) is admitted. The overall heat transfer coefficient and heat transfer area are U = 800 W/(m²·K) and A = 4.0 m² respectively. Derive the dynamic model and determine the time required for the oil to reach 120 °C. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Derive the dynamic model (3 marks).** Energy balance on the oil (assumed perfectly mixed, constant mass, constant C_p): $$M \cdot C_p \cdot \frac{dT}{dt} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T)$$ Rearranging and defining $\tau = \dfrac{M \cdot C_p}{U \cdot A}$: $$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau} \cdot (T_s - T)$$ **Step 2 — Identify the time constant (1 mark).** $$\tau = \frac{M \cdot C_p}{U \cdot A} = \frac{800 \times 2500}{800 \times 4.0} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{3200} = 625 \text{ s}$$ *(Note: C_p must be converted to J/kg·K: 2.5 kJ/kg·K = 2500 J/kg·K.)* **Step 3 — Use the analytical solution (1 mark).** $$T(t) = T_s - (T_s - T_0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for time `t` (2 marks).** $$T(t) = T_s - (T_s - T_0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ $$e^{-t/\tau} = \frac{T_s - T(t)}{T_s - T_0}$$ $$-\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln\left(\frac{T_s - T(t)}{T_s - T_0}\right)$$ $$t = \tau \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_s - T_0}{T_s - T(t)}\right)$$ Substitute $T_s = 133.5$, $T_0 = 25$, $T(t) = 120$: $$t = 625 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{133.5 - 25}{133.5 - 120}\right) = 625 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{108.5}{13.5}\right)$$ $$t = 625 \cdot \ln(8.037) = 625 \times 2.0835$$ $$\boxed{\,t \approx 1302 \text{ seconds} \approx 21.7 \text{ minutes}\,}$$ --- **A(b) [7 Marks]:** Continuing the same problem, calculate (i) the cumulative mass of steam consumed to reach 120 °C, and (ii) the instantaneous condensate flow rate `F_so` at the instant the oil is at 90 °C. **Model Solution:** **Part (i) — Cumulative steam mass (4 marks):** Energy gained by oil: $$Q_{total} = M \cdot C_p \cdot (T_f - T_0) = 800 \times 2.5 \times (120 - 25) = 190{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$ Cumulative steam: $$M_{steam} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\lambda} = \frac{190{,}000}{2164}$$ $$\boxed{\,M_{steam} \approx 87.8 \text{ kg of steam}\,}$$ **Part (ii) — Instantaneous condensate flow at T = 90 °C (3 marks):** Heat transfer rate at T = 90 °C: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T) = 800 \times 4.0 \times (133.5 - 90) = 3200 \times 43.5 = 139{,}200 \text{ W}$$ Instantaneous condensate rate: $$F_{so} = \frac{Q}{\lambda} = \frac{139{,}200 \text{ J/s}}{2{,}164{,}000 \text{ J/kg}}$$ $$\boxed{\,F_{so} \approx 0.0643 \text{ kg/s} = 231.5 \text{ kg/hr}\,}$$ --- ### ❖ Question B `[KTU University Exam - Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]` **B(a) [7 Marks]:** Develop the complete dynamic model of a steam-jacketed vessel with a *time-varying* steam flow rate `F_si(t)`. Clearly state all assumptions, write the two governing ODEs, and explain why this system requires numerical solution. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State the assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Perfect mixing in vessel and jacket. 2. Constant physical properties (ρ, C_p, U, A, λ). 3. Negligible heat losses to surroundings. 4. Saturated steam at constant jacket pressure → $T_s$ is constant in time. 5. Constant mass of process liquid (batch operation). 6. Negligible kinetic and potential energy changes. **Step 2 — Jacket Mass Balance (2 marks):** $$\frac{dM_s}{dt} = F_{si}(t) - F_{so}$$ **Step 3 — Heat Transfer Coupling + Vessel Energy Balance (2 marks):** Heat transfer from jacket to vessel: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T)$. The condensate rate is obtained by coupling: $F_{so} \cdot \lambda = U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T)$, giving $F_{so} = \dfrac{U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T)}{\lambda}$. Process side energy balance: $$M \cdot C_p \cdot \frac{dT}{dt} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T)$$ **Step 4 — Coupled System (1 mark):** $$\begin{cases} \dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{M \cdot C_p} \cdot (T_s - T) \\[1.0em] \dfrac{dM_s}{dt} = F_{si}(t) - \dfrac{U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T)}{\lambda} \end{cases}$$ with IC: $T(0) = T_0$, $M_s(0) = M_{s,0}$. **Step 5 — Why Numerical Solution is Required:** The second equation contains the term $T$ inside a nonlinear product $(T_s - T)$ multiplied by a coefficient involving $M_{si}(t)$, which is itself an arbitrary function of time. A general closed-form solution does not exist. The system is a set of coupled, time-varying, nonlinear ODEs and must be solved numerically (e.g., RK4) step by step. --- **B(b) [7 Marks]:** For a steam-jacketed vessel with the following data — M = 1000 kg, C_p = 3.2 kJ/kg·K, U = 1200 W/(m²·K), A = 5.0 m², T_s = 150 °C, T_0 = 30 °C, steam flow F_si(t) = 0.5 + 0.05·t kg/s — compute the temperature and steam inventory at t = 100 s using Euler's method with step size h = 50 s (i.e., take two steps from t = 0 to t = 100 s). **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute constants (1 mark).** $$\tau = \frac{M \cdot C_p}{U \cdot A} = \frac{1000 \times 3200}{1200 \times 5.0} = \frac{3{,}200{,}000}{6000} \approx 533.33 \text{ s}$$ The ODE system: $$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau}(T_s - T), \qquad \frac{dM_s}{dt} = F_{si}(t) - \frac{U \cdot A (T_s - T)}{\lambda}$$ For simplicity, assume $\lambda = 2200$ kJ/kg = 2,200,000 J/kg. (Examiner may give `λ` explicitly.) **Step 2 — First Euler step: t = 0 → t = 50 s (3 marks).** At t = 0: $T = 30$ °C, $M_s = 0$ (assumed zero inventory initially). Slope of $T$: $\dfrac{dT}{dt}\bigg|_0 = \dfrac{1}{533.33}(150 - 30) = \dfrac{120}{533.33} = 0.225$ K/s. Slope of $M_s$: $F_{si}(0) = 0.5 + 0.05 \times 0 = 0.5$ kg/s. Heat transfer rate: $Q_0 = 1200 \times 5.0 \times (150 - 30) = 6000 \times 120 = 720{,}000$ W. $F_{so,0} = \dfrac{720{,}000}{2{,}200{,}000} = 0.3273$ kg/s. $\dfrac{dM_s}{dt}\bigg|_0 = 0.5 - 0.3273 = 0.1727$ kg/s. Updated values: $$T_1 = 30 + 50 \times 0.225 = 30 + 11.25 = 41.25 \text{ °C}$$ $$M_{s,1} = 0 + 50 \times 0.1727 = 8.635 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3 — Second Euler step: t = 50 → t = 100 s (3 marks).** At t = 50: $T = 41.25$ °C, $M_s = 8.635$ kg. Slope of $T$: $\dfrac{dT}{dt}\bigg|_{50} = \dfrac{1}{533.33}(150 - 41.25) = \dfrac{108.75}{533.33} = 0.2039$ K/s. Slope of $M_s$: $F_{si}(50) = 0.5 + 0.05 \times 50 = 3.0$ kg/s. Heat transfer rate: $Q_{50} = 6000 \times (150 - 41.25) = 6000 \times 108.75 = 652{,}500$ W. $F_{so,50} = \dfrac{652{,}500}{2{,}200{,}000} = 0.2966$ kg/s. $\dfrac{dM_s}{dt}\bigg|_{50} = 3.0 - 0.2966 = 2.7034$ kg/s. Updated values: $$T_2 = 41.25 + 50 \times 0.2039 = 41.25 + 10.195 = 51.45 \text{ °C}$$ $$M_{s,2} = 8.635 + 50 \times 2.7034 = 8.635 + 135.17 = 143.81 \text{ kg}$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\,T(100\,\text{s}) \approx 51.4 \text{ °C}, \quad M_s(100\,\text{s}) \approx 143.8 \text{ kg}\,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (loss of 1–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to convert kJ to J when computing τ. The denominator $U \cdot A$ is in W = J/s, so the numerator $M \cdot C_p$ must also be in J/K. **Always convert C_p to J/kg·K before substitution.** > 2. **Sign Convention Mistakes (loss of 1–2 marks):** Heat is *added to* the vessel, so the sign on the right side of $\frac{dT}{dt}$ must be **positive** when $T < T_s$. A negative sign flipped on $T$ is a classic deduction. > 3. **Forgetting Initial Conditions (loss of 1 mark):** Every numerical problem in this module *must* state $T(0) = T_0$ explicitly. Examiners will not award full marks without it. > 4. **Numerical Method Rigor (loss of 1 mark in Part B(b)):** Euler's method uses the *left-endpoint* slope. Do **not** mix it with RK4 or with the analytical solution unless the question says "compare". > 5. **Latent Heat Units (loss of 0.5–1 mark):** Always express λ in J/kg when Q is in W, and in kJ/kg when Q is in kJ/s. Mixing these is the most common single numerical error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Steam Jacketed Vessel** - [x] A steam jacketed vessel is a **two-zone** system: **jacket (steam side, isothermal)** + **vessel (process side, well-mixed)**. - [x] The fundamental model is a **first-order linear ODE**: $M \cdot C_p \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dt} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T)$. - [x] The **thermal time constant** $\tau = \dfrac{M \cdot C_p}{U \cdot A}$ fully characterizes the heating dynamics. - [x] Analytical solution: $T(t) = T_s - (T_s - T_0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$. - [x] At $t = \tau$, the temperature rise reaches about **63.2%** of $(T_s - T_0)$. - [x] At $t = 4\tau$, the system is at **> 98%** of steady state (rule of thumb for design). - [x] **Newton's law of cooling** governs the heat transfer: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T)$. - [x] **Coupling equation**: $F_{so} \cdot \lambda = U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T)$. - [x] **Cumulative steam** for a finite temperature rise: $M_{steam} = \dfrac{M \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda}$. - [x] For **variable steam flow**, the system becomes **coupled nonlinear ODEs** — solve with **Euler** or **RK4**. - [x] Always **convert C_p to J/kg·K** before computing `τ`. - [x] The jacket temperature $T_s$ is **fixed by the steam pressure**, not by the heat demand. - [x] KTU board loves two-part 14-mark questions: **(a)** analytical, **(b)** numerical/computational. - [x] Six modelling assumptions (perfect mixing, constant properties, saturated steam, no heat loss, no phase change, constant holdup) are **mandatory preamble** in any KTU answer. - [x] **Do not forget the initial condition** $T(0) = T_0$ — it is the only thing that makes the analytical solution unique.

topics.title: Isothermal Constant and Variable Holdup CSTR in Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Isothermal Constant and Variable Holdup CSTR in Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — ISOTHERMAL CONSTANT AND VARIABLE HOLDUP CSTR IN SERIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **Isothermal Constant Holdup CSTR in Series** is a configuration in which **n** Continuous Stirred Tank Reactors (CSTRs) of **equal volume (V)** are connected in cascade, with the effluent of reactor *i* becoming the feed to reactor *i+1*. The system is **isothermal** (T = constant), operates at **steady state**, and each reactor exhibits **perfect mixing** (spatially uniform concentration and temperature).

An **Isothermal Variable Holdup CSTR in Series** is a generalization where the volume (and hence the space time τ) of each reactor may differ: **V₁ ≠ V₂ ≠ ... ≠ Vₙ**. The design objective is to determine the **optimal distribution of volumes** to minimize the **total reactor volume** for a specified conversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PEPOT527 Module 2**
> Students must master:
> 1. Mass balance derivation for a single CSTR.
> 2. Recursive concentration equation for *n* CSTRs in cascade.
> 3. Conversion formulae for first- and second-order reactions.
> 4. Optimum holdup distribution (Levenspiel's rule).
> 5. Comparison of CSTR-in-series with a single CSTR and with a PFR.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Relay" 🚰

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture (The Bucket Relay):**
> Imagine a relay of buckets on a conveyor belt, where each bucket is a CSTR. Water (the reactant) is poured into Bucket 1, mixed perfectly, then poured into Bucket 2, and so on. Each bucket has the **same residence time** if all buckets are identical in size (**constant holdup**). If we make some buckets larger than others (**variable holdup**), we can control *how long* the water reacts at each stage — useful when the reaction rate changes dramatically with concentration (e.g., a second-order reaction slows down at low concentrations, so a *larger* later tank is beneficial).

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

- **Volumetric flow rate:** $v_0$ [m³/s]
- **Reactor volume:** $V_i$ [m³]
- **Space time (Holdup time):** $\tau_i = V_i / v_0$ [s]
- **Mean residence time:** $\bar{t} = \tau$ (at steady state for incompressible fluid)
- **Rate constant:** $k$ [units depend on order] — **bold** in standard notation
- **Concentration of A in reactor *i*:** $C_{A,i}$ [kmol/m³]

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Mass Balance — Single CSTR

At steady state, the **mole balance** for species A in an isothermal CSTR is:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0$$

In terms of concentration and volumetric flow (assuming constant density):

$$v_0 C_{A0} - v_0 C_A + r_A V = 0$$

For a single reaction $A \rightarrow$ products with rate $-r_A = kC_A^n$:

$$V = \frac{v_0(C_{A0} - C_A)}{kC_A^n}$$

This is the **fundamental CSTR design equation**.

### 2.2 Extension to *n* CSTRs in Series

For reactor *i* in the cascade, the inlet concentration is $C_{A,i-1}$ and outlet is $C_{A,i}$:

$$V_i = \frac{v_0(C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i})}{kC_{A,i}^n}$$

This is a **recursive relation** — solving it stage by stage gives the full concentration profile.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAE — CSTRs IN SERIES (MEMORIZE)**

| # | Equation | Application / Notes |
|---|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | $V_i = v_0(C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i}) / (kC_{A,i}^n)$ | General design eq. for reactor *i* |
| 2 | $\tau_i = V_i / v_0$ | Space time definition |
| 3 | $C_{A,i} = C_{A,i-1} / (1 + k\tau_i)$ | **First-order, *n* = 1** |
| 4 | $C_{A,n} = C_{A0} / \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 + k\tau_i)$ | First-order general series |
| 5 | $1 - X_A = (1 + k\tau)^{-n}$ | First-order, **equal holdup** |
| 6 | $\tau = [(1-X_A)^{-1/n} - 1] / k$ | Total space time per tank, 1st order |
| 7 | $V_{\text{total}} = n v_0[(1-X_A)^{-1/n} - 1] / k$ | First-order, equal holdup, total volume |
| 8 | $C_{A,i} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4k\tau_i C_{A,i-1}}}{2k\tau_i}$ | **Second-order, *n* = 2** |
| 9 | $E(t) = \frac{1}{(n-1)!}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)^{n-1}\frac{1}{\tau}e^{-t/\tau}$ | RTD for *n* equal CSTRs |
| 10 | $\sigma^2 = \bar{t}^2 / n$ | Variance of RTD — *n* equal CSTRs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The CSTR-in-series model is the standard approach for designing **multi-stage fermentation reactors**, **polymerization cascades**, and **biological wastewater treatment trains** where each stage operates at a different biomass concentration and reaction rate.

### 2.4 Optimum Holdup Distribution — Levenspiel's Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Levenspiel's Optimum Distribution Theorem**
> - For **first-order** reactions (n = 1): **Equal** holdups minimize total volume.
> - For **order n > 1** (e.g., second-order): Use **increasing** holdup ($V_1 < V_2 < \cdots < V_n$).
> - For **zero-order** (n = 0): Use **decreasing** holdup ($V_1 > V_2 > \cdots > V_n$).

**Intuition:** When reaction rate slows down at low concentrations (high order), each successive reactor needs a *longer* residence time to continue the reaction, so subsequent tanks must be larger.

### 2.5 Comparison with a Single CSTR and a PFR

- A **single CSTR** always requires a **larger** volume than a PFR for the same conversion (for n ≥ 1).
- **n equal CSTRs in series** approach PFR performance as $n \to \infty$.
- **CSTR series** is preferred when:
  - Slow reactions need long residence times.
  - Reaction is autocatalytic (best with backmixing).
  - Temperature control is critical in exothermic reactions.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — STEP-BY-STEP LOGIC

## 2.6 Block-by-Block Logical Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Define the system boundary.**
Each CSTR is a control volume. Species A enters, reacts, and leaves at steady state. Accumulation = 0.

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state mole balance.**
$$v_0 C_{A,i-1} = v_0 C_{A,i} + kC_{A,i}^n V_i$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_i$ (design) or $C_{A,i}$ (analysis).**
Rearranging gives the **design equation** (1) in the cheat sheet.

**Step 4 — Chain reactors recursively.**
The exit of reactor *i* becomes the inlet of reactor *i+1*: $C_{A,i} \to C_{A,i+1\text{,in}}$.

**Step 5 — Apply the reaction order constraint.**
For n = 1: linear → closed-form solution. For n = 2: quadratic → use positive root of $\sqrt{1+4k\tau_iC_{A,i-1}}$.

**Step 6 — Optimize (variable holdup case).**
For variable holdup, use **calculus of variations** or **AM-GM inequality** to find the $\tau_i$ distribution that minimizes $V_T = \sum V_i$ subject to the conversion constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CSTRs in Series Outperform a Single CSTR (for n ≥ 1 reactions):**
> In a single CSTR, the reactant is immediately diluted to the *exit* concentration, which is the **lowest** concentration in the system. For reactions where rate depends on concentration ($n \geq 1$), this **lowers the average rate**. A series of smaller CSTRs keeps the concentration higher in early reactors, boosting the overall rate.

## 2.7 The AM-GM Proof of Equal-Holdup Optimality (First-Order)

We want to minimize $V_T = v_0 \sum_{i=1}^n \tau_i$ subject to the constraint:
$$\prod_{i=1}^n (1 + k\tau_i) = \frac{C_{A0}}{C_{A,n}}$$

By the **Arithmetic Mean – Geometric Mean (AM-GM) inequality**:

$$\left[\prod_{i=1}^n (1+k\tau_i)\right]^{1/n} \leq \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (1+k\tau_i) = 1 + k\bar{\tau}$$

where $\bar{\tau} = (\sum \tau_i)/n$. Therefore:

$$1 + k\bar{\tau} \geq \left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_{A,n}}\right)^{1/n}$$

with **equality if and only if** all $\tau_i$ are equal. This proves that for first-order reactions, equal holdup minimizes total volume.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ✏️ STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Recursive Concentration for First-Order Series

Starting from the design equation for reactor *i*:
$$V_i = \frac{v_0(C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i})}{kC_{A,i}}$$

Divide both sides by $v_0$ to get $\tau_i$:
$$\tau_i = \frac{C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i}}{kC_{A,i}}$$

Expand the numerator and isolate $C_{A,i-1}$:
$$\tau_i k C_{A,i} = C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i}$$
$$C_{A,i-1} = C_{A,i} + k\tau_i C_{A,i} = C_{A,i}(1 + k\tau_i)$$

Solve for $C_{A,i}$:
$$C_{A,i} = \frac{C_{A,i-1}}{1 + k\tau_i}$$

Apply recursively for *n* reactors with equal holdup ($\tau_i = \tau$ for all *i*):

$$C_{A,1} = \frac{C_{A0}}{1+k\tau}, \quad C_{A,2} = \frac{C_{A,1}}{1+k\tau} = \frac{C_{A0}}{(1+k\tau)^2}, \quad \ldots$$

In closed form:
$$\boxed{\,C_{A,n} = \frac{C_{A0}}{(1+k\tau)^n}\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Volume in Terms of Conversion (First-Order)

Define fractional conversion $X_A = 1 - C_{A,n}/C_{A0}$, so $C_{A,n} = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$. Substituting into the closed-form expression:

$$C_{A0}(1 - X_A) = \frac{C_{A0}}{(1+k\tau)^n}$$

$$(1 - X_A) = (1+k\tau)^{-n}$$

Invert both sides:
$$(1 - X_A)^{-1} = (1+k\tau)^n$$

Take the *n*-th root:
$$(1 - X_A)^{-1/n} = 1 + k\tau$$

Solve for $\tau$:
$$k\tau = (1 - X_A)^{-1/n} - 1$$
$$\tau = \frac{(1 - X_A)^{-1/n} - 1}{k}$$

Multiply by $n \cdot v_0$ to get the total reactor volume:
$$\boxed{\,V_T = \frac{n v_0}{k}\left[(1 - X_A)^{-1/n} - 1\right]\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Second-Order Reaction in CSTRs in Series

For $-r_A = kC_A^2$, the design equation for reactor *i* is:
$$\tau_i = \frac{C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i}}{kC_{A,i}^2}$$

Multiply both sides by $kC_{A,i}^2$:
$$k\tau_i C_{A,i}^2 = C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i}$$

Rearrange into a standard quadratic in $C_{A,i}$:
$$k\tau_i C_{A,i}^2 + C_{A,i} - C_{A,i-1} = 0$$

Apply the quadratic formula $x = \frac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ (taking the positive root since $C_{A,i} > 0$):

$$C_{A,i} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4k\tau_i C_{A,i-1}}}{2k\tau_i}$$

This is the **recursive working formula** for second-order CSTRs in series.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Equal Holdup, First-Order)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Problem (KTU-Style):**
> An isothermal CSTR train of **3 equal-volume reactors** processes a feed with $C_{A0} = 2.0$ kmol/m³. The first-order rate constant is $k = 0.5$ min⁻¹. The total volumetric flow rate is $v_0 = 1.0$ m³/min, and the total residence time is $\bar{t} = 6$ min. Find:
> (a) The concentration at the exit of the 2nd reactor.
> (b) The overall conversion.

**Solution:**

(a) Each reactor has $\tau = 6/3 = 2$ min and $k\tau = 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0$.

$$C_{A,1} = \frac{2.0}{1+1.0} = 1.0 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

$$C_{A,2} = \frac{1.0}{1+1.0} = 0.5 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

(b) Overall conversion:
$$X_A = 1 - \frac{0.5}{2.0} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75 \text{ (or 75%)}$$

**Verification using the closed-form formula:**
$$1 - X_A = (1+1.0)^{-3} = 0.125 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_A = 0.875$$

Wait — there is a discrepancy. The *closed-form* formula gives $X_A = 0.875$ for *three* reactors, while the step-by-step above only computed to the *second* reactor. Continuing:

$$C_{A,3} = \frac{0.5}{1+1.0} = 0.25 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$
$$X_A = 1 - 0.25/2.0 = 0.875 \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting that the question asked for the exit of the **2nd** reactor (giving $X_{A,2} = 0.75$) but using the closed-form formula for **3** reactors (giving $X_A = 0.875$). Always **re-read the question carefully** to identify the correct stage.

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Optimum Holdup for Two CSTRs in Series (2nd Order)

Let $V_1$ and $V_2$ be the volumes of two CSTRs in series for a second-order reaction. We wish to find $C_{A,1}$ (the intermediate concentration) that minimizes $V_T = V_1 + V_2$ for fixed $C_{A0}$ and $C_{A,2}$.

$$V_1 = \frac{v_0(C_{A0} - C_{A,1})}{kC_{A,1}^2}, \quad V_2 = \frac{v_0(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})}{kC_{A,2}^2}$$

Differentiate $V_T$ w.r.t. $C_{A,1}$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{dV_T}{dC_{A,1}} = \frac{v_0}{k}\left[\frac{-kC_{A,1}^2 - (C_{A0}-C_{A,1})(2kC_{A,1})}{k^2C_{A,1}^4} + \frac{1}{C_{A,2}^2}\right] = 0$$

Simplify the $V_1$ derivative:
$$\frac{dV_1}{dC_{A,1}} = \frac{v_0}{kC_{A,1}^3}\left(C_{A,1} - 2C_{A0}\right)$$

Setting the total derivative to zero:
$$\frac{C_{A,1} - 2C_{A0}}{C_{A,1}^3} + \frac{1}{C_{A,2}^2} = 0$$

$$C_{A,2}^2(C_{A,1} - 2C_{A0}) = -C_{A,1}^3$$

$$C_{A,1}^3 + C_{A,2}^2 C_{A,1} = 2C_{A0}C_{A,2}^2$$

$$\boxed{\,C_{A,1}(C_{A,1}^2 + C_{A,2}^2) = 2C_{A0}C_{A,2}^2\,}$$

This cubic equation gives the optimum intermediate concentration. Solving yields $C_{A,1} < (C_{A0}+C_{A,2})/2$, confirming that **the first reactor should be smaller** (smaller volume = higher concentration held longer at the inlet).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — CSTR Train in Series

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEED([Feed: v0, CA0]) --> R1["CSTR 1\nV1, tau1"]
    R1 --> R2["CSTR 2\nV2, tau2"]
    R2 --> R3["CSTR 3\nV3, tau3"]
    R3 --> RN["CSTR n\nVn, taun"]
    RN --> PROD([Product: CA,n])

    R1 -. CA1 .-> R2
    R2 -. CA2 .-> R3
    R3 -. CA3 .-> RN
    RN -. CAn .-> PROD

    style FEED fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style R1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style R2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style R3 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style RN fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style PROD fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.2 Conversion vs Number of Reactors (First-Order, Fixed Total Volume)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Conversion XA vs Number of Equal CSTRs in Series (k*tau_total = 2)"
    x-axis "Number of Reactors, n" [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, 20]
    y-axis "Conversion, XA" 0 --> 1
    line [0.667, 0.800, 0.857, 0.889, 0.909, 0.953, 0.974]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** As *n* increases, the conversion approaches that of a PFR ($\approx 0.865$ for $k\tau_{\text{total}}=1$; the values shown reflect $k\tau_{\text{total}}=2$). The curve **asymptotes** to $X_A \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$ *only if* $k\tau_{\text{total}} \to \infty$.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Design Workflow for CSTRs in Series

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Reaction Order n] --> B{Is holdup equal?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Equal-Holdup Formulas]
    B -- No --> D[Define Optimization Objective]
    C --> E1[First-Order: tau = (1-XA)^-1/n - 1 / k]
    C --> E2[Second-Order: Solve Quadratic Recursively]
    D --> F[Apply Levenspiel Rule]
    F --> G1[n=1: Equal Volumes]
    F --> G2[n>1: Increasing Volumes]
    F --> G3[n=0: Decreasing Volumes]
    E1 --> H[Compute V_T = n*v0*tau]
    E2 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Verify RTD: sigma^2 = tbar^2 / n]
    I --> J[Final Design Output]

    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.4 Block-Level RTD Schematic — *n* CSTRs in Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Series[Series of n CSTRs]
        direction LR
        S1[Tank 1\ntau] --> S2[Tank 2\ntau] --> S3[Tank 3\ntau] --> Sn[Tank n\ntau]
    end
    IN([Pulse Input at t=0]) --> S1
    Sn --> OUT([Output: E_t Distribution])

    style IN fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style OUT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style S1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style S2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style S3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style Sn fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
```

> [!NOTE]
> **RTD Insight:** As *n* increases, the RTD narrows (variance $\sigma^2 = \bar{t}^2/n \to 0$) and approaches the **plug-flow** Dirac-delta response. This is the mathematical proof that *n* CSTRs in series behave like a PFR as $n \to \infty$.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q1.** *A series of three equal-volume isothermal CSTRs is used to carry out a first-order liquid-phase reaction. The feed concentration is $C_{A0} = 1.5$ kmol/m³ and the desired exit concentration is $C_{A,3} = 0.15$ kmol/m³. If $k = 0.8$ min⁻¹ and $v_0 = 0.5$ m³/min, calculate the volume of each reactor.* **[3 Marks]**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> Step 1 — Overall conversion: $X_A = 1 - 0.15/1.5 = 0.90$
>
> Step 2 — Using the equal-holdup formula for *n* = 3:
>
> $$\tau = \frac{(1 - 0.90)^{-1/3} - 1}{0.8} = \frac{(0.10)^{-1/3} - 1}{0.8}$$
>
> Step 3 — Compute $(0.10)^{-1/3} = (10)^{1/3} \approx 2.154$
>
> Step 4 — Therefore $\tau = (2.154 - 1)/0.8 = 1.442$ min
>
> Step 5 — Each reactor volume: $V = v_0 \cdot \tau = 0.5 \times 1.442 = 0.721$ m³

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2.** *State the design equation for an isothermal CSTR. For a first-order reaction, derive the expression for exit concentration from the n-th reactor in a series of equal-volume CSTRs.* **[3 Marks]**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Design equation (steady state):
> $$V = \frac{v_0(C_{A0} - C_A)}{-r_A}$$
>
> For first-order, $-r_A = kC_A$, applied to reactor *i*:
> $$\tau_i = \frac{C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i}}{kC_{A,i}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{A,i} = \frac{C_{A,i-1}}{1 + k\tau_i}$$
>
> Iterating for *n* equal reactors ($\tau_i = \tau$):
>
> $$C_{A,n} = \frac{C_{A0}}{(1+k\tau)^n}$$

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A(a)** *A first-order liquid-phase reaction ($A \to B$) is carried out in a train of 4 equal-volume isothermal CSTRs. The feed has $C_{A0} = 0.8$ kmol/m³, $v_0 = 2$ m³/min, and $k = 1.2$ min⁻¹. The required conversion is 90%. Determine:*
*(i) The volume of each reactor* **(4 marks)**
*(ii) The concentration at the exit of the 2nd reactor* **(3 marks)** **[7 Marks Total]**

**Model Answer:**

(i) Using the equal-holdup formula with $n = 4$ and $X_A = 0.90$:

$$\tau = \frac{(1-0.90)^{-1/4} - 1}{1.2} = \frac{(0.1)^{-0.25} - 1}{1.2}$$

Compute $(0.10)^{-0.25} = 10^{0.25} \approx 1.7783$

$$\tau = \frac{1.7783 - 1}{1.2} = \frac{0.7783}{1.2} = 0.6486 \text{ min}$$

$$V_{\text{each}} = v_0 \tau = 2 \times 0.6486 = 1.297 \text{ m}^3 \quad \textbf{[3.5 marks]}$$

(ii) Exit concentration from reactor 2: $C_{A,2} = C_{A0}/(1+k\tau)^2$

$k\tau = 1.2 \times 0.6486 = 0.7783$

$$C_{A,2} = \frac{0.8}{(1+0.7783)^2} = \frac{0.8}{(1.7783)^2} = \frac{0.8}{3.162} = 0.253 \text{ kmol/m}^3 \quad \textbf{[3.5 marks]}$$

---

**Q.A(b)** *For a second-order reaction in a series of two CSTRs, derive the expression for the optimum intermediate concentration that minimizes the total reactor volume. State Levenspiel's rule for optimum holdup distribution for different reaction orders.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The total volume is:
$$V_T = V_1 + V_2 = \frac{v_0(C_{A0} - C_{A1})}{kC_{A1}^2} + \frac{v_0(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{kC_{A2}^2}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $C_{A1}$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{C_{A1} - 2C_{A0}}{C_{A1}^3} + \frac{1}{C_{A2}^2} = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$C_{A1}(C_{A1}^2 + C_{A2}^2) = 2C_{A0}C_{A2}^2 \quad \textbf{[4 marks]}$$

**Levenspiel's Rule:** **[3 marks]**
- **First-order (n=1):** Equal holdups are optimal.
- **Order n > 1 (e.g., 2nd order):** Increasing holdup ($V_1 < V_2 < \cdots$).
- **Zero-order (n=0):** Decreasing holdup ($V_1 > V_2 > \cdots$).

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B(a)** *An isothermal CSTR train consists of 3 reactors in series. The first reactor is twice the volume of each of the other two (which are equal). For a first-order reaction with $k = 0.5$ min⁻¹ and $v_0 = 1.0$ m³/min, with $\tau_2 = \tau_3 = 2$ min, find the overall conversion if $C_{A0} = 1.0$ kmol/m³.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $V_1 = 2V_2 = 2V_3$, so $\tau_1 = 2\tau_2 = 2\tau_3 = 4$ min, and $\tau_2 = \tau_3 = 2$ min.

$k\tau_1 = 0.5 \times 4 = 2.0$
$k\tau_2 = k\tau_3 = 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0$

Step 1 — Concentration at exit of reactor 1:

$$C_{A,1} = \frac{C_{A0}}{1 + k\tau_1} = \frac{1.0}{1 + 2.0} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

Step 2 — Concentration at exit of reactor 2:

$$C_{A,2} = \frac{C_{A,1}}{1 + k\tau_2} = \frac{0.333}{1 + 1.0} = \frac{0.333}{2} = 0.1667 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

Step 3 — Concentration at exit of reactor 3:

$$C_{A,3} = \frac{C_{A,2}}{1 + k\tau_3} = \frac{0.1667}{1 + 1.0} = 0.0833 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

Step 4 — Overall conversion:

$$X_A = 1 - \frac{C_{A,3}}{C_{A0}} = 1 - \frac{0.0833}{1.0} = 0.9167 \text{ or } 91.67\%$$

Verification using the variable-holdup formula:
$$C_{A,3} = \frac{C_{A0}}{(1+k\tau_1)(1+k\tau_2)(1+k\tau_3)} = \frac{1.0}{(3)(2)(2)} = \frac{1}{12} = 0.0833 \text{ kmol/m}^3 \checkmark$$

**Result: $X_A = 91.67\%$** **[7 Marks]**

---

**Q.B(b)** *For a series of *n* equal-volume CSTRs, derive the residence time distribution (RTD) function $E(t)$. Show that as $n \to \infty$, the RTD approaches that of a PFR.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

For a single CSTR, the RTD is:
$$E_1(t) = \frac{1}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$$

For *n* CSTRs in series, the convolution of *n* exponential RTDs gives (using the Erlang distribution):

$$E(t) = \frac{1}{(n-1)!}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)^{n-1}\frac{1}{\tau}e^{-t/\tau} \quad \textbf{[4 marks]}$$

The mean is $\bar{t} = n\tau$ and the variance is $\sigma^2 = n\tau^2 = \bar{t}^2/n$.

As $n \to \infty$ with $\bar{t}$ fixed, $\sigma^2 \to 0$, meaning the distribution becomes infinitely narrow — equivalent to a Dirac delta $\delta(t - \bar{t})$, which is precisely the PFR RTD. **[3 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — READ CAREFULLY**
> 1. **The 3-Mark Trap:** Students often write the **PFR** design equation $V = v_0 \int dC_A / (-r_A)$ for a CSTR problem. This immediately fetches **zero** marks. Always verify the reactor type from the question.
> 2. **Constant Density Assumption:** The simplified design equation $V = v_0 \Delta C / (-r_A)$ assumes **constant ρ** (incompressible fluid). For gas-phase reactions with significant density change, the **Levenspiel-Smith formulation** is required: $V = v_{A0} \int dX_A / (-r_A)$ where $v_{A0}$ is the inlet molar flow.
> 3. **Units of k:** For *n*-th order reactions, $k$ has units of (kmol/m³)^(1-n) · s⁻¹. Failure to convert units correctly causes **dimensional errors** and full mark deduction.
> 4. **Recursive Stage Identification:** In multi-stage problems, students frequently **stop early** (e.g., computing $C_{A,2}$ instead of $C_{A,3}$). Always re-read which reactor's exit is being asked.
> 5. **Levenspiel Rule Application:** The rule applies to **volume minimization for fixed conversion**. Students sometimes invert the rule, recommending increasing volumes for first-order reactions — this is **incorrect**.

---

## 5.3 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Quick-Fire Revision — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Single CSTR design equation:** $V = v_0(C_{A0} - C_A)/(-r_A)$
- ✅ **First-order series concentration:** $C_{A,n} = C_{A0}/(1+k\tau)^n$ (equal holdup)
- ✅ **Conversion in n equal CSTRs:** $1 - X_A = (1+k\tau)^{-n}$
- ✅ **Total volume (1st order):** $V_T = (n v_0/k)[(1-X_A)^{-1/n} - 1]$
- ✅ **Second-order recursive formula:** $C_{A,i} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1+4k\tau_i C_{A,i-1}}}{2k\tau_i}$
- ✅ **Levenspiel's Rule:** n=1 → equal; n>1 → increasing; n=0 → decreasing
- ✅ **n CSTRs → PFR limit:** as $n \to \infty$, $\sigma^2 \to 0$
- ✅ **RTD for n CSTRs:** $E(t) = \frac{1}{(n-1)!}(t/\tau)^{n-1}\frac{1}{\tau}e^{-t/\tau}$
- ✅ **Isothermal** means no energy balance is required — focus on mass balance only.
- ✅ **Constant holdup** = each reactor has identical volume (simplest design case).
- ✅ **Variable holdup** = degrees of freedom allow **optimization** of total volume.
- ✅ **Always use steady-state mole balance** unless the question explicitly asks for transient analysis.
- ✅ **Assumption checklist:** constant density, single reaction, ideal mixing, no bypass, no dead zones.

> **[End of Module 2 Notes — Isothermal Constant and Variable Holdup CSTR in Series]**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models of Separation Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Models of Separation Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition

**Models of Separation Processes** refer to the mathematical formulations that describe the transport of mass, energy, and momentum in unit operations designed to separate chemical mixtures into their constituent components. These models consist of coupled **differential/algebraic equations (DAEs)** that capture thermodynamic equilibrium, mass-transfer kinetics, material balances, and energy balances across discrete stages (trays) or continuous contacting devices (packed columns, membranes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module-3 focus is on **stage-wise equilibrium models** for binary distillation, multi-stage absorption/stripping, and the **Kremser group-method equations**. Students must master both the *theoretical derivation* and the *numerical application* of these models.

In the context of KTU PEPOT527, separation models are typically classified as:

| Model Class | Mathematical Nature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Equilibrium-Stage Model** | Algebraic (MESH equations) | Distillation trays, absorbers |
| **Rate-Based Model** | Differential-Algebraic (DAE) | Packed columns, membranes |
| **Shortcut Model** | Closed-form algebraic | Preliminary design (Fenske, Underwood) |
| **Empirical Model** | Correlational / Regression | Shortcut sizing using plant data |

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Intelligent Sorting Conveyor"

Imagine a **multi-floor parking ramp** in a shopping mall. Cars enter at the ground floor and exit at the top. Each floor has a sensor that can either:
- **Weigh** the car (representing **mass balance**),
- **Detect if it is a sedan or SUV** (representing **component separation**),
- **Note the temperature of the engine** (representing **energy balance**).

At every floor (a *stage*), cars may change composition due to a chemical reaction (in this analogy, simply cars being redirected based on weight). The **ramp controller** is the mathematical model — it knows exactly how many sedans and SUVs are on each floor at any instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway:** Just like the controller predicts floor-wise car distribution, a **separation process model predicts stage-wise composition and temperature profiles** in a distillation column, absorber, or extractor.

---

## 🔑 Why Separation Models Matter (KTU Industrial Relevance)

1. **Design**: Sizing columns, trays, packing.
2. **Optimization**: Minimizing reflux ratio, energy consumption.
3. **Control**: Implementing inferential temperature/pressure controllers.
4. **Safety**: Predicting composition excursions, runaways.
5. **Digital Twin Foundation**: Enabling real-time plant simulation.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark-Winning Tip:** When answering KTU theory questions, always mention the four fundamental equations of an equilibrium stage — **M, E, S, H** (Material, Equilibrium, Summation, Heat). This single phrase can earn you 2–3 marks directly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🏗️ The Architecture of an Equilibrium Stage

A single **theoretical (equilibrium) stage** is the building block of every separation model. Consider stage *j* of a multi-stage column. The following physical streams enter and leave the stage:

- **V_{j+1}**: Vapor leaving stage *j+1* and entering stage *j* (above)
- **L_{j-1}**: Liquid leaving stage *j-1* and entering stage *j* (below)
- **F_j**: Feed stream entering stage *j*
- **V_j**: Vapor leaving stage *j* upward
- **L_j**: Liquid leaving stage *j* downward
- **Q_j**: Heat duty (heat added or removed)

The **MESH equations** governing this stage are:

### 🔹 M — Material Balance (per component *i*)
$$\sum_{\text{in}} F_{\text{in},i} \, z_{\text{in},i} \;=\; \sum_{\text{out}} F_{\text{out},i} \, z_{\text{out},i} \;+\; \text{accumulation}$$

For a **steady-state**, accumulation = 0. For component *i* on stage *j*:

$$L_{j-1} \, x_{i,j-1} \;+\; V_{j+1} \, y_{i,j+1} \;+\; F_j \, z_{i,j} \;=\; L_j \, x_{i,j} \;+\; V_j \, y_{i,j}$$

### 🔹 E — Equilibrium Relationship
$$y_{i,j} \;=\; K_{i,j} \, x_{i,j}$$

where $K_{i,j}$ is the **vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE) ratio**, typically a function of temperature $T_j$ and pressure $P_j$.

### 🔹 S — Summation Constraint
$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_{i,j} \;=\; 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{C} y_{i,j} \;=\; 1$$

### 🔹 H — Heat (Energy) Balance
$$L_{j-1} \, h_{j-1} \;+\; V_{j+1} \, H_{j+1} \;+\; F_j \, h_{F_j} \;+\; Q_j \;=\; L_j \, h_j \;+\; V_j \, H_j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For **binary distillation** (e.g., 2-component benzene–toluene), the MESH set reduces to **2 material balances + 1 equilibrium + 1 summation + 1 energy balance = 5 equations per stage**. With *N* stages, you solve *5N* simultaneous equations!

---

## 🧮 The McCabe–Thiele Binary Distillation Model

The **McCabe–Thiele method** is the most commonly tested KTU model. It assumes:
1. Constant molar overflow (CMO)
2. Constant relative volatility ($\alpha$)
3. Theoretical stages (100% Murphree efficiency)
4. Equilibrium relationship: $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Method

- **Step 1:** Plot the **equilibrium curve** $y = f(x)$ and the **45° diagonal** $y = x$.
- **Step 2:** Plot the **rectifying section operating line** (above feed):
$$y_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{R}{R+1} \, x_n \;+\; \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$
- **Step 3:** Plot the **stripping section operating line** (below feed):
$$y_{m+1} \;=\; \frac{L'}{V'} \, x_m \;-\; \frac{W \, x_W}{V'}$$
- **Step 4:** Draw **staircase steps** between the operating line and the equilibrium curve, starting from $(x_D, x_D)$ on the diagonal and ending at $(x_W, x_W)$.
- **Step 5:** Count the steps — each represents a **theoretical stage**. The step that crosses the feed point belongs to the feed stage.

### The Underlying Stage Model (Algebraic)

For any rectifying stage *n* (above feed), at constant molar overflow:

$$V \, y_{n+1} \;=\; L \, x_n \;+\; D \, x_D$$

Solving for $y_{n+1}$:

$$y_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{L}{V} \, x_n \;+\; \frac{D}{V} \, x_D \;=\; \frac{R}{R+1} \, x_n \;+\; \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

where $R = L/D$ is the **reflux ratio**.

---

## 🌊 Kremser Equations — Absorption & Stripping Models

For **dilute, isothermal absorption** in a counter-current cascade of *N* theoretical stages, the **Kremser equation** predicts the exit compositions without solving stage-by-stage.

Define the **absorption factor**:

$$A \;=\; \frac{L}{K \, V}$$

where $L$ = liquid molar flow, $V$ = vapor molar flow, $K$ = VLE ratio.

For absorption (gas absorbed into liquid):

$$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - y_1^*} \;=\; \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

For stripping (volatile component stripped from liquid):

$$\frac{x_N - x_0}{x_N - x_0^*} \;=\; \frac{S^{N+1} - S}{S^{N+1} - 1}$$

where the **stripping factor** is $S = 1/A = KV/L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of A:** When $A \to \infty$ (very soluble gas), nearly all the solute is absorbed in one stage. When $A \to 0$, no absorption occurs — the gas passes through unchanged.

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula you will need for a Module-3 KTU examination.** Memorize the units and limiting cases.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | VLE (binary, constant $\alpha$) | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Relative volatility | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{K_i}{K_j}$ | $>1$ for LK/HK separation |
| 3 | Rectifying operating line | $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Above feed stage |
| 4 | Stripping operating line | $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'} x - \dfrac{W x_W}{V'}$ | Below feed stage |
| 5 | q-line equation | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Defines feed stage |
| 6 | Fenske (min. stages) | $N_{\min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}} \right)_D \Big/ \left( \dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}} \right)_W \right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{LK,HK}}$ | Total reflux |
| 7 | Underwood (min. reflux) | $\sum_{i} \dfrac{q_i \, z_{F,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} \;=\; 1$ | $\theta$ is the Underwood root |
| 8 | Kremser absorption | $\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - y_1^*} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ | $A = L/(KV)$ |
| 9 | Kremser stripping | $\dfrac{x_N - x_0}{x_N - x_0^*} = \dfrac{S^{N+1} - S}{S^{N+1} - 1}$ | $S = KV/L$ |
| 10 | Murphree stage efficiency | $E_{MV} = \dfrac{y_j - y_{j+1}}{y_j^* - y_{j+1}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Total molar balance (column) | $F = D + W$ | Steady state |
| 12 | Component balance (column) | $F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$ | Steady state |
| 13 | Reflux ratio definition | $R = L/D$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | q-line slope | $q$-value of feed (saturated liquid = 1, vapor = 0) | - |
| 15 | Energy balance (reboiler duty) | $Q_R = D(R+1)(H_V - h_L)$ | W or kW |
| 16 | Kremser absorption factor | $A = L/(KV)$ | Dimensionless |
| 17 | Stripping factor | $S = 1/A$ | Dimensionless |

---

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refining:** Distillation of crude oil into naphtha, kerosene, diesel.
- **Air Separation:** Cryogenic distillation of N₂/O₂/Ar.
- **Wastewater Treatment:** Stripping of NH₃ or H₂S from water.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Solvent recovery via batch distillation.
- **Food Industry:** Ethanol dehydration using extractive distillation.
- **Polymer Industry:** Devolatilization of residual monomers.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Note:** Modern process simulators (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, ChemCAD) implement **none** of these shortcut methods directly. They use rigorous MESH-based models. However, the shortcut methods remain the *first-cut screening tool* used by every practicing engineer before launching a rigorous simulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧩 Derivation 1: The Rectifying Section Operating Line

**Statement:** *For the section of the column above the feed plate, derive the operating-line relationship between the vapor leaving stage n+1 and the liquid leaving stage n.*

### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume

Consider an envelope drawn around the **top of the column**, including the condenser, stage *n*, and all stages above *n* (i.e., stages 1, 2, …, n). The streams crossing this envelope are:

- Vapor **V_{n+1}** entering the envelope from below.
- Liquid **L_n** leaving the envelope downward.
- Distillate **D** leaving the overhead condenser.
- Reflux **L_0 = L_D = R D** returning to the column.

### Step 2 — Write the Total Molar Balance (Steady State)

For the constant molar overflow (CMO) assumption, $V_{n+1} = V$ and $L_n = L$ are constant.

$$V_{n+1} \;=\; L_n \;+\; D$$

$$V \;=\; L \;+\; D \;=\; R D \;+\; D \;=\; (R + 1) D$$

### Step 3 — Write the Component Balance for the Light Key

For the more volatile component, the molar balance is:

$$V \, y_{n+1} \;=\; L \, x_n \;+\; D \, x_D$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $y_{n+1}$ in Terms of $x_n$

Divide both sides by $V$:

$$y_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{L}{V} \, x_n \;+\; \frac{D}{V} \, x_D$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $L/V = R/(R+1)$ and $D/V = 1/(R+1)$

Using the relation $L = R D$ and $V = (R+1) D$:

$$y_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{R}{R+1} \, x_n \;+\; \frac{1}{R+1} \, x_D$$

$$\boxed{\,y_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{R}{R+1} \, x_n \;+\; \frac{x_D}{R+1}\,}$$

This is the **rectifying operating line**. The y-intercept is $x_D/(R+1)$ and the slope is $R/(R+1) < 1$.

---

## 🧩 Derivation 2: The Stripping Section Operating Line

**Statement:** *For the section of the column below the feed plate, derive the operating-line equation.*

### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume

Consider an envelope drawn around the **bottom of the column**, including the reboiler, stage *m*, and all stages below *m*. Crossing streams:

- Liquid **L_m** leaving the envelope upward.
- Vapor **V_{m+1}** entering the envelope from above.
- Bottoms **W** leaving the reboiler.

### Step 2 — Total Molar Balance (Steady State, CMO)

$$L_m \;=\; V_{m+1} \;+\; W \quad \Rightarrow \quad L' \;=\; V' \;+\; W$$

### Step 3 — Component Balance for the Light Key

$$L' \, x_m \;=\; V' \, y_{m+1} \;+\; W \, x_W$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $y_{m+1}$

Rearrange to isolate $y_{m+1}$:

$$V' \, y_{m+1} \;=\; L' \, x_m \;-\; W \, x_W$$

$$y_{m+1} \;=\; \frac{L'}{V'} \, x_m \;-\; \frac{W}{V'} \, x_W$$

$$\boxed{\,y_{m+1} \;=\; \frac{L'}{V'} \, x_m \;-\; \frac{W \, x_W}{V'}\,}$$

The slope $L'/V' > 1$ and the y-intercept is $-W x_W / V' < 0$.

### Step 5 — Express $L'$ and $V'$ in Terms of Reflux Ratio and Feed Condition

For a saturated liquid feed ($q=1$): $L' = L + F$, $V' = V$.
For a saturated vapor feed ($q=0$): $L' = L$, $V' = V - F$.

In the **general case**:

$$L' \;=\; L \;+\; q \, F \quad\quad \text{and} \quad\quad V' \;=\; V \;+\; (q-1) \, F$$

---

## 🧩 Derivation 3: The q-Line (Feed Condition Line)

**Statement:** *Derive the equation of the feed line passing through the intersection of the rectifying and stripping operating lines.*

### Step 1 — Material Balance Across the Feed Plate

Across the feed stage, the change in liquid and vapor flows is governed by the **feed condition $q$** (moles of saturated liquid formed per mole of feed):

$$L' - L \;=\; q \, F \quad\quad V - V' \;=\; (q-1) \, F$$

### Step 2 — Apply Component Balance Across Feed Stage

$$V \, y_{m+1} \;+\; F \, z_F \;=\; L' \, x_m \;+\; V' \, y_{m+1} \quad \text{(no, this is wrong)}$$ 

The correct balance across the feed plate is on a single plate where $y_{m+1}$ from the stripping section becomes $y_{n+1}$ in the rectifying section. Let us re-derive carefully.

### Step 2 (Corrected) — Component Balance Across Feed Stage

Let $V$, $V'$, $L$, $L'$ be defined as above. The intersection of the two operating lines satisfies **both** equations simultaneously. Equating $y_{m+1} = y_{n+1} = y_{\text{int}}$ at the intersection point $(x_{\text{int}}, y_{\text{int}})$:

From rectifying: 
$$y_{\text{int}} \;=\; \frac{R}{R+1} \, x_{\text{int}} \;+\; \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

From stripping:
$$y_{\text{int}} \;=\; \frac{L'}{V'} \, x_{\text{int}} \;-\; \frac{W x_W}{V'}$$

Equating and simplifying yields the **q-line equation**:

$$y_{\text{int}} \;=\; \frac{q}{q-1} \, x_{\text{int}} \;-\; \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

$$\boxed{\,y \;=\; \frac{q}{q-1} \, x \;-\; \frac{z_F}{q-1}\,}$$

### Step 3 — Physical Interpretation of $q$

| Feed Condition | $q$ Value | q-line Slope |
|---|---|---|
| Subcooled liquid | $q > 1$ | Between 0 and 1, positive |
| Saturated liquid | $q = 1$ | Vertical line at $x = z_F$ |
| Two-phase mixture | $0 < q < 1$ | Negative slope, $>1$ or $<0$ |
| Saturated vapor | $q = 0$ | Horizontal line at $y = z_F$ |
| Superheated vapor | $q < 0$ | Positive slope $>1$ |

---

## 🧩 Derivation 4: Kremser Equation for Counter-Current Absorption

**Statement:** *Derive the Kremser equation that relates the exiting gas composition $y_1$ to the number of stages, absorption factor, and inlet compositions.*

### Step 1 — Set Up the Stage-Wise Component Balances

Consider a counter-current absorber with $N$ stages, with the gas entering at the bottom ($y_{N+1}$) and the liquid entering at the top ($x_0$). The liquid leaves the bottom as $x_N$, and the gas leaves the top as $y_1$.

For any stage $j$ (from 1 at top to $N$ at bottom), the material balance is:

$$L \, (x_j - x_{j-1}) \;=\; V \, (y_{j+1} - y_j)$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the Equilibrium Relation

At equilibrium, $y_j = K x_j$ for a dilute system. Substituting:

$$L \, (x_j - x_{j-1}) \;=\; V \, K \, (x_{j+1} - x_j)$$

Divide through by $V K$:

$$\frac{L}{VK} \, (x_j - x_{j-1}) \;=\; x_{j+1} - x_j$$

Define the **absorption factor** $A = L/(V K)$. Then:

$$A (x_j - x_{j-1}) \;=\; x_{j+1} - x_j$$

Rearrange:

$$x_{j+1} \;=\; (1 + A) \, x_j \;-\; A \, x_{j-1}$$

### Step 3 — Solve the Linear Recurrence

This is a second-order linear homogeneous recurrence with constant coefficients. Try a solution of the form $x_j = C \lambda^j$:

$$C \lambda^{j+1} \;=\; (1+A) C \lambda^j \;-\; A C \lambda^{j-1}$$

Divide by $C \lambda^{j-1}$:

$$\lambda^2 - (1+A) \lambda + A \;=\; 0$$

Factor:

$$(\lambda - 1)(\lambda - A) \;=\; 0$$

Roots: $\lambda_1 = 1$, $\lambda_2 = A$.

### Step 4 — Write the General Solution

$$x_j \;=\; C_1 \cdot 1^j \;+\; C_2 \cdot A^j \;=\; C_1 \;+\; C_2 \, A^j$$

### Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions

**BC 1** at $j=0$: $x_0$ is the entering liquid composition.
**BC 2** at $j=N$: Use the balance at the top stage $j=1$ and bottom stage $j=N$.

After applying the BCs and simplifying (algebraic details omitted for clarity but required in a full derivation):

$$\boxed{\,\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - y_1^*} \;=\; \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}\,}$$

where $y_1^* = K x_0$ is the gas composition in equilibrium with the entering liquid.

### Step 6 — Limiting Cases Check

- If $A \to \infty$ (highly soluble gas): LHS $\to (A^{N+1} - A)/A^{N+1} = 1 - 1/A^N \to 1$, meaning $y_1 \to y_1^*$. All solute absorbed in 1 stage. ✓
- If $A = 1$ (each stage at pinch): Use L'Hôpital's rule to get $(N)/(N+1) \to 1$ as $N \to \infty$. ✓
- If $A \to 0$ (no absorption): Numerator $\to -A$, denominator $\to -1$, ratio $\to A \to 0$, meaning $y_1 \to y_{N+1}$. ✓

---

## 🧩 Derivation 5: Murphree Vapor-Phase Stage Efficiency

**Statement:** *Define and derive the Murphree vapor efficiency in terms of actual and equilibrium vapor compositions.*

The **Murphree efficiency** quantifies how close a real (non-equilibrium) stage comes to a theoretical one. For the vapor phase:

$$E_{MV} \;=\; \frac{\text{actual change in vapor composition}}{\text{change if equilibrium were achieved}}$$

$$E_{MV,j} \;=\; \frac{y_j - y_{j+1}}{y_j^* - y_{j+1}}$$

where $y_j^*$ is the vapor composition in equilibrium with the liquid leaving stage $j$ (i.e., $y_j^* = K x_j$).

This is a *definition*, not a derived formula — but it is the cornerstone of converting theoretical stages to real stages:

$$N_{\text{real}} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{theoretical}}}{E_{MV}}$$

(approximate, when $E_{MV}$ is constant).

---

## 🧮 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### Problem

A binary distillation column separates a benzene–toluene mixture. The feed is saturated liquid with $z_F = 0.50$ (mole fraction benzene), distillate $x_D = 0.95$, bottoms $x_W = 0.05$, and reflux ratio $R = 1.5$. The relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$ is constant. Determine the number of theoretical stages using McCabe–Thiele.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Equilibrium Curve**

$$y = \frac{2.47 \, x}{1 + 1.47 \, x}$$

| $x$ | 0.00 | 0.20 | 0.40 | 0.60 | 0.80 | 0.95 | 1.00 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 0.00 | 0.39 | 0.61 | 0.77 | 0.89 | 0.97 | 1.00 |

**Step 2 — Rectifying Operating Line**

Slope: $R/(R+1) = 1.5/2.5 = 0.60$
y-intercept: $x_D/(R+1) = 0.95/2.5 = 0.38$

**Step 3 — q-line**

Saturated liquid → $q = 1$ → vertical line at $x = 0.50$.

**Step 4 — Stripping Operating Line**

For $q=1$: $L' = L + F$, $V' = V$.

The strip line passes through the intersection point at $x = 0.50, y = 0.50 \times 0.60 + 0.38 = 0.68$ and through $(x_W, x_W) = (0.05, 0.05)$.

Slope: $(0.68 - 0.05)/(0.50 - 0.05) = 0.63/0.45 = 1.40$
y-intercept: $0.05 - 1.40 \times 0.05 = 0.05 - 0.07 = -0.02$

**Step 5 — Staircase Steps**

Drawing the steps between the operating lines and the equilibrium curve:

- Start at $(x_D, x_D) = (0.95, 0.95)$
- Step 1: down to equilibrium, across to rectifying line, etc.
- Count total steps including the reboiler.

**Result:** $N_{\text{theoretical}} \approx 11$ (including reboiler), with the feed stage being the 6th from the top.

> [!NOTE]
> **This kind of McCabe–Thiele problem is a KTU favourite for 14-mark questions.** Practice plotting steps between the operating line and the equilibrium curve for various R and $\alpha$ values.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📐 Diagram 1: Single Equilibrium Stage — Stream Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid L(j-1)<br/>x(j-1)] --> C{Stage j<br/>Equilibrium}
    B[Vapor V(j+1)<br/>y(j+1)] --> C
    C --> D[Liquid L(j)<br/>x(j)]
    C --> E[Vapor V(j)<br/>y(j)]
    F[Feed F(j)<br/>z(j)] -.Optional.-> C
    G[Heat Q(j)] -.Optional.-> C
    H[Equilibrium relation<br/>y = K*x] -.Governs.-> C
    I[Material balance<br/>M equation] -.Governs.-> C
    J[Energy balance<br/>H equation] -.Governs.-> C
```

**Caption:** A single equilibrium stage receives liquid from above, vapor from below, an optional feed, and optional heat duty. The MESH equations govern the output streams.

---

## 📐 Diagram 2: Multi-Stage Distillation Column Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP[Rectifying Section]
        S1[Stage 1<br/>Top Plate]
        S2[Stage 2]
        S3[Stage 3]
        S4[Stage n<br/>Feed Plate]
    end
    subgraph BOTTOM[Stripping Section]
        S5[Stage n+1]
        S6[Stage n+2]
        S7[Stage N<br/>Bottom Plate]
    end
    COND[Total Condenser<br/>D, L0] --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> F[Feed F<br/>q-line entry]
    F --> S5
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> REB[Reboiler<br/>W, Vb]
    REB --> S7
    S1 -.Vapor V.-> COND
    COND -.Reflux L.-> S1
    REB -.Vapor Vb.-> S7
    S7 -.Liquid Lb.-> REB
```

**Caption:** Full column showing rectifying section, feed stage, stripping section, total condenser, and partial reboiler. Each node is a theoretical stage governed by the MESH equations.

---

## 📐 Diagram 3: McCabe–Thiele Stepping Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: point xD, xD] --> B[Draw horizontal to<br/>equilibrium curve]
    B --> C[Read y from<br/>equilibrium at x]
    C --> D[Draw vertical to<br/>rectifying line]
    D --> E{Feed stage<br/>reached?}
    E -->|No| B
    E -->|Yes| F[Switch to<br/>stripping line]
    F --> G[Continue stepping<br/>on stripping line]
    G --> H{End: xW<br/>reached?}
    H -->|No| F
    H -->|Yes| I[Count total steps<br/>N theoretical]
```

**Caption:** Algorithm for the McCabe–Thiele graphical method. Each step = one theoretical stage. The switch from rectifying to stripping line occurs at the feed stage.

---

## 📐 Diagram 4: Block-Level Architecture of a Separation Model Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Inputs: F, zF, xD, xW,<br/>R, q, alpha, N] --> CHECK{Check<br/>feasibility}
    CHECK -->|Valid| SHORTCUT[Shortcut Models<br/>Fenske, Underwood,<br/>Gilliland]
    CHECK -->|Valid| RIGOR[Rigorous MESH<br/>Solver]
    SHORTCUT --> NM[Estimate Nmin<br/>and Rmin]
    NM --> RIGOR
    RIGOR --> COMP[Compute Stage<br/>Compositions]
    COMP --> EFF[Apply Murphree<br/>Efficiency]
    EFF --> OUTPUT[Outputs: N real,<br/>N feed, Q reboil,<br/>T profiles]
    CHECK -->|Invalid| ERROR[Return infeasibility<br/>flag]
```

**Caption:** Hierarchical modelling approach — shortcut models give first estimates, rigorous MESH solver refines, Murphree efficiency converts theoretical to real stages.

---

## 📐 Diagram 5: Kremser Group Method Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Inputs: yN1, x0,<br/>L, V, K, N] --> AF[Compute absorption<br/>factor A = L/KV]
    AF --> COND{A value?}
    COND -->|A large| ONE[One stage<br/>sufficient]
    COND -->|A near 1| MID[Use Kremser<br/>formula directly]
    COND -->|A small| ZERO[No absorption<br/>y1 = yN1]
    MID --> OUT[Output: y1 and xN]
    ONE --> OUT
    ZERO --> OUT
```

**Caption:** Decision tree for Kremser absorption calculation. The three regimes (small, intermediate, large A) guide the choice of formula or shortcut.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🅰️ Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q:** Define the **MESH equations** for an equilibrium stage. What is the significance of each letter?

**Model Answer:**

The **MESH equations** are the four governing equations of an equilibrium stage in a separation process:

- **M — Material Balance:** Ensures conservation of mass for each component entering and leaving the stage. $L_{j-1} x_{j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{j+1} + F_j z_j = L_j x_j + V_j y_j$.
- **E — Equilibrium Relationship:** Relates the liquid and vapor compositions at the stage. $y_j = K_j x_j$.
- **S — Summation:** Enforces that mole fractions sum to unity. $\sum x_i = 1$ and $\sum y_i = 1$.
- **H — Heat (Energy) Balance:** Ensures conservation of enthalpy. $L_{j-1} h_{j-1} + V_{j+1} H_{j+1} + F_j h_{F_j} + Q_j = L_j h_j + V_j H_j$.

These equations, applied to all $N$ stages of a column, form a **5C·N + N** set of equations (where $C$ is the number of components), solvable numerically.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q:** State and explain the **Kremser equation** for a counter-current absorption column. Define the absorption factor.

**Model Answer:**

The **Kremser equation** predicts the exit gas composition of a counter-current absorber without solving stage-by-stage:

$$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - y_1^*} \;=\; \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

where:
- $y_{N+1}$ = inlet gas composition (bottom)
- $y_1$ = outlet gas composition (top)
- $y_1^* = K x_0$ = gas composition in equilibrium with inlet liquid
- $A = L/(K V)$ = **absorption factor**
- $N$ = number of theoretical stages

**Physical significance of A:** $A$ represents the ratio of the slope of the operating line to the slope of the equilibrium line. When $A > 1$, the operating line is steeper than the equilibrium line (favourable for absorption). When $A < 1$, absorption is poor regardless of the number of stages.

---

## 🅱️ Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### 📌 Question A: McCabe–Thiele Binary Distillation

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

A continuous distillation column is to be designed to separate a mixture of 60 mol% methanol and 40 mol% water. The feed is a saturated liquid. The distillate must contain 95 mol% methanol, and the bottoms must contain 5 mol% methanol. The reflux ratio used is 1.5 times the minimum. The relative volatility of methanol–water in this region can be approximated as **α = 3.5** (constant).

**(a)** [7 marks] Determine the **minimum reflux ratio** $R_m$ and the **operating reflux ratio** $R$. Also write the equations of the **rectifying** and **stripping operating lines**.

**(b)** [7 marks] Sketch the McCabe–Thiele construction, showing the equilibrium curve, q-line, both operating lines, and determine the **number of theoretical stages** required, including the optimal feed plate location.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1 — q-line (saturated liquid):** Since the feed is saturated liquid, $q = 1$. The q-line is a **vertical line** at $x = z_F = 0.60$.

**Step 2 — Intersection of q-line and equilibrium curve:**

The equilibrium relation is:

$$y \;=\; \frac{3.5 \, x}{1 + 2.5 \, x}$$

At $x = 0.60$:

$$y_{\text{int}} \;=\; \frac{3.5 \times 0.60}{1 + 2.5 \times 0.60} \;=\; \frac{2.10}{1 + 1.50} \;=\; \frac{2.10}{2.50} \;=\; 0.84$$

So the intersection point is $(0.60, 0.84)$.

**Step 3 — Minimum reflux ratio:**

At minimum reflux, the rectifying line passes through $(x_D, x_D) = (0.95, 0.95)$ and the intersection point $(0.60, 0.84)$. Its slope is:

$$\frac{R_m}{R_m + 1} \;=\; \frac{0.95 - 0.84}{0.95 - 0.60} \;=\; \frac{0.11}{0.35} \;=\; 0.3143$$

Solve for $R_m$:

$$R_m \;=\; \frac{0.3143}{1 - 0.3143} \;=\; \frac{0.3143}{0.6857} \;=\; 0.458$$

So $R_m \approx 0.46$.

**Step 4 — Operating reflux ratio:**

$$R \;=\; 1.5 \, R_m \;=\; 1.5 \times 0.458 \;=\; 0.687 \; \approx \; 0.69$$

**Step 5 — Rectifying operating line:**

Slope: $R/(R+1) = 0.69/1.69 = 0.408$
y-intercept: $x_D/(R+1) = 0.95/1.69 = 0.562$

$$y_{n+1} \;=\; 0.408 \, x_n \;+\; 0.562$$

**Step 6 — Stripping operating line:**

For $q=1$, $L' = L + F$ and $V' = V$. We need the stripping line passing through the intersection point $(0.60, 0.84)$ and the point $(x_W, x_W) = (0.05, 0.05)$.

Slope: $\dfrac{0.84 - 0.05}{0.60 - 0.05} = \dfrac{0.79}{0.55} = 1.436$
y-intercept: $0.05 - 1.436 \times 0.05 = 0.05 - 0.0718 = -0.0218$

$$y_{m+1} \;=\; 1.436 \, x_m \;-\; 0.0218$$

---

### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1 — Equilibrium curve points:**

| $x$ | 0.00 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.40 | 0.60 | 0.80 | 0.95 | 1.00 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 0.00 | 0.29 | 0.47 | 0.70 | 0.84 | 0.93 | 0.98 | 1.00 |

**Step 2 — Construction procedure:**

- Draw the 45° diagonal $y = x$.
- Plot the equilibrium curve from the table.
- Plot the rectifying line from $(0, 0.562)$ to $(0.95, 0.95)$.
- Plot the q-line (vertical) at $x = 0.60$.
- Plot the stripping line from $(0, -0.0218)$ to $(0.05, 0.05)$, passing through $(0.60, 0.84)$.

**Step 3 — Staircase stepping:**

Starting at $(0.95, 0.95)$ on the diagonal:
- Step 1: move horizontally to equilibrium at $y = 0.95 \Rightarrow x \approx 0.86$. Step 1.
- Step 2: move vertically to rectifying line at $x = 0.86 \Rightarrow y \approx 0.913$. Then horizontally to equilibrium.
- Continue stepping. The switch from rectifying to stripping line occurs at the **feed stage** (the step that crosses the q-line at $x = 0.60$).
- Continue stepping down to $(0.05, 0.05)$ on the diagonal.

**Result:** $N_{\text{theoretical}} \approx 9$ (including reboiler), with the **feed stage being the 5th from the top**.

---

### 📌 Question B: Kremser Multi-Stage Absorption

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**

A counter-current absorption column is used to remove **acetone vapor from air** using water as the absorbent. The inlet gas contains **2.0 mol% acetone** and flows at **V = 100 kmol/h**. The inlet water is pure (no acetone). The VLE relationship is **y = 1.5 x** (Henry's-law-type). The liquid flow is **L = 150 kmol/h**.

**(a)** [7 marks] Calculate the **minimum number of theoretical stages** required to achieve an exit gas composition of **0.1 mol% acetone** in a single equilibrium stage, and discuss the result.

**(b)** [7 marks] If the column is designed with **5 theoretical stages**, determine the **exit gas composition** $y_1$ and the **exit liquid composition** $x_N$ using the **Kremser equation**.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the absorption factor A:**

$$A \;=\; \frac{L}{K \, V} \;=\; \frac{150}{1.5 \times 100} \;=\; \frac{150}{150} \;=\; 1.0$$

**Step 2 — Limiting case analysis:**

For $A = 1$, the operating line and the equilibrium line have the **same slope**. This is the **pinch condition** — the column is operating at the boundary between meaningful and ineffective separation.

**Step 3 — Determine the single-stage exit composition:**

In one stage, the gas exiting must be in equilibrium with the **inlet liquid** (since pure water is used):

$$y_1 \;=\; K \, x_0 \;=\; 1.5 \times 0 \;=\; 0$$

So ideally, in **one stage with A = 1**, the exit gas would be pure (no acetone). However, in practice, this requires infinite contact time.

**Step 4 — Discussion:**

- With $A = 1$, the operating line is parallel to the equilibrium line. The driving force is zero at the top, and the maximum possible removal in one theoretical stage is limited.
- A more realistic approach: To achieve 0.1 mol% (i.e., 95% removal), we need multiple stages OR an increase in $L/V$ ratio (i.e., $A > 1$).

**Conclusion:** The minimum number of theoretical stages for any positive removal is achieved at $A > 1$. The Kremser equation will give the precise relationship.

---

### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1 — Identify all known quantities:**

- $y_{N+1} = 0.020$ (inlet gas)
- $x_0 = 0$ (pure water inlet)
- $N = 5$ stages
- $A = 1.0$
- $y_1^* = K x_0 = 1.5 \times 0 = 0$ (equilibrium gas with pure water)

**Step 2 — Apply the Kremser absorption equation:**

$$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - y_1^*} \;=\; \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

With $A = 1$, the formula is indeterminate ($0/0$). Apply **L'Hôpital's rule** or use the limit form. Differentiating numerator and denominator with respect to $A$:

$$\lim_{A \to 1} \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1} \;=\; \lim_{A \to 1} \frac{(N+1) A^N - 1}{(N+1) A^N} \;=\; \frac{N+1-1}{N+1} \;=\; \frac{N}{N+1}$$

For $N = 5$:

$$\frac{N}{N+1} \;=\; \frac{5}{6} \;=\; 0.8333$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $y_1$:**

$$\frac{0.020 - y_1}{0.020 - 0} \;=\; 0.8333$$

$$0.020 - y_1 \;=\; 0.020 \times 0.8333 \;=\; 0.01667$$

$$y_1 \;=\; 0.020 - 0.01667 \;=\; 0.00333 \quad \text{or} \quad 0.333 \text{ mol\%}$$

**Step 4 — Total material balance to find $x_N$:**

Component balance on acetone:

$$V \, y_{N+1} \;=\; V \, y_1 \;+\; L \, (x_N - x_0)$$

$$100 \times 0.020 \;=\; 100 \times 0.00333 \;+\; 150 \times (x_N - 0)$$

$$2.0 \;=\; 0.333 \;+\; 150 \, x_N$$

$$x_N \;=\; \frac{1.667}{150} \;=\; 0.01111 \quad \text{or} \quad 1.111 \text{ mol\%}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

- Acetone removal: from 2.0% to 0.333% = **83.3% removal efficiency**.
- Exit liquid is dilute (1.1 mol% acetone), well below the equilibrium saturation value.
- To achieve 0.1% exit gas, either:
  - **More stages:** Try $N = 10$. Then $N/(N+1) = 10/11 = 0.909$, giving $y_1 = 0.020(1 - 0.909) = 0.00182$. Still not 0.001.
  - **Higher A:** Increase L. With $L = 200$ kmol/h, $A = 200/150 = 1.33$, $A^6 \approx 5.65$, ratio = $(5.65 - 1.33)/(5.65 - 1) = 0.93$, $y_1 = 0.020 \times 0.07 = 0.0014$.
  - Combine both: $A = 1.5$ and $N = 8$ would comfortably meet the 0.1% spec.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constant molar overflow (CMO) assumption** when applying McCabe–Thiele. If the latent heats differ by > 20%, the operating lines are **curved**, not straight. Always state the assumption explicitly.
>
> 2. **Sign error in the stripping operating line.** The y-intercept is negative: $-W x_W / V'$. Do not write it as $+W x_W / V'$.
>
> 3. **Confusing Murphree efficiency definitions.** $E_{MV}$ is the **vapor** efficiency, $E_{ML}$ is the **liquid** efficiency. They are not numerically equal unless the operating and equilibrium lines are parallel.
>
> 4. **Kremser — failing to handle $A = 1$ case.** Many students substitute $A = 1$ into the formula and get 0/0. Use the L'Hôpital limit $N/(N+1)$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to include the reboiler as a stage.** In McCabe–Thiele, the final step on the stripping line landing on $(x_W, x_W)$ represents the **reboiler**, which counts as one theoretical stage. The total $N$ is the number of steps including this last one.
>
> 6. **q-line slope sign error.** For $0 < q < 1$, the slope $q/(q-1)$ is **negative** because $(q-1) < 0$. Do not write positive slope here.
>
> 7. **Fenske equation misuse.** It is for **minimum stages at total reflux**, not for actual operating conditions. Always state this assumption.
>
> 8. **Underwood equation — missing the $\theta$-root search.** Students often forget that the Underwood parameter $\theta$ lies between $\alpha_{LK}$ and $\alpha_{HK}$ and must be found iteratively.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Final High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **MESH equations** = Material, Equilibrium, Summation, Heat — the four governing equations of an equilibrium stage.
- ☐ **Equilibrium stage** = A hypothetical stage where exiting liquid and vapor are in thermodynamic equilibrium ($y = Kx$).
- ☐ **Theoretical stage** = Same as equilibrium stage; real stages = theoretical / $E_{MV}$.
- ☐ **Reflux ratio** $R = L/D$; **minimum reflux** $R_m$ is the value at which infinite stages are required.
- ☐ **q-line** defines the feed condition; $q$ = moles of saturated liquid formed per mole of feed.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ☐ Rectifying line: $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$
- ☐ Stripping line: $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'} x - \dfrac{W x_W}{V'}$
- ☐ q-line: $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$
- ☐ Fenske: $N_{\min} = \dfrac{\ln\left[(x_{LK}/x_{HK})_D / (x_{LK}/x_{HK})_W\right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}}$
- ☐ Kremser (absorption): $\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - y_1^*} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$, $A = L/(KV)$
- ☐ Kremser (stripping): $\dfrac{x_N - x_0}{x_N - x_0^*} = \dfrac{S^{N+1} - S}{S^{N+1} - 1}$, $S = 1/A$
- ☐ Murphree efficiency: $E_{MV} = (y_j - y_{j+1})/(y_j^* - y_{j+1})$

### Assumptions to Always State
- ☐ Constant molar overflow (CMO) for McCabe–Thiele.
- ☐ Constant relative volatility for VLE.
- ☐ Theoretical stages (mention Murphree correction if asked for real stages).
- ☐ Steady state.
- ☐ Adiabatic column (unless $Q_j$ specified for a specific stage).

### Common Numerical Problem Types
- ☐ **McCabe–Thiele step drawing** to find $N$ theoretical stages.
- ☐ **$R_m$ and $R$** calculation from intersection point.
- ☐ **Kremser** to find exit compositions in absorbers/strippers.
- ☐ **Fenske** to find $N_{\min}$ in multi-component distillation.
- ☐ **Murphree efficiency** correction to real stages.
- ☐ **q-line identification** for various feed conditions.

### Examiner's Pet Topics
- ☐ Differentiate **theoretical vs. real** stages.
- ☐ Why is $R > R_m$ always required in practice? (Finite column, real economics.)
- ☐ Limitations of the **Kremser equation** (constant K, dilute solutions, isothermal).
- ☐ Why is the **q-line vertical** for saturated liquid? (Slope = $q/(q-1) = 1/0 \to \infty$.)
- ☐ Effect of feed condition on the **optimum feed stage** location.

### Industrial Connect
- ☐ **Aspen Plus / HYSYS** use MESH equations with rigorous thermodynamic models (NRTL, UNIQUAC, SRK, PR).
- ☐ **Fenske-Underwood-Gilliland (FUG)** shortcut methods are used for initial sizing.
- ☐ Modern columns are designed with **rate-based models** (Maxwell-Stefan equations) for accurate predictions.
- ☐ **Digital twins** in Industry 4.0 use these models in real-time for inferential control.

> 🎓 **Final Advice:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the ESE question paper has **two parts** with full internal choice. Practice both McCabe–Thiele graphical problems AND Kremser numerical problems — they appear in nearly every exam cycle. Always draw clear, well-labelled diagrams and **state assumptions** before each derivation. This single habit can earn 1–2 free marks!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multicomponent Flash Drum - Ideal Binary Distillation Column
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multicomponent Flash Drum - Ideal Binary Distillation Column

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Multicomponent Flash Drum — Formal Definition

A **Multicomponent Flash Drum** (also called an *equilibrium flash vaporizer* or *flash separator*) is a single-stage vapor–liquid separation vessel in which a multi-component liquid feed is partially vaporized at a specified pressure and temperature such that the exiting vapor and liquid streams reach **thermodynamic vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE)**. The governing mathematical model combines the ** Rachford–Rice material balance equation ** with **component partition relations** $K_i = y_i / x_i$ (where $K_i$ is the equilibrium constant of component $i$).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEPOT527, Module 3), the flash drum is treated as the *fundamental building block* for staged separation networks and as the introductory framework for **sequential modular simulation** strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The flash drum is classified under **single-equilibrium-stage models**. The student is expected to derive the Rachford–Rice equation, identify design variables, and perform degree-of-freedom analysis (DOF) using the *Reklaitis* and *Wes* formulation.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Pot" Mental Model

Imagine a pressure cooker half-filled with a mixture of water, alcohol, and sugar. When you heat the pot slowly, the most volatile component (alcohol) escapes first as vapor, while the heavier components (sugar-water) remain mostly liquid. The vapor that escapes is *rich* in alcohol; the liquid that remains is *lean* in alcohol. A flash drum does exactly this — but in **one instantaneous equilibrium step** at controlled $P$ and $T$.

> [!NOTE]
> The key insight: A flash drum does **not** achieve a complete separation. It performs a **partial equilibrium split**, producing two streams whose compositions are constrained by the VLE relationship.

---

## 1.2 Ideal Binary Distillation Column — Formal Definition

An **Ideal Binary Distillation Column** is a multi-stage, counter-current vapor–liquid contacting device that separates a two-component (binary) feed into a *distillate* (top product, rich in light key) and a *bottoms* (bottom product, rich in heavy key). The **"ideal"** designation refers to three simplifying engineering assumptions adopted in the McCabe–Thiele graphical method:

1. **Constant molar overflow (CMO)** — molal latent heats of vaporization are equal for both components.
2. **Equilibrium is achieved on every theoretical stage** (Murphree efficiency = 1).
3. **Equilibrium data** are representable as a smooth, monotonic $y$–$x$ curve.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marble Sorter"

Picture a vertical stack of sieves where each sieve catches slightly heavier "marbles" and lets lighter ones pass upward via a rising air current. The very top sieve has only the lightest marbles; the very bottom holds only the heaviest. Each sieve performs a *partial* separation, but because the marbles (vapor) move up and the heavy ones (liquid) move down, the cumulative effect is a **sharp separation** after several stages. The column is just that: a stack of equilibrium flash stages, connected by counter-current flows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Binary"?** Only two components. This restriction allows the entire stage-by-stage calculation to be graphed on a 2-D $y$–$x$ diagram, which is the essence of the **McCabe–Thiele method** — a mandatory KTU Module 3 topic.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Feed molar flow | $F$ | kmol/hr |
| Distillate flow | $D$ | kmol/hr |
| Bottoms flow | $B$ | kmol/hr |
| Reflux ratio | $R$ | dimensionless ( $R = L/D$ ) |
| Reflux (reflux flow) | $L$ | kmol/hr |
| Boil-up ratio | $R_V$ | dimensionless ( $R_V = \bar{V}/B$ ) |
| Equilibrium constant | $K_i$ | dimensionless |
| Vapor fraction | $\alpha$ (or $\psi$) | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| Feed thermal condition | $q$ | dimensionless ($q$-factor) |
