F_{A1} = k C_{A1} \cdot V_1$$ **Step 2: Convert to Volumetric Flow and Concentration** Using $F_{A0} = \nu_0 C_{A0}$ and $F_{A1} = \nu_0 C_{A1}$ (constant density): $$\nu_0 C_{A0} - \nu_0 C_{A1} = k C_{A1} \cdot V_1$$ **Step 3: Factor and Rearrange** Dividing throughout by $\nu_0$ and rearranging: $$C_{A0} = C_{A1} + \frac{k C_{A1} V_1}{\nu_0} = C_{A1}\left(1 + \frac{k V_1}{\nu_0}\right)$$ **Step 4: Introduce Space Time $\tau_1 = V_1/\nu_0$** $$C_{A0} = C_{A1}(1 + k\tau_1)$$ **Step 5: Express Outlet Concentration of First Tank** $$C_{A1} = \frac{C_{A0}}{1 + k\tau_1}$$ **Step 6: Apply Same Logic to Second Tank** The second tank sees $C_{A1}$ as its inlet: $$C_{A2} = \frac{C_{A1}}{1 + k\tau_2}$$ **Step 7: Substitute and Chain** For equal-sized tanks, $\tau_1 = \tau_2 = \tau$: $$C_{A2} = \frac{C_{A0}}{(1+k\tau)^2}$$ **Step 8: Generalize to N Tanks** By mathematical induction: $$C_{AN} = \frac{C_{A0}}{(1+k\tau)^N}$$ **Step 9: Convert to Conversion Form** Since $X_N = 1 - C_{AN}/C_{A0}$: $$\boxed{X_N = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+k\tau)^N}}$$ **Verification for limiting cases:** - As $N \to 1$: $X_1 = 1 - \frac{1}{1+k\tau} = \frac{k\tau}{1+k\tau}$ ✓ (matches single CSTR) - As $N \to \infty$: $X_\infty = 1 - \lim_{N\to\infty}(1+k\tau)^{-N} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ ✓ (matches PFR) ### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Volume Distribution for Unequal CSTRs in Series **Objective:** Minimize total volume $V_T = V_1 + V_2 + ... + V_N$ for a given conversion $X_N$ and first-order reaction. **Step 1: Write the General Stage Equation** For the i-th stage: $$V_i = \frac{\nu_0(X_i - X_{i-1})}{k(1 - X_i)}$$ **Step 2: Express $X_i$ in Terms of $\tau_i$ and $X_{i-1}$** From $X_i = 1 - \frac{1-X_{i-1}}{1+k\tau_i}$: $$V_i = \frac{\nu_0}{k} \left[\frac{X_i - X_{i-1}}{1 - X_i}\right] = \frac{\nu_0}{k} \left[\frac{k\tau_i (1-X_{i-1})}{1 - \frac{1-X_{i-1}}{1+k\tau_i}}\right]$$ **Step 3: Set Up Lagrangian for Optimization** Using the Lagrange multiplier method to minimize $V_T$ subject to fixed $X_N$: $$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i + \lambda(X_N - X_{target})$$ **Step 4: Apply the Optimality Condition** Taking partial derivatives with respect to $V_i$ and setting to zero (using the chain rule through $X_i$): $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial V_i} = 0 \implies V_1 = V_2 = ... = V_N$$ **Conclusion:** For a first-order reaction, the optimal design is **equal-sized reactors**. The proof uses the principle that at the optimum, the marginal cost of an additional unit of volume in any tank must be equal. ### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Style Problem **Problem:** A first-order liquid-phase reaction ($A \rightarrow B$, $k = 0.5 \text{ min}^{-1}$) is to be carried out in CSTRs in series. The feed concentration is $C_{A0} = 2 \text{ mol/L}$ and the volumetric flow rate is $\nu_0 = 50 \text{ L/min}$. **(a) Calculate the total volume required to achieve 80% conversion using a single CSTR.** **(b) Repeat for two equal CSTRs in series.** **(c) Repeat for PFR. Comment on the results.** **Solution (a): Single CSTR** The space time required: $$X = \frac{k\tau}{1+k\tau} \implies 0.8 = \frac{0.5\tau}{1+0.5\tau}$$ Solving: $$0.8(1 + 0.5\tau) = 0.5\tau$$ $$0.8 + 0.4\tau = 0.5\tau$$ $$0.8 = 0.1\tau \implies \tau = 8 \text{ min}$$ $$V_{single} = \nu_0 \tau = 50 \times 8 = 400 \text{ L}$$ **Solution (b): Two Equal CSTRs in Series** Using the formula: $$X_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+k\tau)^2} \implies 0.8 = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+0.5\tau)^2}$$ $$\frac{1}{(1+0.5\tau)^2} = 0.2 \implies (1+0.5\tau)^2 = 5$$ $$1 + 0.5\tau = \sqrt{5} = 2.236 \implies \tau = 2.472 \text{ min}$$ Total volume: $$V_{total} = N \cdot \nu_0 \cdot \tau = 2 \times 50 \times 2.472 = 247.2 \text{ L}$$ **Solution (c): PFR** $$X = 1 - e^{-k\tau} \implies 0.8 = 1 - e^{-0.5\tau}$$ $$e^{-0.5\tau} = 0.2 \implies 0.5\tau = \ln(5) = 1.609$$ $$\tau = 3.219 \text{ min}$$ $$V_{PFR} = \nu_0 \tau = 50 \times 3.219 = 160.95 \text{ L}$$ **Comparison Table:** | **Reactor Type** | **Total Volume (L)** | **Volume Saved vs. Single CSTR** | |---|---|---| | Single CSTR | 400.0 | — | | 2 CSTRs in series | 247.2 | 38.2% | | PFR | 161.0 | 59.8% | **Conclusion:** The volume requirement decreases as we move from single CSTR → CSTRs in series → PFR, confirming that PFR is the most volume-efficient design. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: CSTRs in Series Flow Architecture ```mermaid graph LR FEED["Feed Stream
F_A0, C_A0, T_0"]:::feedStyle R1["CSTR 1
Volume V1
Space Time t1
Outlet: C_A1"]:::reactorStyle R2["CSTR 2
Volume V2
Space Time t2
Outlet: C_A2"]:::reactorStyle R3["CSTR N
Volume VN
Space Time tN
Outlet: C_AN"]:::reactorStyle PROD["Product Stream
F_AN, C_AN, X_N"]:::productStyle AG1["Agitator 1
Perfect Mixing"]:::agitatorStyle AG2["Agitator 2
Perfect Mixing"]:::agitatorStyle AGN["Agitator N
Perfect Mixing"]:::agitatorStyle JACK1["Cooling Jacket 1
Isothermal Control"]:::jacketStyle JACK2["Cooling Jacket 2
Isothermal Control"]:::jacketStyle JACKN["Cooling Jacket N
Isothermal Control"]:::jacketStyle FEED --> R1 R1 --> R2 R2 -.-> R3 R3 --> PROD AG1 --- R1 AG2 --- R2 AGN --- R3 JACK1 --- R1 JACK2 --- R2 JACKN --- R3 classDef feedStyle fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1B3A5C,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef reactorStyle fill:#E94B3C,stroke:#7A1F1A,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef productStyle fill:#50C878,stroke:#1F5C36,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef agitatorStyle fill:#F5A623,stroke:#7A4D0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef jacketStyle fill:#9013FE,stroke:#4A0E7A,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Stage-Wise Mass Balance in Cascade ```mermaid graph TD INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS
F_A0, C_A0, k, T, n"]:::inputStyle COMP1["Compute Space Time
t1 = V1 / nu_0"]:::computeStyle C1A["Compute C_A1
C_A1 = C_A0 / 1+k*t1"]:::computeStyle C2A["Compute C_A2
C_A2 = C_A1 / 1+k*t2"]:::computeStyle CNA["Compute C_AN
General Recursive Formula"]:::computeStyle CONV["Compute Overall Conversion
X_N = 1 - C_AN / C_A0"]:::outputStyle COMPARE["Compare with:
1. Single CSTR Volume
2. PFR Volume
3. Equal vs Unequal Distribution"]:::decisionStyle OPTIMIZE["OPTIMIZE
Minimize V_Total
dV/dX_i = 0"]:::decisionStyle FINAL["FINAL DESIGN
V_total, N, V_each"]:::outputStyle INPUT --> COMP1 COMP1 --> C1A C1A --> C2A C2A --> CNA CNA --> CONV CONV --> COMPARE COMPARE --> OPTIMIZE OPTIMIZE --> FINAL classDef inputStyle fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1B3A5C,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef computeStyle fill:#F5A623,stroke:#7A4D0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef decisionStyle fill:#9013FE,stroke:#4A0E7E,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef outputStyle fill:#50C878,stroke:#1F5C36,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.3 Conversion Profile Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph SINGLE["SINGLE CSTR PROFILE"] S1["Inlet
C_A0 = 2.0 mol/L"]:::high S2["Outlet
C_A = 0.4 mol/L
X = 80 percent"]:::low end subgraph SERIES["TWO CSTRs IN SERIES PROFILE"] T1["Tank 1 Inlet
C_A0 = 2.0"]:::high T2["Tank 1 Outlet
C_A1 = 0.894"]:::mid T3["Tank 2 Outlet
C_A2 = 0.4"]:::low end subgraph PFRLIM["PFR LIMIT PROFILE (N -> Infinity)"] P1["Inlet
C_A0 = 2.0"]:::high P2["Continuous
Decay Curve"]:::curve P3["Outlet
C_A = 0.4"]:::low end classDef high fill:#E94B3C,stroke:#7A1F1A,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px classDef mid fill:#F5A623,stroke:#7A4D0E,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px classDef low fill:#50C878,stroke:#1F5C36,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px classDef curve fill:#9013FE,stroke:#4A0E7A,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **State the design equation for a CSTR in series and derive the conversion expression for N equal-sized CSTRs carrying out a first-order reaction.** **Model Answer:** The design equation for the i-th CSTR in a cascade is: $$V_i = \frac{F_{A0}(X_i - X_{i-1})}{-r_{A,i}}$$ For a first-order reaction $-r_A = kC_A$ and constant density: $$\tau_i = \frac{X_i - X_{i-1}}{k(1-X_i)}$$ For equal-sized tanks with $\tau_1 = \tau_2 = \ldots = \tau$, recursive application gives: $$C_{AN} = \frac{C_{A0}}{(1+k\tau)^N}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{X_N = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+k\tau)^N}}$$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Compare the performance of a single CSTR, N CSTRs in series, and a PFR for the same total volume and a first-order reaction. What is the limiting case as N → ∞?** **Model Answer:** For the same total volume and conversion, the volume requirements follow the order: $$V_{PFR} < V_{N\,CSTRs} < V_{Single\,CSTR}$$ The conversion expressions are: - Single CSTR: $X = \frac{k\tau}{1+k\tau}$ - N CSTRs in series: $X_N = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+k\tau)^N}$ - PFR: $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ As $N \to \infty$, the CSTR cascade approaches PFR behavior because: $$\lim_{N\to\infty}\left(1 + \frac{k\tau}{N}\right)^{-N} = e^{-k\tau}$$ ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for overall conversion in N equal-sized CSTRs in series for a first-order irreversible isothermal reaction $A \rightarrow B$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(b) [7 Marks]** A first-order liquid-phase reaction with $k = 0.25 \text{ min}^{-1}$ is carried out in two CSTRs in series. The feed rate is 100 L/min with $C_{A0} = 1.5 \text{ mol/L}$. The desired overall conversion is 90%. Calculate: (i) The space time of each reactor (ii) The total volume required (iii) Compare with a single CSTR for the same conversion **Model Solution (a):** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state operation 2. Isothermal conditions 3. Constant density (liquid-phase) 4. Perfect mixing in each tank 5. First-order irreversible reaction: $-r_A = kC_A$ 6. Equal-sized reactors **Mole balance on i-th tank:** $$F_{A,i-1} - F_{A,i} + r_{A,i} V_i = 0$$ $$\nu_0 C_{A,i-1} - \nu_0 C_{A,i} = k C_{A,i} V_i$$ Rearranging: $$C_{A,i-1} = C_{A,i}\left(1 + \frac{kV_i}{\nu_0}\right) = C_{A,i}(1 + k\tau)$$ For equal-sized reactors, applying recursively from tank 1 to N: $$C_{A0} = C_{A,N}(1 + k\tau)^N$$ $$C_{A,N} = \frac{C_{A0}}{(1+k\tau)^N}$$ Therefore: $$X_N = 1 - \frac{C_{A,N}}{C_{A0}} = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+k\tau)^N}$$ **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Space time calculation:** For two CSTRs in series: $$X_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+k\tau)^2}$$ $$0.90 = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+0.25\tau)^2}$$ $$(1+0.25\tau)^2 = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10$$ $$1 + 0.25\tau = \sqrt{10} = 3.162$$ $$\tau = \frac{2.162}{0.25} = 8.65 \text{ min}$$ **(ii) Total volume:** $$V_{total} = 2 \times \nu_0 \times \tau = 2 \times 100 \times 8.65 = 1730 \text{ L}$$ **(iii) Comparison with single CSTR:** For single CSTR: $$0.90 = \frac{k\tau_s}{1+k\tau_s} = \frac{0.25\tau_s}{1+0.25\tau_s}$$ $$0.90 + 0.225\tau_s = 0.25\tau_s$$ $$0.90 = 0.025\tau_s \implies \tau_s = 36 \text{ min}$$ $$V_{single} = 100 \times 36 = 3600 \text{ L}$$ **Volume saved by using 2 CSTRs in series:** $$\text{Savings} = \frac{3600 - 1730}{3600} \times 100\% = 51.94\%$$ --- **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of optimum volume distribution in unequal-sized CSTRs in series for a first-order reaction. Prove that for minimum total volume, all CSTRs must be of equal size. **(b) [7 Marks]** An irreversible second-order reaction $A + A \rightarrow Products$ is to be carried out in two CSTRs in series. Given: $C_{A0} = 4 \text{ mol/L}$, $\nu_0 = 25 \text{ L/min}$, $k = 0.5 \text{ L/(mol·min)}$, and total reactor volume = 200 L. Determine the optimum split of volumes $V_1$ and $V_2$ for maximum overall conversion. **Model Solution (a):** The objective is to find the volumes $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_N$ that minimize $V_T = \sum V_i$ subject to fixed overall conversion $X_N$. **For a first-order reaction**, the exit concentration from the i-th tank is: $$C_{A,i} = \frac{C_{A,i-1}}{1+k\tau_i}$$ **Lagrangian Formulation:** Minimize $V_T = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\frac{\nu_0(X_i - X_{i-1})}{k(1-X_i)}$ subject to $X_N = X_{target}$. Setting $\frac{\partial V_T}{\partial X_i} = 0$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$: $$\frac{\partial V_T}{\partial X_i} = \frac{\nu_0}{k}\left[\frac{1}{1-X_i} - \frac{X_i - X_{i-1}}{(1-X_i)^2}\cdot(-1) + \frac{1}{1-X_{i+1}}\right] = 0$$ After algebraic simplification: $$\frac{1}{(1-X_i)^2} = \frac{1}{(1-X_{i+1})^2} + \frac{1}{(1-X_{i-1})^2}$$ This symmetric condition, combined with the boundary conditions, gives: $$\tau_1 = \tau_2 = \ldots = \tau_N \implies V_1 = V_2 = \ldots = V_N$$ **Conclusion:** For a first-order reaction, the optimum design consists of **equal-sized CSTRs in series**, which is why the equal-volume formula is so widely used in industry. **Model Solution (b):** For a second-order reaction: $-r_A = kC_A^2$ The design equation for each CSTR: $$V_i = \frac{\nu_0 C_{A0}(X_i - X_{i-1})}{k C_{A,i}^2} = \frac{\nu_0 C_{A0}(X_i - X_{i-1})}{k C_{A0}^2(1-X_i)^2}$$ $$\tau_i = \frac{X_i - X_{i-1}}{kC_{A0}(1-X_i)^2}$$ **Optimal Distribution Principle:** For maximum conversion with fixed total volume, we set: $$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{1-X_2}{1-X_1}\right) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{kC_{A0}\tau_2}{kC_{A0}\tau_1}}$$ Using the **iterative graphical or numerical approach** (KTU expects the conceptual method): Let $V_1 + V_2 = 200$ L. Compute $X_2$ as a function of $V_1$ and find the maximum. **Trial 1:** $V_1 = V_2 = 100$ L - $\tau_1 = 100/25 = 4$ min - From $X_1 = \frac{kC_{A0}\tau_1}{1+kC_{A0}\tau_1} = \frac{0.5 \times 4 \times 4}{1+0.5 \times 4 \times 4} = \frac{8}{9} = 0.889$ - $C_{A1} = 4(1-0.889) = 0.444$ mol/L - $\tau_2 = 4$ min - $X_2 = \frac{0.5 \times 0.444 \times 4}{1+0.5 \times 0.444 \times 4} = \frac{0.889}{1.889} = 0.471$ - Overall: $X_2 = 1 - (1-0.889)(1-0.471) = 1 - 0.111 \times 0.529 = 0.941$ **Trial 2:** $V_1 = 120$ L, $V_2 = 80$ L - $\tau_1 = 4.8$ min, $X_1 = 0.906$, $C_{A1} = 0.376$ - $\tau_2 = 3.2$ min, $X_2 = 0.376$ - Overall: $X_2 = 1 - 0.094 \times 0.624 = 0.941$ **Trial 3:** $V_1 = 80$ L, $V_2 = 120$ L - $\tau_1 = 3.2$ min, $X_1 = 0.836$, $C_{A1} = 0.656$ - $\tau_2 = 4.8$ min, $X_2 = 0.557$ - Overall: $X_2 = 1 - 0.164 \times 0.443 = 0.927$ **Conclusion:** Maximum overall conversion ≈ **0.941 or 94.1%** achieved with $V_1 \approx V_2 \approx 100$ L, confirming that for this case, equal volumes are nearly optimal. However, for highly non-first-order reactions, the optimal distribution would be **skewed** with more volume in the first tank. ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:** > > 1. **Confusing $\tau$ (space time) with $t$ (residence time):** For liquids with constant density, they are equal, but for gases, you MUST use $\tau$ based on inlet conditions. > > 2. **Forgetting to multiply by N in total volume calculation:** Total volume = $N \times V_i$, NOT just $V_i$. This is a 2-mark deduction error in Part B. > > 3. **Mixing up PFR and CSTR formulas:** Always remember PFR uses exponential form, CSTR uses algebraic form. > > 4. **Not stating assumptions:** KTU examiners award 1-2 marks for clearly stating assumptions in derivation questions. > > 5. **Algebraic errors in conversion:** When solving $(1+k\tau)^N = $ value, take Nth root carefully and watch for sign errors. > > 6. **Ignoring the limiting case:** Always mention that as $N \to \infty$, the cascade approaches PFR behavior. > > 7. **Unit inconsistency:** Space time is in minutes, $k$ must be in min⁻¹. Always check units match. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — CSTRs in Series** - **Core Formula:** $X_N = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+k\tau)^N}$ for equal-sized tanks and first-order reactions - **Limiting Cases:** - $N = 1$: Reduces to single CSTR formula - $N \to \infty$: Approaches PFR behavior ($X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$) - **Performance Order (volume for same X):** $V_{PFR} < V_{cascade} < V_{single\,CSTR}$ - **Optimization Rule:** For first-order reactions, **equal-sized reactors** give minimum total volume - **Total Volume Formula:** $V_{total} = N \cdot \nu_0 \cdot \tau$ (where $\tau$ is per-tank space time) - **Key Assumptions:** Steady state, isothermal, constant density, perfect mixing, no bypassing - **Concentration Profile:** Stepwise decreasing (tank 1 highest, tank N lowest) - **Industrial Applications:** Polymerization, fermentation, wastewater treatment, petrochemicals - **Comparison with PFR:** CSTRs in series are easier to construct, maintain, and operate than PFR; cascade is the practical compromise - **Multiple Reactions:** For series/parallel reactions, intermediate-stage samples are valuable for selectivity control - **Design Strategy:** Always start with equal-volume design, then optimize if reaction order ≠ 1 - **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks for derivation (7 marks) + numerical problem (7 marks) on this topic > [!TIP] > **MNEMONIC TO REMEMBER:** "**Cascade Cuts Capacity, Closer to PFR**" — More CSTRs in series = less total volume needed = closer to PFR performance.

topics.title: Isothermal CSTR

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT527 - PROCESS MODELLING & SIMULATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Isothermal CSTR ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition An **Isothermal Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** — also called a **Mixed Flow Reactor (MFR)** or **Backmix Reactor** — is an ideal reactor model in which the contents are assumed to be **perfectly mixed**, so that the composition, temperature, and physical properties are **spatially uniform** throughout the reactor volume and **identical to those of the exit stream**. The term *isothermal* explicitly indicates that the reactor temperature **T** is held constant at all times (steady state and transient), implying that any heat released or absorbed by reaction is instantaneously removed (or supplied) by a perfectly efficient heat-exchange system, so that no energy balance is required to solve the model. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEPOT527 – Module 4):** > An *ideal CSTR* is the canonical building block of reactor modelling. The governing equation is a single **ordinary differential equation (ODE)** in time for the transient case, and a single **algebraic equation** for the steady-state case. Every commercial stirred-tank, autoclave, fermenter, and most slurry/liquid-phase catalytic units are modelled as a CSTR in their first engineering approximation. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a large, well-stirred kitchen **stockpot** on a constant-temperature hot plate (which keeps the soup at exactly the same temperature no matter how fast it cooks): - A thin stream of cold broth containing raw vegetables (**feed**, molar flow $F_{A0}$) pours in continuously from the top. - An identical stream of cooked soup (**effluent**, molar flow $F_A$) leaves through a tap at the bottom. - A motorized stirrer runs so vigorously that **every spoonful inside the pot has the exact same taste, colour, and temperature** as the soup pouring out of the tap. - The hot plate senses the cooking heat and adjusts the flame instantly — the soup temperature **never changes**, no matter how vigorously the vegetables are cooking. The pot *volume* is the reactor volume **V**, the *tap flow* sets the **space time $\tau$**, and the *cooking rate* is the **reaction rate $-r_A$**. The "degree of cooking" of the vegetables leaving the tap is the **conversion $X_A$**. This is precisely the physical picture of an ideal isothermal CSTR. ### Standard Metrics and Definitions | Term | Symbol | Units (SI) | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Reactor Volume | $V$ | $\mathrm{m^3}$ | Liquid hold-up of the tank | | Volumetric Feed Rate | $v_0$ | $\mathrm{m^3\,s^{-1}}$ | Inlet liquid flow rate | | Molar Feed Rate of A | $F_{A0}$ | $\mathrm{mol\,s^{-1}}$ | Inlet molar flow of reactant A | | Effluent Molar Rate of A | $F_A$ | $\mathrm{mol\,s^{-1}}$ | Outlet molar flow of reactant A | | Concentration of A | $C_A$ | $\mathrm{mol\,m^{-3}}$ | Spatially uniform inside the tank | | Conversion | $X_A$ | dimensionless | Fraction of A reacted | | Space Time | $\tau$ | $\mathrm{s}$ | $V / v_0$ — average residence time | | Space Velocity | $v_s$ | $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ | $v_0 / V$ — reciprocal of $\tau$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > The defining mathematical feature of a CSTR is that **the rate of reaction evaluated at the exit conditions equals the rate of reaction everywhere inside the tank** (because of perfect mixing). This single fact collapses the spatial integral of a PFR into a trivial algebraic step and is the reason the CSTR design equation is so much simpler than its tubular counterpart. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Modelling Assumptions of an Ideal Isothermal CSTR 1. **Perfect mixing** — composition and temperature are uniform at every point in the fluid. 2. **Exit equals contents** — the effluent stream has the same composition and temperature as the fluid inside the reactor ($C_{A,\text{out}} = C_{A,\text{in-tank}}$). 3. **Constant density** — for liquids, $\rho$ does not change with composition or temperature, so $v = v_0$ (the volumetric flow rate is the same at inlet and outlet). 4. **Isothermal operation** — temperature $T$ is a known, fixed function of time (usually $T$ = constant). The energy balance is decoupled from the material balance. 5. **Single, well-defined fluid phase** — no mass-transfer resistances between phases inside the reactor. 6. **Steady feed conditions** — $F_{A0}$, $F_{B0}$, $v_0$ and $T_0$ do not vary with time (this is a *steady-state* assumption, not strictly required by the model but is the standard KTU exam scenario). > [!TIP] > If assumption 3 fails (gas-phase reaction, or liquid reaction with large density change), we **must** use the molar balance form $F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = dN_A/dt$ rather than the concentration form. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if you blindly use $v_0(C_{A0} - C_A) = -r_A V$ for a gas-phase CSTR. ### 2.2 Derivation of the Core Design Equation — The Logic Chain The mole balance on a single reactant **A** over the entire reactor volume $V$ is the **universal starting point** for every reactor model in chemical reaction engineering: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IN} - \text{OUT} + \text{GENERATION} = \text{ACCUMULATION} \end{aligned} $$ Translating into symbols for component A: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{A0} - F_A + r_A \, V = \dfrac{dN_A}{dt} \end{aligned} $$ Now apply the three CSTR-specific substitutions: 1. **Perfect-mixing identity:** $F_A = v \cdot C_A$ (where $C_A$ is the concentration *inside* the tank, identical to the exit). 2. **Moles inside the tank:** $N_A = V \cdot C_A$ (uniform concentration, constant $V$). 3. **Liquid-phase constant density:** $v = v_0$. These three substitutions, together with $F_{A0} = v_0 C_{A0}$, transform the general balance into the canonical CSTR form, which is derived in full in **Section 3**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are the **entire analytical toolkit** required to solve any Part A or Part B KTU 2024 question on an isothermal CSTR. Memorize them, and their boundary conditions, line by line. | # | Equation Name | Steady-State Form | Transient Form | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | General Mole Balance | $F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0$ | $F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt}$ | Foundation of every reactor model | | 2 | CSTR Design Equation (molal) | $V = \dfrac{F_{A0} - F_A}{-r_A}$ | $V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V$ | Derived assuming $v = v_0$ and $N_A = V C_A$ | | 3 | CSTR Design Equation (in $\tau$) | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$ | $\tau \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = C_{A0} - C_A + r_A \tau$ | $\tau = V / v_0$ | | 4 | Space Time & Space Velocity | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{v_0}, \quad v_s = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | Same | $\tau$ has units of time; $v_s$ has units of $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ | | 5 | Conversion Definition | $X_A = \dfrac{F_{A0} - F_A}{F_{A0}} = 1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C_{A0}}$ | Same | $0 \le X_A \le 1$ | | 6 | First-Order Conversion | $X_A = \dfrac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$ | $C_A(t) = C_{A0}\,\dfrac{1 + k\tau\,e^{-(1/\tau + k)t}}{1 + k\tau}$ | $-r_A = k C_A$ | | 7 | Second-Order Conversion | $X_A = \dfrac{(1 + 2k C_{A0} \tau) - \sqrt{1 + 4 k C_{A0} \tau}}{2 k C_{A0} \tau}$ | Requires numerical ODE solver | $-r_A = k C_A^{2}$ | | 8 | Zero-Order Conversion | $X_A = k \tau / C_{A0}$ (if $X_A \le 1$) | Linear ramp then constant | $-r_A = k$ | | 9 | Reversible $A \rightleftharpoons B$ | $X_A = \dfrac{k_1 \tau}{(1 + k_1 \tau) + k_2 \tau}$ | Transient form requires two coupled ODEs | $K = k_1 / k_2$ at equilibrium | | 10 | Molar Flow Conversion | $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ | Same | Used in gas-phase / variable-density problems | > [!WARNING] > In the formulas above, the rate $r_A$ follows the **convention** $r_A = (\text{moles of A generated}) / (\text{volume} \cdot \text{time})$. For a *consumption* reaction, the **rate of disappearance** is $-r_A > 0$. Never confuse $r_A$ (signed) with $-r_A$ (unsigned disappearance) in your algebra — the KTU examiner is ruthless about this sign convention. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The isothermal CSTR is the workhorse model for: - **Waste-water biological treatment** (activated-sludge process) — the aeration tank is essentially a CSTR. - **Polymerization reactors** in the lab and pilot plant — stirred-tank batch and semi-batch reactors are designed using CSTR-in-series models. - **Liquid-phase catalytic hydrogenation** in slurry reactors — the catalyst particles are small enough that external mass-transfer resistance is negligible, so the liquid phase behaves as a CSTR. - **Fermentation broths and bioreactors** — perfect mixing assumption is excellent for industrial fermenters. - **Series-of-CSTR models** of real non-ideal tubular reactors (the **tanks-in-series** model used to characterize residence-time distributions). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State CSTR Design Equation **Starting Point — General Mole Balance on A** (always begins here for any reactor type): $$ \begin{aligned} \underbrace{F_{A0}}_{\text{molar flow in}} - \underbrace{F_A}_{\text{molar flow out}} + \underbrace{r_A V}_{\text{net generation}} = \underbrace{\dfrac{dN_A}{dt}}_{\text{accumulation}} \end{aligned} $$ where $r_A$ is the rate of generation of A per unit volume (negative for consumption). At **steady state**, accumulation vanishes ($dN_A/dt = 0$), so: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V = \dfrac{F_{A0} - F_A}{-r_A} \end{aligned} $$ **Apply the perfect-mixing assumption** to relate the exit molar flow to the inside concentration: $$ \begin{aligned} F_A = v_0 \, C_A \quad \text{(exit composition = inside composition)} \end{aligned} $$ **Apply constant-density assumption** for liquid systems (so volumetric flow rate $v_0$ is unchanged through the reactor): $$ \begin{aligned} F_{A0} = v_0 \, C_{A0} \end{aligned} $$ **Substitute both into the steady-state mole balance:** $$ \begin{aligned} v_0 C_{A0} - v_0 C_A + r_A V &= 0 \\[4pt] v_0 (C_{A0} - C_A) &= -r_A V \end{aligned} $$ **Define space time** $\tau \equiv V / v_0$ and **conversion** $X_A \equiv (C_{A0} - C_A)/C_{A0}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad V = v_0 \cdot \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **canonical CSTR design equation** at steady state. > [!NOTE] > **Critical difference from a PFR:** For a PFR, the right-hand side is *integrated* along the length: $\tau_{\text{PFR}} = \int_0^{X_A} dX_A' / (-r_A')$. For a CSTR, the integral collapses to a *single evaluation* of the rate at the exit composition, because the rate is uniform throughout the tank. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Transient (Unsteady-State) CSTR Design Equation In a **batch-fed CSTR** (e.g., a fermenter whose feed is started, or a tank that has been contaminated and is being cleaned), accumulation is non-zero. The general mole balance is the same, but $dN_A/dt \neq 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = \dfrac{dN_A}{dt} \end{aligned} $$ Using the same perfect-mixing identities and the fact that $N_A = V C_A$ with $V$ constant: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{A0} - v_0 C_A + r_A V = V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} \end{aligned} $$ Dividing through by $V$ and substituting $\tau = V/v_0$: $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{C_{A0}}{\tau} - \dfrac{C_A}{\tau} + r_A = \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} \end{aligned} $$ Rearranging into the standard control-theory form: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A = C_{A0} + r_A \tau \end{aligned} $$ For a **first-order reaction** $-r_A = k C_A$, this is a linear, first-order ODE with the analytical solution: $$ \begin{aligned} C_A(t) = C_{A,\text{ss}} + \bigl(C_A(0) - C_{A,\text{ss}}\bigr)\, e^{-(1/\tau + k)\,t} \end{aligned} $$ where the **steady-state** concentration $C_{A,\text{ss}}$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{A,\text{ss}} = \dfrac{C_{A0}}{1 + k \tau} \end{aligned} $$ and the **time constant** of approach to steady state is: $$ \begin{aligned} t_c = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{\tau} + k} = \dfrac{\tau}{1 + k \tau} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This transient solution is the foundation of **start-up analysis** in KTU Module 4. After roughly **4 to 5 time constants** ($t \approx 4 t_c$), the reactor is within 1–2 % of its steady-state operating point. KTU examiners frequently ask for the time required to reach 99 % of steady state — that is, find $t$ such that $(C_A(t) - C_{A,\text{ss}})/(C_A(0) - C_{A,\text{ss}}) = 0.01$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Conversion Expression for a First-Order Reaction For $-r_A = k C_A$ with $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, the steady-state design equation becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_A} = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \dfrac{X_A}{k(1 - X_A)} \end{aligned} $$ **Solve algebraically for $X_A$:** $$ \begin{aligned} k \tau (1 - X_A) &= X_A \\[4pt] k \tau - k \tau X_A &= X_A \\[4pt] k \tau &= X_A (1 + k \tau) \\[4pt] X_A &= \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau} \end{aligned} $$ **Limiting cases (board-favourite verification step):** - $k \tau \to 0$ (very short residence time or very slow reaction): $X_A \to 0$ ✓ - $k \tau \to \infty$ (very long residence time or very fast reaction): $X_A \to 1$ ✓ --- ### 3.4 Derivation of the Conversion Expression for a Second-Order Reaction For $-r_A = k C_A^{2}$ with $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_A^{2}} = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}^{2}(1 - X_A)^{2}} = \dfrac{X_A}{k C_{A0} (1 - X_A)^{2}} \end{aligned} $$ Let $y = 1 - X_A$, so $X_A = 1 - y$. Multiply through: $$ \begin{aligned} k C_{A0} \tau \, y^{2} + y - 1 = 0 \end{aligned} $$ This is a **quadratic in $y$** with the physically meaningful root (the one satisfying $0 \le y \le 1$): $$ \begin{aligned} y = \dfrac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4 k C_{A0} \tau}}{2 k C_{A0} \tau} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} X_A = 1 - y = 1 - \dfrac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4 k C_{A0} \tau}}{2 k C_{A0} \tau} \end{aligned} $$ or, equivalently, in the form most KTU textbooks print: $$ \begin{aligned} X_A = \dfrac{(1 + 2 k C_{A0} \tau) - \sqrt{1 + 4 k C_{A0} \tau}}{2 k C_{A0} \tau} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > When solving for $X_A$ from the quadratic, **always verify** that the chosen root is in the interval $[0, 1]$. The other root is negative (unphysical). KTU board examiners explicitly check this. --- ### 3.5 Worked Example — Analytical Matrix **Problem:** A first-order, liquid-phase, isothermal reaction $A \to B$ takes place in a CSTR. Given: $C_{A0} = 2.0 \,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$, $v_0 = 10 \,\mathrm{L\,min^{-1}}$, $V = 100 \,\mathrm{L}$, and $k = 0.05 \,\mathrm{min^{-1}}$. Find the steady-state conversion and effluent concentration. **Solution (full marks, KTU style):** **Step 1 — Compute the space time:** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau = \dfrac{V}{v_0} = \dfrac{100 \,\mathrm{L}}{10 \,\mathrm{L\,min^{-1}}} = 10 \,\mathrm{min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the dimensionless group $k \tau$:** $$ \begin{aligned} k \tau = (0.05 \,\mathrm{min^{-1}})(10 \,\mathrm{min}) = 0.5 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the first-order conversion formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} X_A = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau} = \dfrac{0.5}{1 + 0.5} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.5} = 0.333 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the effluent concentration:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A) = (2.0)(1 - 0.333) = 1.333 \,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Verification by direct substitution into the design equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau &= \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_A} = \dfrac{(2.0)(0.333)}{(0.05)(1.333)} = \dfrac{0.667}{0.0667} = 10.0 \,\mathrm{min} \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $X_A = 33.3\%$, $C_A = 1.33 \,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$, and the design equation self-consistently returns $\tau = 10$ min. --- ### 3.6 Comparison Matrix — CSTR vs. PFR (for the same duty) | Performance Metric | CSTR | PFR | |---|---|---| | Conversion for first-order at given $\tau$ | $X_A = k\tau / (1 + k\tau)$ | $X_A = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ | | Conversion for second-order | Quadratic closed-form | Implicit transcendental; needs numerical solution | | Volume required for fixed $X_A$ | Larger (always, for $\alpha > 0$) | Smaller (always) | | Selectivity for parallel reactions | Favours intermediate product | Favours intermediate product (opposite) | | Sensitivity to parameter changes | High (can lead to multiple steady states) | Low (smooth) | | Best engineering choice for | Liquid-phase, slow reactions, polymerizations | Gas-phase, fast reactions, catalytic | > [!IMPORTANT] > For a single, irreversible, $n$-th-order reaction with positive order $n > 0$, the **CSTR always requires a larger volume than a PFR** to achieve the same conversion. The KTU examiner may ask you to *prove* this by computing the volume ratio $V_{\text{CSTR}} / V_{\text{PFR}}$ for a specific kinetic form. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — CSTR with Full Stream Annotations The following Mermaid block renders the CSTR as a **block-level functional architecture**, mapping every inlet stream, every outlet stream, the reactor volume, the mixing zone, and the auxiliary control loops. This is the schematic most commonly expected in a KTU 14-mark answer. ```mermaid flowchart LR feedTank["FEED TANK"]:::tank -->|F_A0 mol per s| feedLine["FEED LINE"] feedLine -->|v_0 m3 per s, T_0| inletValve["INLET VALVE"] feedTank -.->|F_B0 mol per s| feedLine inletValve --> cstrBody["CSTR MIXING TANK
Volume V m3
Isothermal T const"] cstrBody --> stirrer["IMPELLER
Perfect Mixing"] stirrer -.-> cstrBody jacket["HEAT EXCHANGER JACKET
Isothermal Control Loop"] -.->|Q removed or added| cstrBody cstrBody -.->|T measured| jacket cstrBody -->|Effluent v m3 per s| outletValve["OUTLET VALVE"] outletValve -->|F_A mol per s| productTank["PRODUCT TANK"] outletValve -->|F_B mol per s| productTank sensorC["C_A SENSOR INSIDE TANK"] -.-> cstrBody sensorC -.->|Same value| outletValve classDef tank fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` **Reading guide for the diagram:** - The **solid arrows** denote material (mass) flow. - The **dotted arrows** denote either an internal control signal (T from tank to jacket), a feedback measurement (C_A sensor reading the tank), or a side stream of inert component B. - The notation $F_{A0}$ means *molar flow rate of species A at the inlet*; $F_A$ is the same quantity at the exit. - The key invariant enforced by the perfect-mixing assumption is shown explicitly: the **C_A sensor** reads the same value as the **outlet valve** delivers. This is what makes the design equation so compact. --- ### 4.2 Mass-Balance Block Diagram — The Core "In – Out + Gen = Acc" Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR a["F_A0 IN
Molar Feed Rate"]:::in b["F_A OUT
Molar Effluent Rate"]:::out c["r_A V
Generation"]:::gen d["dN_A per dt
Accumulation"]:::acc a --> sum(("= ")) b --> sum c --> sum d --> sum classDef in fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef out fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef gen fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#EF6C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef acc fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` This block diagram is the **conceptual heart** of every reactor model. Each block can be turned on or off depending on the situation: | Block | CSTR Steady State | CSTR Transient | PFR Steady State | PFR Transient | |---|---|---|---|---| | IN $F_{A0}$ | ON | ON | ON | ON | | OUT $F_A$ | ON | ON | ON ($= v\,C_A(z)$) | ON | | GEN $r_A V$ | ON | ON | ON ($= r_A(z)\,dV$) | ON | | ACC $dN_A/dt$ | **OFF** | **ON** | **OFF** | **ON** | --- ### 4.3 Solution Methodology Flowchart — How to Attack any CSTR Problem ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Start: Isothermal CSTR Problem]) --> readData["Read given data
V, v0, C_A0, kinetics, T"] readData --> checkSS{"Steady or
Transient?"} checkSS -- Steady --> ssPath["Use algebraic design equation
tau = C_A0 X_A over minus r_A"] checkSS -- Transient --> trPath["Use ODE form
V dC_A per dt = F_A0 minus F_A plus r_A V"] ssPath --> rateChoice{"Order of
reaction?"} rateChoice -- Zero --> z["X_A = k tau per C_A0"] rateChoice -- First --> f["X_A = k tau over 1 plus k tau"] rateChoice -- Second --> s["Quadratic in 1 minus X_A"] rateChoice -- General n --> g["Implicit; solve numerically"] trPath --> linCheck{"Linear in C_A?"} linCheck -- Yes --> lin["Closed-form exponential solution"] linCheck -- No --> num["Numerical integration
Runge-Kutta 4"] z --> answer["Compute X_A and C_A"] f --> answer s --> answer g --> answer lin --> answer num --> answer answer --> verify["Sanity check:
0 le X_A le 1"] verify --> done([End]) ``` > [!TIP] > When asked "compare the volume required for a CSTR vs. a PFR", follow this flow **twice** — once down the CSTR branch, once down the PFR branch — and then form the ratio $V_{\text{CSTR}} / V_{\text{PFR}}$. The KTU model answer almost always requires a numerical comparison table at the end. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]** **A1.** *Define an ideal Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor. List any three modelling assumptions.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **ideal Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** is a perfectly mixed reactor in which the contents are spatially uniform in composition, temperature, and physical properties, and the exit stream has properties identical to those of the fluid inside the tank. It is assumed to operate at constant temperature (isothermal). Three key modelling assumptions: 1. **Perfect mixing** — composition and temperature are uniform throughout the reactor volume; exit composition equals the tank composition. 2. **Constant fluid density** — for liquid-phase systems, the volumetric flow rate is the same at inlet and outlet ($v = v_0$). 3. **Isothermal operation** — temperature $T$ is fixed and uniform; the energy balance is decoupled from the material balance. *(1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for any three assumptions, 1 mark for the isothermal emphasis — full marks.)* --- **A2.** *For an isothermal CSTR carrying out a first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$, derive the expression for conversion in terms of space time.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** For $-r_A = k C_A$, the steady-state CSTR design equation gives: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_A} = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0} (1 - X_A)} = \dfrac{X_A}{k(1 - X_A)} \end{aligned} $$ Cross-multiplying and solving for $X_A$: $$ \begin{aligned} k \tau (1 - X_A) = X_A \;\Longrightarrow\; k\tau = X_A (1 + k\tau) \;\Longrightarrow\; X_A = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau} \end{aligned} $$ *(1 mark for the design equation, 1 mark for the substitution, 1 mark for the final expression.)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Pattern: Choose ONE of the two alternatives.]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** *Starting from the general mole balance, derive the steady-state design equation for an isothermal CSTR in the form $\tau = C_{A0} X_A / (-r_A)$. State clearly each modelling assumption you invoke.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *A first-order, liquid-phase, isothermal reaction $A \to B$ is carried out in a CSTR. The feed concentration is $C_{A0} = 4.0 \,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$, the rate constant is $k = 0.2 \,\mathrm{min^{-1}}$, and the desired conversion is 75 %. Calculate: (i) the required space time, (ii) the required reactor volume if the volumetric flow rate is $v_0 = 50 \,\mathrm{L\,min^{-1}}$, and (iii) the effluent concentration of A.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution A(a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the general mole balance on species A over the entire reactor volume $V$: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = \dfrac{dN_A}{dt} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the steady-state condition: $dN_A/dt = 0$, giving: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{A0} - F_A = -r_A V \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V = \dfrac{F_{A0} - F_A}{-r_A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the perfect-mixing assumption: $F_A = v_0 C_A$ and $F_{A0} = v_0 C_{A0}$. Substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} v_0 C_{A0} - v_0 C_A = -r_A V \;\Longrightarrow\; v_0 (C_{A0} - C_A) = -r_A V \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Define space time $\tau = V / v_0$ and conversion $X_A = (C_{A0} - C_A)/C_{A0}$. Substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** State the assumptions explicitly: perfect mixing, constant density (so $v = v_0$), isothermal, single phase, well-defined feed, uniform volume. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Note that for a PFR the same balance would lead to $\tau = \int dX_A / (-r_A)$; for a CSTR, because $-r_A$ is uniform, the integral collapses to a single evaluation at the exit. **Model Solution A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the dimensionless group using the target conversion $X_A = 0.75$: $$ \begin{aligned} X_A = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau} \;\Longrightarrow\; k \tau = \dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A} = \dfrac{0.75}{0.25} = 3.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Solve for the space time: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau = \dfrac{3.0}{0.2} = 15 \,\mathrm{min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the reactor volume: $$ \begin{aligned} V = v_0 \tau = (50 \,\mathrm{L\,min^{-1}})(15 \,\mathrm{min}) = 750 \,\mathrm{L} = 0.75 \,\mathrm{m^3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the effluent concentration: $$ \begin{aligned} C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A) = (4.0)(0.25) = 1.0 \,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Verify by direct substitution into the design equation: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_A} = \dfrac{(4.0)(0.75)}{(0.2)(1.0)} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.2} = 15 \,\mathrm{min} \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** State the final answer in a box and mention engineering interpretation (e.g., "The reactor delivers a 75 % conversion; the effluent A concentration is 1 mol/L, which is suitable for downstream separation by distillation or extraction"). **Final Answer:** $\tau = 15$ min, $V = 750$ L, $C_A = 1.0 \,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** *Explain the physical significance of the design equation $\tau = C_{A0} X_A / (-r_A)$ for a CSTR. Discuss how the conversion changes with space time for zero-order, first-order, and second-order reactions.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *For a second-order liquid-phase reaction $2A \to B$ with $-r_A = k C_A^{2}$, $k = 0.08 \,\mathrm{L\,mol^{-1}\,min^{-1}}$, $C_{A0} = 1.5 \,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$, $V = 200 \,\mathrm{L}$, and $v_0 = 25 \,\mathrm{L\,min^{-1}}$, calculate the conversion achieved in the CSTR.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution B(a) — Conceptual Discussion (7 marks):** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Physical significance — the design equation states that the space time required to achieve a given conversion is the ratio of the "amount reacted" (per unit volume of feed) to the "rate of disappearance" (per unit volume of reactor). It is the **time needed to process one reactor-volume of feed at the rate at which molecules are being consumed**. Operationally, it tells the engineer what reactor volume to specify: $V = v_0 \tau$. **Step 2 (2 marks):** For a **zero-order** reaction $-r_A = k$: $$ \begin{aligned} X_A = k \tau / C_{A0} \end{aligned} $$ Conversion rises **linearly** with $\tau$ until it hits 1 (100 %); beyond that the reaction is simply complete and additional $\tau$ is wasted. **Step 3 (2 marks):** For a **first-order** reaction: $$ \begin{aligned} X_A = k \tau / (1 + k \tau) \end{aligned} $$ Conversion rises **hyperbolically**; it asymptotically approaches 1 as $\tau \to \infty$. The half-life of the approach is at $k\tau = 1$, i.e., $\tau = 1/k$. **Step 4 (1 mark):** For a **second-order** reaction, the conversion is the positive root of a quadratic and rises *more slowly* than first order at large $\tau$. The conversion curve is concave-down at small $\tau$ and concave-up at large $\tau$, with a steeper tail than the first-order case. **Model Solution B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the space time: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau = \dfrac{V}{v_0} = \dfrac{200}{25} = 8 \,\mathrm{min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the dimensionless group $k C_{A0} \tau$: $$ \begin{aligned} k C_{A0} \tau = (0.08)(1.5)(8) = 0.96 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Set up the quadratic. Starting from: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau = \dfrac{X_A}{k C_{A0} (1 - X_A)^{2}} \end{aligned} $$ we get: $$ \begin{aligned} k C_{A0} \tau (1 - X_A)^{2} - X_A = 0 \end{aligned} $$ Expanding with $M \equiv k C_{A0} \tau = 0.96$: $$ \begin{aligned} M (1 - 2 X_A + X_A^{2}) - X_A &= 0 \\[4pt] M X_A^{2} - (2M + 1) X_A + M &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute the numerical values: $$ \begin{aligned} 0.96 X_A^{2} - (2 \times 0.96 + 1) X_A + 0.96 &= 0 \\[4pt] 0.96 X_A^{2} - 2.92 X_A + 0.96 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 (2 marks):** Solve the quadratic using $X_A = \dfrac{b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a = 0.96$, $b = -2.92$, $c = 0.96$: $$ \begin{aligned} X_A = \dfrac{2.92 \pm \sqrt{(2.92)^2 - 4 (0.96)(0.96)}}{2(0.96)} = \dfrac{2.92 \pm \sqrt{8.5264 - 3.6864}}{1.92} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} X_A = \dfrac{2.92 \pm \sqrt{4.84}}{1.92} = \dfrac{2.92 \pm 2.20}{1.92} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Two roots: $X_A = (2.92 + 2.20)/1.92 = 2.67$ (unphysical, > 1) and $X_A = (2.92 - 2.20)/1.92 = 0.375$. Select the physical root. **Final Answer:** $X_A = 0.375 = 37.5\%$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign of $r_A$**: Students mix up $r_A$ (generation, negative for consumption) and $-r_A$ (disappearance, positive). Always write $-r_A$ explicitly in the denominator. > 2. **Algebraic slip in the quadratic**: When expanding $(1 - X_A)^{2}$, the coefficient of $X_A$ is $-(2M + 1)$, not $-(2M - 1)$. This single sign error costs the entire 7 marks in part (b). > 3. **Forgetting to discard the unphysical root**: The second root is almost always negative or greater than 1. The KTU examiner explicitly checks that you selected the root in $[0, 1]$. > 4. **Omitting the assumption list** in the derivation part. The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates **at least 1 to 2 marks** purely for stating the modelling assumptions. A perfect derivation without them loses 15–20 % of the marks. > 5. **Using $v$ instead of $v_0$** in the design equation for a gas-phase CSTR — this is wrong because the volumetric flow changes with conversion. For gases, use the molar form $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and the appropriate $F_T, y_A, \varepsilon$ formulation. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Isothermal CSTR (Module 4):** - ✅ The **CSTR design equation** $\tau = C_{A0} X_A / (-r_A)$ is the single most tested result in this module — derive it cleanly, in under 5 lines, from the general mole balance. - ✅ The **six modelling assumptions** are: perfect mixing, exit = contents, constant density, isothermal, single phase, steady feed — memorize them verbatim. - ✅ The **space time** $\tau = V / v_0$ and **space velocity** $v_s = v_0 / V$ are reciprocals; do not confuse them in numerical problems. - ✅ The **first-order conversion** is $X_A = k\tau / (1 + k\tau)$; verify limits $X_A \to 0$ as $k\tau \to 0$ and $X_A \to 1$ as $k\tau \to \infty$. - ✅ The **second-order conversion** is the smaller root of a quadratic in $(1 - X_A)$; always discard the unphysical root. - ✅ The **transient CSTR** obeys $\tau\, dC_A/dt + C_A = C_{A0} + r_A \tau$; for first-order, the solution is exponential with time constant $t_c = \tau / (1 + k\tau)$. - ✅ **CSTR volume > PFR volume** for the same conversion and positive-order kinetics; the ratio $V_{\text{CSTR}} / V_{\text{PFR}}$ increases as $X_A$ approaches 1. - ✅ The **direction of integration** distinguishes a CSTR (no integration; the rate is uniform) from a PFR (integration over the length). - ✅ For **gas-phase** CSTR problems, use the **molar balance form** with $\varepsilon$ (volumetric expansion factor) and $F_T$, not the concentration form with $v_0$. - ✅ A CSTR has **infinite mixing** and is therefore an **algebraic** model at steady state — the *only* time you need a differential equation is the transient case. - ✅ For a **reversible reaction** $A \rightleftharpoons B$, the equilibrium conversion is $X_{A,\text{eq}} = K / (1 + K)$, and the actual CSTR conversion lies below this, approaching it as $\tau \to \infty$.
